assumptions: UK active BVLOS operator pool: 50-100 organisations by 2028
Simultaneous active submissions: 10-20 (applicant-mode, long-running per REQ016)
Submission pack size: typical 50-500 MB (PDFs, data packs, video snippets)
Peak intake rate: 1-2 applications/week across UK CAA + MAA
Average submission lifecycle: 8-12 weeks (REQ016 phased gates)
Concurrent applicant sessions: avg 15, peak 25 (representing 10-20 active submissions)
Pack completeness check (METER-001 Quick Gate): automated, flagging gaps within 10 minutes
Each submission: ~200-300 MB average (ConOps, SORA evidence, Safety Case, training plans, simulation data)
Classification tier provisioning overhead: 4x replication for OFFICIAL, OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE, SECRET, STRAP (only swarm/MOD trials need upper tiers)

growth_projection.6_month: Submissions increasing 2-3/week; evidence store grows 4-5 GB/month as trials conclude (archive population increases). Quick Gate automation reduces intake processing time from 2 hours to 10 minutes. Concurrent sessions peak at 30 during Protector RG Mk1 trials.
growth_projection.12_month: Steady state ~4-5 submissions/week. Evidence store at ~25 GB cumulative. Database normalized, indexes optimized. Concurrent peak near 40 (multiple parallel trial phases). First major archive cohort (10-15 completed cases) added to long-term storage.
growth_projection.24_month: Project Alvina BVLOS urban-logistics trials begin; intake rate potentially 8-10/week. Evidence store 40-50 GB. Concurrent peaks 50+ users. Clause library revision cycle reaches 2-3 per year (regulatory changes); storage ~6-8 GB with versioning.
growth_projection.36_month: GCAP/Tempest autonomy assurance cases begin (advanced ML/swarm); trials may run in cohorts (5+ parallel). Evidence store reaches 60+ GB. Peak concurrent users 60+. Classification tier segregation becomes critical; separate DB replicas required for SECRET tier (slow access path acceptable for MOD review).

capacity_model.notes: Submissions drive storage growth; phased gate structure (REQ016) requires high-availability application persistence. Classification tier handling adds 40% to storage cost. Quick Gate automation (METER-001) is critical to reduce CAA case officer rework overhead (currently 72% first-submission pass rate per vibe PackCompleteness benchmark).

capacity_model.feature: inspector review sessions and SORA assessment
assumptions: Inspector pool (CAA + MAA combined): 30-60 active users
SORA assessment workload: ~5-10 hours per case (swarm-aware evidence inventory audit per METER-003)
Safety case review: ~8-15 hours per submission
ConOps/ODD review: ~4-6 hours per case
Joint panel deliberation: 15 panels per quarter, each requiring 5-7 person-days synchronous work
Average inspector session duration: 3-4 hours for deep review work; 1-2 hours for coordination/status meetings
Document read/annotate transaction frequency: 150-200 per inspector per review cycle
Concurrent peak: 45 users (combination of 20-30 active SORA reviewers + 15 panelists deliberating + ad-hoc inspectors)
Swarm-aware SORA evidence gap (vibe constraint): status 15%, tolerable >= 3 items/aircraft, regulatory >= 5 items/aircraft; audit workload increases 30% due to complexity
Classification tier handling increases query overhead for SECRET/STRAP tiers (separate DB access path)

growth_projection.6_month: Inspector pool growing 5-10 FTE. Each SORA assessment requires 5-10 hours with swarm-specific evidence inventory (METER-003). Peak concurrent: 40 users during panel deliberations. Review transaction load increases 25%.
growth_projection.12_month: Inspector pool 40-50 FTE. Established reviewer cohorts (SORA team, safety team, panels). Peak concurrent: 50 users. Swarm-aware SORA evidence adoption (currently 15% per vibe benchmark) improves to ~40%, reducing rework but increasing initial audit complexity. Audit query database load peaks at 100 TPS during panel season (Q3, Q4).
growth_projection.24_month: Inspector pool near full capacity (50-60 FTE). Panels run in overlapping cohorts (3-5 simultaneous). Peak concurrent: 55+ users. AMLAS/SACE case evidence uploads increase database transaction load 40%. Advanced ML assurance (REQ009) adds 2-3 hours per case (explainability evidence review).
growth_projection.36_month: Pool maxed at ~60 FTE (further growth requires CAA/MAA hiring). Advanced BVLOS + GCAP/Tempest cases add complexity; peak review workload 15+ hours per case. Concurrent peaks 60+ during multi-panel seasons. Archive query performance degrades; historical case evidence search requires optimized indexing.

capacity_model.notes: SORA assessment (REQ002) is the most labor-intensive review task. Swarm-aware evidence gap (vibe SwarmAwareSORAEvidence: status 15%, tolerable 80%) means many cases will require extended review cycles and rework. METER-003 (Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit) is a manual domain-expert task; automated support tools (evidence taxonomy tagging, traceability hyperlinks) are essential to keep TPS throughput below 100 during peak panel seasons.

capacity_model.feature: joint review panel deliberation and decision recording
assumptions: Joint review panels per quarter: 5-15 (per parent user story timeframe 2026-2028)
Panel size: 8-12 members (CAA + MAA + external advisors)
Panel deliberation duration: 5-10 days per panel (including pre-read, meetings, breakouts, final decision)
Panel panel action frequency: ~100 actions per panel (evidence annotations, decision votes, condition records, rationale entries)
Audit trail record size per action: ~100 KB (metadata + narrative + evidence references)
Immutable write-heavy workload; 100 actions/panel × 15 panels/year = 1,500 panel actions/year = ~130 KB/year of audit trail base
Decision record archive: ~20 MB per case (decision summary, conditions, compliance verification checklist, post-trial close-out plan)
Expected throughput: 15-30 approvals per year by 2028 (conservative ramp-up: first trials are de-risking cohorts)
Concurrent panel members during live deliberation: 8-20 (not all present simultaneously; async + sync mix)
Deliberation session peak: 20 users during plenary vote sessions (quorum requirements)

growth_projection.6_month: First panel (Protector RG Mk1 trial 1) convenes; 100-150 actions recorded; audit trail ~15-20 MB created. Deliberation recorded at 15 TPS peak (plenary sessions).
growth_projection.12_month: Second and third panels (Project Alvina BVLOS trials) active or completed; cumulative audit trail ~100 MB. First decision archives populated (10-15 cases approved or conditionally approved). Parallel panel prep increases concurrent users to 15-18.
growth_projection.24_month: 5-8 panels completed; cumulative audit trail ~500 MB. Decision record archive 200+ MB. GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy cases begin; panel workload intensity increases (more conditions, longer deliberations). Concurrent peaks near 20 during multi-panel overlap.
growth_projection.36_month: 10-15 panels completed per plan; cumulative audit trail 1-1.5 GB. Archive reaches 300+ MB. Advanced ML assurance (REQ009) and swarm autonomy (REQ008) cases require specialized breakout sessions, extending deliberation window. Archive search performance becomes important (historical precedent lookup).

capacity_model.notes: Panel deliberation is low-concurrency but high-importance data. Immutable audit trail (REQ012 MOR obligations) must survive system failures; write-once storage (S3 versioning, WORM volumes) recommended. Decision records are the final authoritative output; any corruption is regulatory non-compliance. Concurrent peak (20 users) occurs during plenary votes; async tools (Slack integration, async decision polling) can reduce synchronous demand.

assumptions: Clause library: ~200 MB per major revision (JARUS SORA v2.5, AMLAS/SACE templates, CAP 722 updates, ML explainability templates, swarm-specific guidance)
Revision cadence: quarterly (3-4 major revisions/year, 1-2 minor revisions/quarter)
5-year retention: ~4 GB cumulative (200 MB × 5 major + 3 minor per year × 5 years = ~4 GB)
Search queries: ~100-150 inspector/case officer per review cycle (finding relevant precedent, guidance clauses, compliance templates)
Average search latency requirement: <500 ms (interactive inspector workload)
Full-text indexing overhead: 50% of library size (~100 MB index per 200 MB library)
Vector embeddings for semantic search (AI inference component): ~50 MB per library version
Classification tier variant storage: OFFICIAL library + OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE addenda (security-specific clauses, MOD coordination templates) = 1.2x base size for upper tiers
Concurrent search peak: 15 inspectors searching during pre-panel preparation (case officer sessions)

growth_projection.6_month: Initial SORA v2.5 + AMLAS baseline library deployed (200 MB); first minor revision (+20 MB). Search query load establishes baseline (~50 TPS peak during prep week). Semantic search prototype tuned.
growth_projection.12_month: JARUS SORA v2.5 adoption increases; library grows with swarm-specific clauses (+50 MB new content). Second major revision (CAP 722A updates, ML explainability addenda). Library size ~250 MB. Search load 60-70 TPS peak.
growth_projection.24_month: GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy clauses added; ML assurance library expands (+100 MB). Project Alvina BVLOS operational lessons learned integrated (+30 MB). Library size ~380 MB. Full-text index becomes critical for sub-500ms latency; semantic search routine (integration with case officer assist AI).
growth_projection.36_month: Mature library: 400-500 MB. Cumulative versions ~2-3 GB retained. Classification tier variants (SECRET addenda for MOD swarm trials) add 50-100 MB. Search query load peaks 80 TPS during large multi-panel seasons. Indexing performance tuning complete; latency <300 ms target achieved.

capacity_model.notes: Clause library is regulatory guidance + compliance artifact. Versioning is critical (audit trail REQ012); cannot delete or modify historical clauses. Full-text + semantic search (powered by AI inference component) essential for case officer productivity. REQ018 (security classification handling) requires OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE and SECRET variants; search performance isolation (slow-path for classified queries) acceptable. This is a read-heavy workload; CDN caching can reduce origin load by 50%.

assumptions: REQ020 (Post-Trial Assurance & Close-out): absent in original user stories but mandatory from CAP 722A §23 (post-approval archive obligations)
Average case archive size: 20 MB per approved trial (decision record + evidence summary + compliance checklist + lessons-learned packs)
Approval throughput: 15-30 cases/year by 2028 (conservative ramp-up; first cohorts are de-risking trials)
10-year retention for investigation (REQ012 MOR obligations): 15 cases/year avg × 10 years = 150 cases × 20 MB = 3 GB minimum
Video evidence from flight tests: 200-500 MB per trial (simulation footage, flight telemetry video, incident recordings) — stored separately from decision record (cold-path archive)
Simulation data packs: 100-200 MB per trial (model checkpoints, test scenarios, failure-mode demonstrations)
Classification tier segregation: OFFICIAL cases in standard archive; OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE + SECRET cases in separate air-gapped replicas (regulatory requirement CAP 722H)
Archive access pattern: low-frequency (incident investigation, lessons-learned review, regulatory audit) — acceptable latency 1-2 hours for retrieval
Write-once storage (WORM compliance): all evidence immutable after case closure; bit-rot protection via checksums
Current state (2026 Apr): ~2-3 cases completed (Protector RG Mk1 pilot); archive population modest. Peak growth 2026-2027 as trials ramp.

growth_projection.6_month: First 5-8 cases closed; archive ~100-150 MB. Video evidence segregated to cold-path S3 Glacier Deep Archive. WORM compliance baseline established (versioning disabled; checksums computed at ingest).
growth_projection.12_month: 10-15 cases in archive; ~250-300 MB plus video (500 MB - 1 GB in Glacier). Lessons-learned packs begin aggregation (REQ020); ~50-100 MB new content. Classification tier segregation operational for SECRET tier trials (separate DB replica exists but sparsely populated).
growth_projection.24_month: 25-40 cases archived; ~400-600 MB structured evidence + 1-2 GB video. Lessons-learned cross-case analysis enabled (trends in approval conditions, failure modes, regulatory guidance changes). Archive search (incident lookup, precedent discovery) routine. Classification tier segregation mature; SECRET tier archive begins to populate (GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy trials bring MOD classified evidence).
growth_projection.36_month: 45-60 cases in archive; ~700 MB - 1 GB core evidence + 2-4 GB video + lessons-learned ~300 MB. 10-year retention cycle begins (oldest cases reach 10-year mark, eligible for disposition per CAP 722A). Archive retrieval SLA: <1 hour for incident investigation (REQ012).

capacity_model.notes: Archive is critical for regulatory compliance (REQ012 MOR obligations, REQ020 post-trial assurance). REQ020 was absent in original requirements but mandatory per CAP 722A §23 — this is a gap finding (see ada-findings.json). Immutable storage + classification tier segregation drives cost (WORM volumes, separate S3 buckets with encryption keys per tier). Video evidence in Glacier is cost-effective (~0.004 GBP/GB/month) but retrieval latency acceptable for investigation use case. Lessons-learned aggregation requires NLP/ML support (cluster similar findings, cross-reference regulatory updates); AI inference component supports this.

assumptions: REQ018: Security Classification Handling — no user story in original requirements; critical gap for MOD-involved trials

Classification tiers: OFFICIAL (open, no special handling), OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE (restricted distribution, UK eyes), SECRET (MOD eyes only), STRAP (Special Tactical Requirement, MOD + limited CAA personnel)
Storage provisioning: separate DB replicas per tier (data segregation, compliance with JSP 440 Information Security Manual)
Access control: role-based; CAA personnel only on OFFICIAL/OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE; MOD liaison on SECRET/STRAP; joint panel membership segregated (MOD panelists only present for classified discussions)
Network isolation: SECRET/STRAP evidence accessed only from air-gapped CAA secure facility (no cloud; on-premises only)
Encryption overhead: data-at-rest (AES-256) + data-in-transit (TLS 1.3 with classified cipher suites); key management per tier
Expected tier distribution by 2028: ~60% OFFICIAL, ~25% OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE (swarm trials with commercial data), ~10% SECRET, ~5% STRAP (GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy only)

Tier 1 (OFFICIAL): cloud-hosted, high availability; Tier 2-4 (OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE, SECRET, STRAP): on-premises or dedicated managed service (lower availability SLA acceptable for low-concurrency SECRET/STRAP work)
Concurrent users: mostly Tier 1 (OFFICIAL) users; SECRET/STRAP peak 5-10 during MOD trials

growth_projection.6_month: OFFICIAL tier deployed; OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE tier preparation underway (Project Alvina swarm trials may have commercial supplier data). Initial compliance audit (JSP 440) conducted. No SECRET tier demand yet.
growth_projection.12_month: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE tier operational (first commercial swarm trials ingest supplier SORA evidence, compliance data). Separate DB replica deployed on-premises. SECRET tier infrastructure planned (GCAP/Tempest roadmap). Classification handling procedures documented (CAP 722H compliance checklist).
growth_projection.24_month: OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE tier mature; ~25% of submissions classified at this tier. SECRET tier deployed as-needed (first GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy pilot); air-gapped access established (MOD liaison office equipped with secure workstations). STRAP tier reserved (not yet populated).
growth_projection.36_month: All four tiers operational. Tier distribution: OFFICIAL 60% (20-30 GB), OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE 25% (10-15 GB on-premises DB), SECRET 10% (3-5 GB air-gapped), STRAP 5% (1-2 GB, very limited access). Access audit logs (separate from evidence store) track all tier-crossing operations; compliance reporting to CAA Information Security team monthly.

capacity_model.notes: REQ018 (Security Classification Handling) absent in user stories but critical for MOD-involved trials (GCAP/Tempest autonomy assurance). This is a major capacity driver: on-premises SECRET/STRAP infrastructure (separate DB, air-gapped network, secure facilities) costs ~2-3x cloud per GB. Tier segregation adds operational complexity (separate backup/disaster-recovery plans per tier, classification-aware access control lists, security audit trails). Recommendation: establish MOD liaison coordination protocol early (2026) to define STRAP expectations before first MOD trial intake.

capacity_model.feature: typical 5-10 vehicle swarm telemetry and C2 communication
assumptions: Typical swarm: 5-10 vehicles (may scale to 20 in advanced trials per GCAP/Tempest)
Telemetry rate: 1-10 Hz per vehicle (1 Hz nominal, 10 Hz peak during DAA encounter or autonomy decision events)
Telemetry data per message: ~500 bytes (position, altitude, velocity, attitude, battery, C2 link quality, autonomous decision flags)
Swarm size 5 vehicles at 5 Hz nominal: 5 × 5 messages/sec × 500 bytes = 12.5 KB/sec = 900 MB/hour per trial
Peak scenarios: 10 vehicles at 10 Hz (DAA encounter): 10 × 10 × 500 = 50 KB/sec = 3.6 GB/hour
Trial duration: 1-2 hours typical BVLOS trial (including pre-flight checks, transits, objective execution, return-to-launch)
Per-trial data volume: 5 vehicles, 5 Hz, 90 minutes = 900 MB/hour × 1.5 hours = 1.35 GB per trial
C2 link bandwidth budget: 100-500 kbps per vehicle (command rate low ~1 Hz, status telemetry 5-10 Hz, inter-vehicle coordination 1-5 Hz)
Inter-vehicle coordination messages: 0.5-2 KB per message, 1-10 Hz coordination cadence per pair; 5-vehicle mesh: ~20 coordination messages/sec = 10-40 KB/sec (depends on swarm decision algorithm)
Total per-swarm C2 + coordination + telemetry: ~50-150 KB/sec nominal, 200+ KB/sec peak (DAA encounters, formation changes)
Ground control segment: receives all telemetry, logs to evidence store, runs real-time autonomy-state machine (validating compliance with authorized flight plan per REQ008)
Storage (evidence logging): all telemetry + C2 messages recorded for post-trial analysis (REQ012 MOR obligations); compression ratio ~2-3x (telemetry is repetitive)
Concurrent swarm trials: 1-3 simultaneous trials by 2028 (assumes 2-3 authorized swarm BVLOS cohorts in parallel); shared ground-control infrastructure

growth_projection.6_month: First swarm trial (Protector RG Mk1): 5 vehicles, 90 min, ~1.5 GB telemetry data. Ground control compute: minimal (5 vehicles × 5 Hz = 25 message/sec). Real-time C2 link: 100-200 kbps per vehicle achievable with 4G/LTE. Evidence logging: 1.5 GB stored (compressed).
growth_projection.12_month: Second swarm trial (Project Alvina): 7 vehicles, potential 2-hour duration; ~2.5 GB telemetry per trial. Concurrent trials begin to overlap (pre-flight prep + post-flight analysis from first trial while second trial executes); peak concurrent database load: 2 trials × 25 msg/sec + coordination = 80 msg/sec peak.
growth_projection.24_month: 3-4 swarm trials in rotation (BVLOS + advanced autonomy pilot); swarm sizes 8-10 vehicles. Telemetry data volume: 3-5 GB per trial. Peak concurrent: 2-3 simultaneous trials. Ground control compute: multi-threaded autonomy state machine (C++ real-time process) required for 100+ msg/sec throughput. AI inference component supports post-trial analysis (deviation detection, anomaly clustering in telemetry streams).
growth_projection.36_month: GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy trials: 10+ vehicles, complex decision-making (swarm formation changes, dynamic re-tasking per REQ008). Per-trial data: 5-10 GB. Concurrent peak: 3 trials possible. C2 link quality becomes critical (low-latency guaranteed delivery for safety-critical de-confliction messages); evidence logging must capture all link-loss events for post-trial investigation. Archive: 20-30 GB cumulative swarm telemetry by end of 2028.

capacity_model.notes: Swarm telemetry is high-volume, time-series data. Real-time processing (ground control autonomy state machine) requires low-latency database (sub-100 ms query latency for in-flight state validation). Evidence logging (all messages recorded) is write-heavy; time-series database (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) recommended over traditional RDBMS. C2 link bandwidth is operational constraint (REQ006 authorized monitored C2); LTE/5G networks typically support 500+ kbps per UAS. Swarm coordination complexity (inter-vehicle message volume) increases with swarm size and decision-making sophistication; 10-vehicle swarm may require 200+ KB/sec combined bandwidth. This capacity model assumes nominal terrestrial networks; if trial over maritime/remote areas, satellite C2 bandwidth becomes constrained (400 kbps max; real-time telemetry rate must reduce to ~1 Hz).

capacity_model.feature: DAA sensor data fusion and encounter resolution
assumptions: DAA (Detect and Avoid) sensors per vehicle: radar + ADS-B receiver (passive listening for cooperative aircraft) + optical/IR (future)
Radar scan rate: 10 Hz (modern solid-state); ~2-5 KB per scan (target list, bearing, altitude, confidence)
ADS-B message rate: 0.5-2 Hz per cooperative aircraft in airspace (highly variable; urban airspace 10+ cooperative targets possible)
DAA data per vehicle: radar 5 × 2 KB = 10 KB/sec + ADS-B 2 × 2 KB = 4 KB/sec = 14 KB/sec nominal
Swarm coordination: vehicles must share filtered DAA track data (not raw radar); coordination rate 1-5 Hz per encounter; message size 2-5 KB
Data fusion (onboard compute): each vehicle processes own sensors + received fusion data from swarmmates; compute load ~10-30% of processing budget (ARM Cortex-A72 equivalent, 2-4 W per vehicle)
Encounter resolution: coordinated de-confliction (determine which aircraft maneuvers, when); safety-critical; latency budget <500 ms (REQ007 DO-365 / F3442 alignment)
Post-trial analysis: all DAA encounter events logged for safety case verification; compression ~5x (only non-background radar returns stored)
Typical BVLOS trial: 1-2 encounters per 90-minute flight (cooperative traffic at altitude, possible non-cooperative intruder at range)
Per-vehicle DAA evidence: ~50-100 MB per trial (raw radar scans + fusion track data + encounter decision logs)
Swarm DAA evidence: 5 vehicles × 80 MB = 400 MB per trial; compressed to ~80 MB for archive (REQ012)

growth_projection.6_month: First trial (Protector RG Mk1): single vehicle DAA baseline; 50-100 MB evidence captured. Radar + ADS-B data logged; no swarm coordination. Encounter events: <1 per trial (test airspace cleared). Ground analysis: simple encounter review (post-flight).
growth_projection.12_month: Multi-vehicle DAA (Project Alvina, 5-7 vehicles): coordinated fusion data exchange established; 300-500 MB raw evidence per trial. Fusion track database: 2-3 concurrent vehicle fusion processes = 30-50 msg/sec coordination load. Post-trial analysis: encounter clustering (find all similar-geometry encounters across multiple trials); ML inference on maneuvering decisions (deviation from expected DO-365 resolutions).
growth_projection.24_month: Advanced autonomy pilots (8-10 vehicles): DAA fusion complexity increases; formation-aware de-confliction (swarm geometry constraints). Per-trial evidence: 500-800 MB raw, 100-150 MB compressed. Real-time analysis (in-flight) of encounter trends; dynamic retasking possible (reposition swarm to avoid predicted intruders). AI inference: neural network trained on swarm encounter data; <500 ms latency target for recommendation (Jetson Orin or equivalent).
growth_projection.36_month: GCAP/Tempest advanced trials: cooperative + non-cooperative encounter classification (identity unknown intruders). Per-trial DAA evidence: 1-2 GB raw. Cumulative encounter dataset: 50+ distinct encounter geometries; ML model confidence high. Archive: 200+ MB DAA evidence by end-year 2028.

capacity_model.notes: DAA is the most compute-intensive onboard function (REQ007). Swarm coordination layer (data fusion between aircraft) is safety-critical and latency-sensitive; 500 ms latency budget is tight for lossy networks. Evidence logging (all sensor streams) is high-volume and essential for regulatory audit (DO-365 compliance); compression recommended. AI inference component (post-trial encounter analysis, encounter similarity clustering, decision validation) is optional but valuable for lessons-learned extraction. This model assumes terrestrial DAA (radar + ADS-B); maritime/polar trials may substitute satellite ADS-B (higher latency, sparser updates).

capacity_model.feature: onboard autonomy compute load (swarm decision-making, flight management)
assumptions: Onboard compute per vehicle: ARM-based mission computer (Jetson Nano or TX2 equivalent; 10-20 W TDP budget, limited by battery endurance)
Autonomy stack: flight management system (guidance + navigation) + swarm coordination layer + DAA sensor processing (see NFR-CAP-SWM-002)
Swarm decision-making (REQ008): determine flight plan changes, formation changes, coordinated maneuvers; compute ~5-20 Hz decision cycle
Decision-making latency budget: 100-200 ms (must complete before next C2 update cycle or sensor refresh)
ML inference load (REQ009): optional ML models for anomaly detection, predictive control; requires ~5-10% compute margin per vehicle
Typical compute budget per vehicle: 80% navigation + 10% coordination + 5% ML + 5% margin = 100%
Single-vehicle autonomy load: ~50-60% CPU utilization nominal (headroom for contingencies, high-frequency sensor inputs)
Failure detection (REQ011): monitors for autonomy faults (stuck decision, resource exhaustion, thermal throttling); triggers crew intervention or fallback behavior
Evidence logging: decision logs (one record per cycle, ~100 bytes) + anomaly alerts (~1 KB per alert); 5 Hz decision rate = 500 bytes/sec nominal
Per-trial autonomy evidence: 90-minute trial at 5 Hz = 27,000 decision records + ~50 anomaly alerts = ~2.7 MB per vehicle per trial; 5 vehicles = 13.5 MB per trial

growth_projection.6_month: Single-vehicle baseline (Protector RG Mk1): basic flight management autonomy; no swarm coordination. Compute load: 40-50% per vehicle. Decision logs: 2-3 MB per trial. ML inference: not yet deployed.
growth_projection.12_month: Multi-vehicle coordination (Project Alvina): swarm decision-making added (formation control, coordinated maneuvers). Compute load increases to 60-70% per vehicle (coordination overhead). Decision logs: 10-15 MB per trial. ML inference prototype (anomaly detection) running on ground control; post-flight analysis only.
growth_projection.24_month: Advanced autonomy pilots: dynamic formation changes, re-tasking mid-trial. Onboard ML inference (Jetson Orin) deployed; real-time anomaly detection. Compute load: 80-85% per vehicle (approaching headroom limits). Decision logs: 20+ MB per trial (more detailed state snapshots for complex maneuvers). Evidence volume grows; storage compression ratio 3-5x (decision logs are repetitive).
growth_projection.36_month: GCAP/Tempest advanced trials: autonomous response to unexpected encounters (adversarial swarm behavior). Compute load: 90%+ per vehicle (thermal throttling risk in sustained high-load scenarios). Decision logs: 30-50 MB per trial (every computation decision recorded for explainability). Onboard compute architecture may require secondary processor (heterogeneous compute: main ARM core + EdgeAI accelerator for ML inference).

capacity_model.notes: Onboard autonomy compute is not a Workbench capacity issue; modeled here to complete swarm operational profile. TDP budget is the critical constraint (battery endurance trade-off): increased autonomy compute = reduced flight duration. Decision logs and evidence recording (REQ012 MOR obligations + REQ009 ML explainability) drive evidence volume. Real-time onboard logging (vs. post-flight downlink) requires local flash storage; 5-10 GB SSD per vehicle typical for BVLOS. ML inference evidence (model inputs, outputs, confidence scores) adds 50% to base decision log volume. This capacity model assumes commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) flight computers; swarm-specific autonomy architectures (e.g., distributed consensus, swarm intelligence algorithms) may increase compute load 20-30%.

capacity_model.feature: ground control segment infrastructure (C2, mission planning, autonomy state machine)
assumptions: Ground control segment: pilot + mission controller + flight test engineer; 3-4 personnel per trial
C2 uplink: commands sent to swarm at 0.5-1 Hz per vehicle (low bandwidth to support LTE networks); command message ~200 bytes
Telemetry downlink: received from swarm at 5-10 Hz per vehicle (primary operational information); processed in real-time autonomy state machine (REQ008)
Mission planning software: pre-flight route planning, dynamic retasking (in-flight route changes); compute ~10-50 ms per update
Autonomy state machine: validates in-flight behavior against authorized flight plan, detects deviations, alerts crew; latency <100 ms
Event logging (ground): all commands sent + all telemetry received + state machine decisions logged for post-trial analysis; compression ~2x (repetitive data)
Typical trial: 90 minutes = 5,400 seconds; telemetry stream 5 Hz × 5 vehicles = 25 msg/sec × 5,400 sec = 135,000 messages = ~67 MB raw telemetry (uncompressed)
Ground control data volume per trial: 67 MB telemetry + 5 MB commands + 10 MB state logs = 82 MB raw, 40 MB compressed (REQ012 evidence archive)

Real-time processing throughput: 25 telemetry msg/sec + 5 command msg/sec + 20 coordination msg/sec (swarm fusion data relayed) = 50 msg/sec typical, 200 msg/sec peak (high-frequency sensor data during DAA encounter)
Database: time-series data (telemetry stream); transaction rate 50-200 TPS (messages + state transitions + event triggers)
Concurrent trials: 1 active trial at any time per ground control facility; multiple facilities possible (distributed across UK CAA + MAA + commercial operators)

growth_projection.6_month: Protector RG Mk1 trial: single ground control facility (CAA test site). Autonomy state machine prototype (Python + ROS, <100 ms latency demonstrated). C2 uplink: LTE network; 100 kbps sufficient. Telemetry stream: 25 msg/sec, 12 MB trial event log (uncompressed). Ground infrastructure: commodity laptop (16 GB RAM, dual-core i7) + 4G modem + mission planning software (QGCS variant).
growth_projection.12_month: Project Alvina trials: two ground control facilities (CAA + MAA). Autonomy state machine: production-grade (C++ real-time process, Jetson Orin or x86 real-time CPU); latency <50 ms. Telemetry stream: 50+ msg/sec (coordination data added). Per-trial ground log: 40-50 MB compressed. Mission planning: dynamic retasking prototype (in-flight route changes based on obstacles or crew input).
growth_projection.24_month: Advanced autonomy pilots: 3 concurrent facilities possible (but single-trial execution per period). Autonomy state machine: fault-tolerant (dual redundancy; primary + hot-standby). C2 link: hybrid (LTE primary + satellite backup for remote trials). Telemetry stream: 150+ msg/sec peak (high-frequency DAA updates during encounters). Per-trial ground log: 50-70 MB. Real-time anomaly detection (ML inference on telemetry streams) deployed; alerts crew to unexpected vehicle behaviors.
growth_projection.36_month: GCAP/Tempest trials: 3+ concurrent ground control facilities operational. Autonomy state machine: multi-swarm aware (coordinating decisions across geographically distributed swarms). C2 link: robust (redundant links, cognitive radio fallback). Telemetry stream: 200+ msg/sec sustained (advanced swarm coordination overhead). Per-trial ground log: 70-100 MB. Advanced mission planning (AI-assisted route optimization, dynamic obstacle avoidance). Archive: 100-200 MB ground control logs by end-2028.

capacity_model.notes: Ground control segment is operationally critical; latency and reliability are safety-essential. Real-time C2 downlink (commands to swarm) typically 100-500 kbps per vehicle; uplink (telemetry) higher bandwidth 200+ kbps per vehicle. Network architecture (cellular vs. satellite vs. fixed RF) depends on trial location and connectivity. Autonomy state machine (REQ008) is the core safety function; must be certified or safety-validated. Evidence logging (all telemetry + decisions) is voluminous; compression and selective archival essential. Cost estimate includes: redundant ground stations (failover), Jetson Orin compute (£1500 per unit × 2-3 units), LTE/satellite modem hardware (£500-2000), and cloud storage for trial archives.

performance_budgets.sla: 99% of workspace creation requests shall complete within 3 s; 100% within 5 s. Measured from HTTP POST to ready-to-upload state.
performance_budgets.source: Parent user story NFR - 'Full workbench open-and-review cycle shall not require more than two working days'; Tier-1 interactive user journey.

performance_budgets.feature: Evidence upload throughput (100 MB pack, Step 2)
performance_budgets.sla: 95% of evidence uploads of packs ≤100 MB shall complete within 30 s under OFFICIAL classification, measured from HTTP POST to artefact detector ready. Per-MB throughput: 3–5 MB/s average, p95.
performance_budgets.source: Parent user story NFR - 'Clause-coverage matrix available within 10 minutes of submission upload'; evidenced by per-MB throughput calculation.

performance_budgets.feature: Coverage matrix generation (Step 2 continuation, 100 MB typical pack)
performance_budgets.sla: 95% of clause-coverage matrix generations for typical 100 MB evidence packs shall complete within 10 minutes (600 s) of upload completion, measured from artefact detector ready to matrix PDF download available. 99% within 12 minutes.
performance_budgets.source: Parent user story NFR - 'Clause-coverage matrix available within 10 minutes of submission upload'; end-to-end budget includes large batch ML inference.

performance_budgets.sla: 99% of Readiness Dashboard renders shall complete within 5 s of upload completion signal, measured from request to interactive page ready.
performance_budgets.source: Derived from parent NFR and typical web UI interactivity standards for operator-facing dashboards.

performance_budgets.sla: 95% of Readiness Checks shall complete within 60 s, measured from user 'check' action to pass/fail verdict and list of blocking issues returned.
performance_budgets.source: Parent user story implied SLA; gates the submission workflow.

performance_budgets.sla: 99% of case-open operations shall complete within 10 s, measured from regulator's case-selection click to interactive case panel fully rendered.
performance_budgets.source: Panel-facing interactive path; tight budget to minimize regulator waiting time.

performance_budgets.sla: 95% of AI-assisted narrative flag renders shall complete within 30 minutes (1800 s) of case panel opening, measured from narrative inference trigger to readable flag explanations available for each evidence section.
performance_budgets.source: Parent user story NFR - 'AI-assisted narrative flags within 30 minutes'; one-shot batch inference path.

performance_budgets.sla: 99% of page/section navigation requests within case review shall render within 2 s, measured from user click to new evidence section visible.
performance_budgets.source: Regulator panel workflow interactivity; tighter budget for cached, non-AI navigation.

performance_budgets.sla: 95% of panel-action (review comment, flag, decision state change) writes shall complete within 500 ms, measured from user submit to immutable audit record confirmed in distributed log.
performance_budgets.source: Regulatory audit and forensic traceability requirement; tight SLA for transactional write.

performance_budgets.sla: 95% of decision record PDF exports shall complete within 5 minutes (300 s), measured from user 'export' action to downloadable file ready.
performance_budgets.source: End-of-workflow export; moderate budget for one-shot, non-critical path.

performance_budgets.sla: 99% of geofence-breach events shall result in containment action (command queued to avionics) within 500 ms (hard deadline 1 s). Measured from breach detection to control signal dispatch.
performance_budgets.source: Parent NFR REQ005; safety-critical containment loop.

performance_budgets.sla: 99% of cooperative DAA encounters shall be resolved (maneuver command issued or non-interference confirmed) within 5 s of first detection. Non-cooperative: within 30 s per DO-365 scenario profile. Hard deadline 60 s.
performance_budgets.source: Parent NFR REQ007; DO-365 compliance for autonomous systems.

performance_budgets.sla: 95% of C2 round-trip commands shall exhibit latency ≤1 s (tactical), p99 ≤2 s. Measured from operator input to aircraft acknowledgment received.
performance_budgets.source: Vibe Requirements - C2 link latency (MeaningfulHumanControl) Tolerable <1 s.

performance_budgets.sla: 95% of swarm consensus cycles (collision avoidance, formation coordination) shall complete within 200 ms of most-recent sensor update from any aircraft, measured at swarm decision heartbeat frequency.
performance_budgets.source: Derived from Parent NFR REQ008 (Swarm Decision-Making); control loop alignment.

performance_budgets.feature: Onboard ML inference for autonomy decisions
performance_budgets.sla: 95% of onboard ML inference for tactical decisions (e.g., next-waypoint selection, DAA evasion maneuver) shall complete within 200 ms, p99 within 400 ms.
performance_budgets.source: Parent NFR REQ009 (ML Assurance Case); airborne autonomy constraint.

performance_budgets.feature: Flight termination command to motor cutoff
performance_budgets.sla: 100% of flight termination commands shall result in motor shutdown within 500 ms (hard deadline), measured from command issuance at operator or autonomous trigger to propeller stop.
performance_budgets.source: Parent NFR REQ011 (Flight Termination); safety-critical hard real-time deadline.

performance_budgets.feature: Evidence search across classification boundary (10-year archive)
performance_budgets.sla: 95% of evidence archive searches (by keyword, regulation clause, aircraft ID, date range) shall return results within 5 s for queries spanning up to 10 years of prior trial records, measured from user query submit to result list rendered.
performance_budgets.source: Derived from workbench archive use-cases; moderate priority for non-critical regulatory queries.

performance_budgets.feature: Coverage matrix generation for 500 MB evidence pack (worst case)
performance_budgets.sla: 95% of coverage matrix generations for large 500 MB evidence packs shall complete within 20 minutes (1200 s), p99 within 30 minutes.
performance_budgets.source: Worst-case scenario; may require horizontal scaling of inference tier.

performance_budgets.feature: Panel action log write under high contention (10 simultaneous reviewers)
performance_budgets.sla: 95% of action log writes during peak panel review (10+ concurrent reviewers) shall complete within 1 s (elevated p95 due to lock contention), p99 within 2 s.
performance_budgets.source: Derived from panel review concurrency model; stress-tested scenario.

performance_budgets.feature: Loss-of-C2 link detection and autonomous survival activation
performance_budgets.sla: 100% of C2 link loss shall be detected and autonomous survival behavior initiated within 1 s of link loss onset, measured from last valid uplink command to first autonomous flight control input.
performance_budgets.source: Vibe Requirements - C2 link latency (C2LinkResilience) and REQ006; safety-critical failsafe.

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: Weekly: 22:00-23:00 Friday UTC; Monthly: 02:00-04:00 first Sunday; classified-tier-aware blackout during active submissions
single_points_of_failure: Single primary database (applicant evidence store)
Classification boundary service (shared with panel tooling)
Identity provider integration (no local fallback)
availability_requirements.reasoning: Applicant workspace (intake, pack assembly, pre-submission) operates asynchronously; business hours availability (09:00-17:30 UK time) acceptable with graceful degradation outside. Parent user story permits offline operation outside hours. 99.5% = 43.8 h/year provides cost-effective redundancy for non-critical submission prep. RPO 15 min balances draft applicant tolerance vs backup overhead.
availability_requirements.recommendation: Implement read-replica active-passive failover with DNS switchover (15 min recovery window acceptable for async intake). Cross-zone backup to secondary UK datacenter. Local cache of classification marking service to survive provider outage. Automate weekly backup validation.

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: No maintenance during scheduled panel windows (Mon-Fri 10:00-16:00 UK time). Emergency patches only; canary deploy outside panel hours with 24h observation
single_points_of_failure: Shared database backend (if not replicated across zones)
Classification boundary service (hard isolation prevents cross-tier failover)
Deliberation log write path (any loss = audit failure)
availability_requirements.reasoning: Joint panel tooling is safety-critical for approval continuity. 99.9% SLA = 8.76 h/year acceptable for planned maintenance only. Active-active across zones required to achieve transparent failover during live deliberation. RPO must be zero (no lost panel action; each approval is non-repudiable). RTO 5 min accounts for client reconnection; 0 sec ideal but 5 min acceptable for session re-establishment.
availability_requirements.recommendation: Deploy active-active pair across two sovereign UK zones with synchronous replication. Classification boundary service must have independent failover (no cross-tier dependencies). Deliberation log uses append-only ledger with immediate durable write; dual-write to evidence archive on commit. Load balancer with health checks every 5 sec. Test failover monthly during dark hours. Panel schedule coordination with IT ops for maintenance windows.

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: Read availability degradation acceptable; write freezes 1h quarterly on explicit scheduling; no maintenance during 48h post-decision window
external_dependencies: Regulatory retention service (National Archives, UK sovereign)
single_points_of_failure: Primary archive write path (if single-node)
Declassification service (no local fallback)
National Archives connectivity (external regulatory dependency)
availability_requirements.reasoning: Post-decision archive is regulatory-retention critical (90+ year mandatory hold). Durable write availability 99.99% = 52.6 min/year downtime acceptable for quarterly maintenance only. Read availability 99.9% (8.76 h/year) acceptable for regulatory retrieval (infrequent, off-peak). RPO zero on write (decision record cannot be lost); RPO acceptable on read (stale archive acceptable for investigation review if indexed). RTO 120 min for full rebuild from distributed replicas.
availability_requirements.recommendation: Implement 3-2-1 backup rule: (3) copies of archive — primary write-optimized node + two read-replica zones, (2) storage media — hot replicas + cold archive tape in UK facility, (1) off-site — encrypted backup to National Archives sovereign infrastructure. Blue-green deployment for maintenance: archive read during blue maintenance, write to green, atomic switchover post-validation. Monthly archive integrity check (cryptographic hash of all case decision PDFs).

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: Weekly cache invalidation 23:00 Friday (CDN edge cache); no read disruption. Source updates (CAA/MAA clause releases) publish M-W 14:00-16:00 with staged rollout (5% edge -> 25% -> 100% over 2h)
external_dependencies: CDN service for clause distribution (sovereign edge nodes UK + Gibraltar)
single_points_of_failure: Clause source feed (if single CAA/MAA endpoint)
CDN edge cache (if localized failure)
Clause versioning service (no fallback to offline bundle)
availability_requirements.reasoning: Clause library is read-heavy, low write rate (weekly updates). 99.95% = 4.38 h/year supports distributed caching (CDN) with local fallback. RPO 60 min acceptable (panel can use stale clause if necessary; audit trail captures version at decision time). RTO 10 min via CDN failover; offline bundle as SPOF mitigation.
availability_requirements.recommendation: CDN-fronted (Cloudflare UK sovereign) with edge cache TTL 24h. Primary source feeds from CAA + MAA with dual-write redundancy. Fallback to embedded offline clause bundle (dated, but functional). Version all clauses with commit hash; audit log records which version applied to each decision. Automate clause freshness check on panel login (alert if >7 days stale).

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: No scheduled maintenance during MOD classification office hours (08:30-17:00 Mon-Fri). Emergency patches via MOD ITSO approval only; failover to backup instance (pre-authorized by CAP 722H process)
external_dependencies: MOD identity provider (single-sign-on for classified access)
MOD marking service (CAP 722H / JSP 440 classification automation)
CAA unclassified identity provider (no cross-tier authentication)
single_points_of_failure: MOD identity provider (if unavailable, no classified access possible)
Marking service (any failure blocks evidence classification)
Cross-tier isolation logic (if bypassed, evidence leaks)
availability_requirements.reasoning: Classification boundary is a hard security requirement; any failure is a security event triggering incident response. 99.9% acceptable (8.76 h/year) given MOD IT ops support and pre-authorized failover. RPO zero (classification decision must be logged immediately; no loss acceptable). RTO 2 min very tight (pre-staged backup instance, DNS switchover only). Active-active across MOD + CAA zones with cryptographic evidence handoff.
availability_requirements.recommendation: Deploy twin active instances: MOD-hosted classified instance + CAA-hosted unclassified instance. No shared database; classify/declassify happens at boundary service only. Every classification event signed with cryptographic commitment to audit log. Dual-approve failover (MOD ITSO + CAA IT Director). Test failover quarterly with MOD ITSO present. Real-time alerting on any classification boundary error; escalates to security team within 30 sec.

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: Log rotation/archival: off-peak, async, never blocks write. No downtime acceptable. Defrag archived ledger: quarterly, on cold archive only (no impact to active log)
external_dependencies: Distributed log storage (Kafka / Pulsar sovereign cluster)
single_points_of_failure: Log write path (ANY failure = audit loss = non-defensible decision)
Timestamp service (if delayed, audit trail loses chronological proof)
availability_requirements.reasoning: Audit/deliberation log is the regulatory record of truth. 100% write availability non-negotiable; any lost action means the decision is indefensible in accident inquiry. Append-only ledger ensures monotonic writes; distributed across 3+ nodes. RPO zero (every panel action committed before UI acknowledgment). RTO zero (read failures acceptable; write-path fault triggers immediate escalation to MOD + CAA leadership).
availability_requirements.recommendation: Use append-only distributed log (Kafka 3-node cluster, replication factor 3, min.insync.replicas=2). Every panel action triggers synchronous write + cryptographic timestamp. Failures logged to immediate SMS/Slack alert. 30-second human verification required for any failed write (manual retry + investigation). Monthly log integrity audit: cryptographic hash of entire log segment, verify against signed manifest. Archive completed deliberation logs monthly to cold storage with immutable wrapper.

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: No maintenance during authorized trial windows. Pre-flight sensor check mandatory; if DAA unavailable at T-15 min, trial aborts. Post-flight inspection window only.
external_dependencies: DO-365 / ED-267 compliance framework (external regulatory standard)
GNSS constellation availability (dependent on SA status)
single_points_of_failure: Single radar sensor (if only one on lead aircraft)
ADS-B receiver (if failure blocks non-cooperative detection)
Sensor fusion algorithm (if single-point computation)
availability_requirements.reasoning: DAA during flight is safety-of-life-critical. Sensor availability 99.99% required (52.6 min/year acceptable downtime, but only acceptable post-flight or pre-trial abort). RPO zero (every detection event must be logged). RTO zero (failover to backup sensor transparent to flight, <100 ms). Dual independent sensors per aircraft with voting logic.
availability_requirements.recommendation: Equip each swarm aircraft with dual independent DAA sensors (radar + vision-based, or dual-frequency radar). Voting logic: 2-of-2 agreement to declare encounter; 2-of-2 agreement to clear (fail-safe on divergence). Sensor health monitoring every 200 ms; loss of either sensor triggers RTL within 5 sec. Test sensor redundancy pre-flight via known encounter stimuli (recorded ADS-B replay). Log all sensor detections + decisions to flight data recorder independent of telemetry.

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: RF spectrum testing 08:00-10:00 Thursday (no trial flights). Ground station software updates during non-operational hours only; aircraft remain autonomous until C2 restored
external_dependencies: Spectrum regulator (Ofcom UK licensed band 2.4 GHz or specific MOD UHF allocation)
Ground control station infrastructure (sovereign UK location)
Inter-vehicle communication network (swarm mesh, independent from C2)
single_points_of_failure: Single ground transmitter (if only one control station)
Frequency band loss (if RF jammed or spectrum conflict)
Autonomous survival logic (if pilot override always demanded, loss of C2 = uncontrolled flight)
availability_requirements.reasoning: C2 link availability 99% tolerable per REQ006 + Vibe targets (C2 loss survivable 90+ sec autonomous). RTO 5 min acceptable (swarm holds formation, RTL initiated post-recovery). RPO zero (every C2 command logged to flight recorder; loss of link event + recovery time recorded). Failover to autonomous decision-making within 5 sec of link loss detection.
availability_requirements.recommendation: Dual-path C2 (licensed UHF + satellite fallback, or dual-frequency ground stations). Link health monitoring: heartbeat every 500 ms; loss detected within 100 ms. Autonomous hold-safe logic: maintain formation, reduce altitude to ground-safe landing zone, execute RTL if C2 absent >90 sec. Ground control station redundancy: dual transmitters at primary site + secondary site failover. Test link loss recovery monthly via controlled RF blackout (Faraday cage).

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: Flight termination system isolation testing: controlled environment only, no active aircraft nearby. Annual certification audit by independent third party (CAA-approved). Pre-flight verification mandatory; any detected degradation triggers trial abort.
external_dependencies: Power supply redundancy (battery + redundant buck converter per motor)
single_points_of_failure: Single termination command path (if only one receiver channel)
Shared motor controller with primary autonomy (if both use same MCU)
Single power supply (if battery failure kills both termination + flight systems)
availability_requirements.reasoning: Flight termination is last-resort safety function. 99.99% availability = 52.6 min/year acceptable (only if independent from autonomy path per K6 principle). RPO zero (termination command must execute immediately on receipt; no queuing). RTO zero (independent hardware path, no software involved, <50 ms motor cutoff).
availability_requirements.recommendation: Independent hardware command path for each aircraft: dedicated RF receiver channel (separate from C2), independent power supply (super-capacitor or isolated battery), hardware-only motor cutoff (no firmware involved). Crew activation: redundant operator input (2-person verification) + automatic watchdog on lost-link (RTL vs termination decided by altitude: <30m AGL = terminate, >30m = RTL). Pre-flight verification: apply termination command to one test motor, verify instant cutoff. Log termination command + motor response to flight recorder before cutoff.

availability_requirements.planned_maintenance_windows: No maintenance during authorized trial window. All preventive maintenance > 30 days pre-trial; emergency patches only if zero safety impact (no changes to C2, autonomy, or termination logic)
external_dependencies: Range Safety Case infrastructure (if MOD range trial)
ATC coordination (Ofcom / NATS if shared airspace)
Trial authorization validation service (CAA final approval)
single_points_of_failure: Single ground control workstation (if only one pilot console)
Network link to airfield (if single ISP/fiber path)
Telemetry relay station (if single antenna)
availability_requirements.reasoning: Ground control availability during authorized trial window is 100% non-negotiable. Parent user story states trial is not blocked by Workbench unavailability, but ground control failure = trial abort with potential safety risk. Active-active dual station setup required; transparent switchover within 1 sec.
availability_requirements.recommendation: Deploy dual ground control stations (primary + hot-standby) with identical crew, both monitoring telemetry. Network redundancy: dual ISP paths to airfield (primary + secondary provider). Automated failover: if primary station loses telemetry >500 ms, crew on standby assumes C2 via hot-switch (pre-briefed). Telemetry logging to redundant recorders (independent from flight data recorder). Test ground-station failover monthly with live crew; post-test debrief + safety review.

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Request rate >500 req/s per instance; CPU >70%; scale up +2 instances; scale down at <200 req/s
scalability.bottleneck_component: None identified; stateless design enables elastic scaling

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Stateless applicant-mode: any edge node. Sticky sessions for inspector panel-mode (session affinity 3-4 hours). CDN (CloudFront) for static assets; cache TTL 1 hour.
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Single instance breaks at ~500-800 req/s (p95 response time >2s). At 100 concurrent users with mixed applicant/inspector workload, expect ~1500 req/s sustained; requires 3-4 instances baseline.
dependencies: App-server capacity (NFR-SCL-WB-002)
Database read replicas for inspector sessions (NFR-SCL-WB-005)
CDN availability for static content

scalability_assessment.component: Artefact detector / Coverage matrix engine (ML inference, GPU-backed)

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Job queue depth >20 items; spin up GPU worker (+1 Jetson Orin or A100 equiv); target p95 latency <600s (NFR-LAT-003)
scalability.bottleneck_component: GPU availability; OCR + semantic inference highly serialisable; batch efficiency critical
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: GBP 1.50/hour per GPU instance (Jetson Orin) or GBP 8.00/hour per A100 (cloud)

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Job queue (Redis or RabbitMQ) distributes coverage-matrix requests to worker pool. Batch processing: aggregate 5-10 concurrent requests per GPU for efficiency. Inference time scales linearly with evidence pack size (100 MB ~240s nominal per budget; 500 MB ~480s per NFR-LAT-018).
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Single GPU instance breaks at ~1 concurrent inference job (OCR + semantic classification serialize on GPU). At 5 concurrent submissions (100 MB avg), requires 5 GPU workers to meet p95 600s SLA. Cost scales ~GBP 7.50/hour at 5x load. Batch inference efficiency drops if queue drains <2 sec between jobs (underutilization >30%).
dependencies: GPU inventory (capex-constrained; shared with AI flag narrative service NFR-SCL-WB-003)
Evidence store read performance (S3 GetObject latency <100ms assumed)
AI inference latency budgets (OCR 180s, semantic classification 200s per NFR-LAT-003)

scalability_assessment.component: AI flag narrative service (LLM-backed, GPU inference)

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Narrative request queue >10 items; spin up GPU worker (vLLM or TensorRT instance); target p95 latency <1800s (NFR-LAT-007, 30 min SLA)
scalability.bottleneck_component: GPU memory (16GB min for 7B model; 40GB for 13B); batch tokenization throughput; model serving framework overhead

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Request queue (async task system) aggregates narrative requests from inspector case reviews. Batch inference: process 2-4 concurrent narratives per GPU per time-step (token streaming). Evidence chunking (60s per budget NFR-LAT-007) must complete before inference.
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Single A100 (40GB) GPU instance handles ~2 concurrent narrative inferences at full-speed token streaming (100 tokens/s). At 20 concurrent narrative requests, requires 10 GPU instances for p95 1800s SLA. Batch efficiency drops sharply if <1 inference in flight (idle time >40% at low concurrency).
dependencies: GPU inventory (shared with coverage matrix engine; total pool must be 10-15 GPUs for both services at 5x growth)
Evidence chunking latency (60s per budget)
Model serving framework (vLLM, TensorRT) stability and throughput guarantees

scalability_assessment.component: Classification boundary service (OFFICIAL vs OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE vs SECRET vs STRAP)

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: OFFICIAL tier: horizontal; scale on QPS >100 queries/s per replica. OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE: 2x on-prem replicas (fixed capacity). SECRET/STRAP: 1x air-gapped instance (no scaling; rebuild triggers slow re-provisioning 2-4 hours per ada-findings).
scalability.bottleneck_component: SECRET/STRAP tier rebuild latency after incident; separate DB replica for upper tiers incurs 40% query overhead vs OFFICIAL tier
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: OFFICIAL: GBP 1.50/hour per replica. OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE on-prem: GBP 800/month per replica. SECRET/STRAP air-gapped: GBP 2000/month (dedicated facility + compliance auditing)

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Tier-specific routing enforced at BFF layer. OFFICIAL queries (60% by 2028) → cloud replicas (auto-scale 1-4 instances). OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE (25%) → on-premises DB (2 replicas, manual failover). SECRET (10%) → air-gapped instance (no failover; RTx planned if failure occurs). STRAP (5%) → MOD-dedicated infrastructure (out-of-scope for CAA scalability).
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: OFFICIAL tier: single DB instance breaks at ~100 QPS (p95 latency >1s). At 100 concurrent inspectors with peak query rate ~150 QPS, requires 2-3 read replicas. OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE: fixed at 2 replicas; overload causes queue backlog. SECRET: fixed at 1 instance; no scale-out; rebuild on failure is 2-4 hour outage (compliance implications severe).
dependencies: Data segregation architecture (JSP 440 compliance)
On-premises facility capacity (OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE + SECRET instances)
Air-gapped network for SECRET/STRAP (bandwidth constraint: 10 Mbps link typical; not a scalability issue for audit-log volume but impacts backup/restore time)

scalability_assessment.component: Clause library search (full-text + semantic, read replicas)

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Search QPS >50 queries/s; add read replica; target p95 latency <500ms (interactive inspector workload)
scalability.bottleneck_component: Full-text index size (100 MB per 200 MB library); semantic search (vector embedding refresh latency ~10s per library update)
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: GBP 1.00/hour per read replica (elasticsearch t3.large equiv)

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Read-heavy workload; all inspector queries route to replicas (master only receives incremental index updates on library revision 3-4x/year). CDN caches static clause-library PDFs (OFFICIAL only; 200 MB library, 1-hour TTL). Semantic search (AI-powered) adds 5s latency; async pre-computation recommended.
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Single ES node breaks at ~50 QPS (p95 latency >500ms at complex semantic queries). At 80 concurrent inspectors (prep week peak), expect ~60 QPS sustained; requires 2-3 read replicas. Vector embedding index (ML inference) is separate from full-text; adds 5-10% to query time.
dependencies: Elasticsearch cluster stability (shard allocation, rebalance impact)
Vector embedding model serving (async; separate GPU workers)
Library versioning (S3 immutable versioning required; 5-year retention = 4 GB cumulative)

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Automatic (S3 auto-scales to millions of objects). Partition by classification tier (separate buckets: s3://caa-wb-official, s3://caa-wb-official-sensitive, etc.) for access control + cost allocation.
scalability.bottleneck_component: None; object storage scales beyond project scope. Metadata query (evidence file inventory) may bottleneck if not indexed (see NFR-SCL-WB-007).
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: GBP 0.023/GB/month (standard) or GBP 0.004/GB/month (Glacier Deep Archive for video evidence)

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Applicant-facing: PUT 100-500 MB evidence packs (100 MB avg); throughput 3-5 MB/s (per NFR-LAT-002). Inspector-facing: GET metadata queries (not bulk downloads). Archive: transition to Glacier after case closure (90-day SLA per REQ020); cost >90% reduction. Lifecycle policy: OFFICIAL →Glacier after 2 years; OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE →encrypted Glacier after 3 years; SECRET/STRAP remain standard (instant retrieval for investigation).
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Conceptually unlimited. Practical limits: AWS bucket quota (1000 buckets; we use 4 for tiers = no issue). GetObject latency SLA (100 ms p95 assumed) maintained by AWS. Cost at 36-month projection (140 GB): ~GBP 3.20/month standard + Glacier transition savings.
dependencies: Metadata query service (separate DB index, see NFR-SCL-WB-007)
S3 bucket policies (classification-aware IAM roles)
Encryption key management (per-tier KMS keys; compliance overhead)

scalability_assessment.component: Primary database (case state, deliberation, metadata index)

scalability.scaling_type: vertical (consistency-constrained) + read replicas for horizontal scale-out
scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Write throughput >100 TPS (transactions/sec); scale master vertically (bigger instance). Read replicas: add 1 replica per 50 concurrent readers; target <100 ms query latency.
scalability.bottleneck_component: Write serialization at master (ACID enforcement for case state consistency). At p95 action-log write (panel deliberation), 500 ms SLA tight (NFR-LAT-009); consensus overhead when >10 concurrent writers.
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: GBP 2.50/hour master (db.r6g.2xlarge equiv, 2 vCPU, 16GB RAM). GBP 1.50/hour per read replica.

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Write-heavy applicant-mode (submission upload state machine); moderate write load inspector-mode (case annotations, panel-action log). Read replicas satisfy inspector case-load queries (concurrent case viewers). Separate read replica for SECRET/STRAP tier (access control + audit isolation).
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Master instance breaks at ~150 write TPS + 500 read TPS (single r6g.2xlarge). At 100 concurrent users (30 active writers, 70 readers), expect ~80 write TPS + 400 read TPS sustained. Requires 1 master + 3 read replicas by 2028. Action-log write contention (panel deliberation, 10+ concurrent panelists): 500 ms p95 budget becomes tight; optimistic locking or separate audit-log shard recommended.
dependencies: Read replica consistency (replication lag <1s acceptable for inspector queries)
Backup strategy (nightly snapshots; weekly archival to S3)
Action-log write optimization (distributed ledger architecture for audit trail, see NFR-SCL-WB-008)

scalability_assessment.component: Audit / deliberation log (immutable append-only distributed ledger, sharded by panel)

scalability.scaling_type: horizontal via sharding by panel ID
scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Panel action write rate >30 actions/sec; shard by panel_id (1 shard per active panel). If single shard exceeds 50 write TPS, split shard (rare; requires manual intervention).
scalability.bottleneck_component: Write quorum latency (replication consensus for 3-replica ledger); p95 500 ms budget (NFR-LAT-009) driven by quorum ACK wait time (~150 ms per budget)
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: GBP 2.00/month per shard replica (low volume; S3 versioning + checksums sufficient for immutability)

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Sharded write path: each panel session writes to dedicated shard (panel_id hash → shard replica 1,2,3). Quorum write: 2-of-3 replicas ACK within 150 ms (SLA envelope). Read path: any replica sufficient (eventual consistency acceptable for audit review, latency <1s).
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Single shard (1 panel) handles ~50 actions/sec (100 bytes each = 5 KB/sec). Panel deliberation: 8-20 concurrent panelists, ~30-50 actions/sec during voting. This workload fits in 1 shard comfortably. Quorum write latency (network round-trip + fsync) is the breaking point; LAN with <10 ms latency required (co-locate replicas in same data center). WAN failover (cross-region replica) breaks 500 ms p95 SLA (latency jumps to 300-600 ms).
dependencies: Low-latency LAN (same availability zone); no cross-region replication
Fsync/durability guarantees (WAL, write-ahead logging enabled)
Panel session affinity (all actions from 1 panel route to same shard; no cross-shard transactions)

scalability_assessment.component: Swarm telemetry and C2 communication (5-20 vehicle ops)

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Telemetry message rate >150 msg/sec (10 vehicles × 10 Hz + coordination); spin up dedicated ground-control compute instance. C2 link bandwidth: 500+ kbps per vehicle (not a scalability bottleneck for LTE/5G).
scalability.bottleneck_component: Ground control autonomy state machine latency <100 ms (REQ008); serializable at single process; throughput limited by single-core CPU clock speed
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: GBP 1.20/hour per swarm ground-control instance (4-core real-time CPU, 8 GB RAM, dual LTE/5G modems)

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Per-swarm dedicated compute instance (no multi-swarm sharing; isolation for safety). Telemetry stream to time-series DB (InfluxDB or TimescaleDB) at 150 msg/sec throughput. Real-time autonomy state machine runs on same instance (low latency <100 ms). Event logging to evidence store async (batch every 10s).
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Single ground-control instance (4-core i7 + 8GB) handles 5-10 vehicle swarm (50-100 msg/sec) comfortably. At 20-vehicle swarm (200+ msg/sec peak), single instance approaches CPU ceiling; add second instance for parallel state machines (distributed consensus required, adds latency). C2 uplink bandwidth per vehicle: 100-500 kbps (LTE/5G provides >1 Mbps per device; not a bottleneck).
dependencies: C2 link connectivity (LTE/5G coverage required; satellite link fallback optional but adds latency)
Real-time kernel (Linux kernel pre-emption patches or RTOS for <100 ms latency guarantee)
Telemetry buffering (ring buffer if state machine stalls; max age <10 ms)

scalability_assessment.component: DAA sensor data fusion and encounter resolution (multi-vehicle coordination)

scalability.scaling_type: horizontal via sensor fusion sharding by encounter cluster
scalability.auto_scale_trigger: DAA encounter events >10/hour; spin up ML inference worker (Jetson Orin) for encounter classification + recommendation (async post-trial). Real-time coordination: 500 ms latency budget (NFR-LAT-012); no horizontal scaling possible (pairwise consensus is O(n²)).
scalability.bottleneck_component: Swarm consensus protocol (pairwise de-confliction decision); latency scales O(n²) with swarm size; 10-vehicle swarm requires ~100 pairwise comparisons, ~50 coordination messages each cycle

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Real-time DAA coordination: broadcast-gossip protocol (each vehicle sends filtered track data to swarmmates at 1-5 Hz). Data fusion: onboard compute only (no ground uplink during encounter; bandwidth constrained). Post-trial encounter analysis: ground-control ML workers cluster similar encounters, train anomaly detector (async, not latency-critical).
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Real-time pairwise de-confliction: 5-vehicle swarm → 10 pairwise comparisons per cycle, ~50 ms compute time (Jetson level). 10-vehicle swarm → 45 pairwise comparisons, ~200 ms compute (Orin level, within 500 ms budget). 20-vehicle swarm → 190 pairwise comparisons, ~800 ms compute (exceeds 500 ms SLA; sequential resolution breaks latency budget). Mitigation: hierarchical swarm clustering (sub-swarms de-conflict internally, then parent de-conflict clusters) or distributed decision making (each aircraft pair makes independent decision).
dependencies: Onboard compute budget (DAA inference + coordination messaging consume 30-40% of available CPU; battery endurance trade-off)
Coordination link reliability (inter-vehicle mesh network; loss-of-link triggers fallback behavior)
Ground-truth encounter dataset (50+ real encounters required to train anomaly detector for GCAP/Tempest trials)

scalability_assessment.component: Onboard autonomy compute (decision-making, ML inference for tactical decisions)

scalability.scaling_type: vertical (battery/thermal budget constrained; no horizontal scaling possible)
scalability.auto_scale_trigger: None; single compute platform per vehicle. ML inference load: add GPU accelerator (Jetson Xavier or Orin) if onboard compute budget >80% utilization.
scalability.bottleneck_component: Battery endurance (every watt of compute = 10-20 min flight time lost). Thermal dissipation (sustained high compute throttles CPU clock). TDP budget: 10-20 W total per vehicle.
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: GBP 800 hardware cost per vehicle (Jetson Nano) or GBP 3000 (Jetson Orin) amortized

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Single onboard compute platform per vehicle (ARM Cortex-A72 or better). Decision-making cycle: 5-10 Hz (nominal) or 20+ Hz (encounter mode). ML inference (optional): 200 ms latency budget (NFR-LAT-015); Jetson inference time 50-80 ms for typical model.
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Onboard compute is TDP-constrained, not throughput-constrained. At 85% utilization, throttling begins (thermal runaway risk). At 95% utilization, decision latency degrades (>200 ms; breaks autonomy loop). Advanced autonomy (GCAP/Tempest trials) requires secondary GPU accelerator (Jetson Orin) to stay <80% utilization.
dependencies: Battery thermal management (heat sink design, airflow adequate)
Flight duration SLA (longer missions require more efficient algorithms; compute off-loading to ground not viable for <500 ms latency decisions)
GPU accelerator availability (Jetson supply chain constraints in 2026-2027 may impact GCAP/Tempest ramp)

scalability_assessment.component: Ground control segment (C2 link, mission planning, autonomy state machine)

scalability.scaling_type: horizontal via geographic distribution (multiple ground stations)
scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Concurrent trials >1; establish second ground-control facility. Multi-facility coordination: not implemented (trials execute sequentially or in different airspace); no cross-facility load balancing.
scalability.bottleneck_component: Mission planning optimization (dynamic retasking); compute time scales O(n) with number of waypoints; 500 waypoint mission = 10-50 ms re-planning time. C2 link latency (shared spectrum); at 2-3 concurrent swarms, spectrum congestion possible.
scalability.scaling_unit_cost: GBP 3.10/hour per facility (compute + LTE/satellite modem + secure communications)

scalability_assessment.load_distribution: Per-facility ground control (no geographic load balancing). Mission planning: pre-flight optimization + in-flight dynamic re-tasking (async, <100 ms latency acceptable). Autonomy state machine: dedicated real-time process (1 core per swarm). Telemetry stream: time-series DB (shared across all swarms at facility).
scalability_assessment.breaking_point: Single facility handles 1 active swarm comfortably (6 personnel: pilot, mission controller, 2 flight engineers, 2 observers). At 2-3 concurrent trials, facility becomes congested (personnel limits more than compute limits). C2 link bandwidth: 5 vehicles × 500 kbps each = 2.5 Mbps total; LTE/5G easily supports. Spectrum sharing (3+ concurrent swarms over shared frequency band) may require cognitive radio or licensed-band coordination.
dependencies: Geographic diversity (UK-South + UK-North coverage; no long-haul cross-facility synchronization)
C2 link diversity (LTE primary + satellite backup recommended for remote trials)
Mission planning optimizer performance (O(n) or better; O(n²) algorithms break re-tasking SLA)

security_performance.feature: TLS termination at CDN edge
security_performance.reasoning: TLS 1.3 with session resumption adds ~5 ms on new connections; negligible on keep-alive. CAA/MAA Workbench handles classified material (OFFICIAL/OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE/SECRET/STRAP), so TLS is mandatory. UK-sovereign CDN providers (Zenlayer, Cloudflare private core) terminate TLS at edge to minimize latency impact on applicant portal. Noyce NFR-LAT-001 budgets 100 ms for 'network + CDN'; TLS overhead is absorbed within that budget.
security_performance.recommendation: Use TLS 1.3 with ECDHE + AES-GCM. Enable session tickets for 1-hour resumption window. Monitor certificate validation latency via synthetic tests. Acceptable at current 5 ms overhead.

security_performance.feature: WAF inspection (applicant portal ingress)
security_performance.reasoning: ModSecurity-compatible WAF (e.g., AWS WAFv2, UK Crown Commercial Service approved) inspects inbound applicant requests. Typical overhead: 10-30 ms depending on rule depth. CAA Workbench requires deep inspection of evidence uploads for malicious PDF/Office payloads and SQL injection patterns, so ruleset is non-trivial. Noyce NFR-LAT-001 'web_server_auth' budgets 200 ms; WAF adds 15 ms (~7.5% of that component). Applicant portal has high attack surface (credential-stuffing, large-payload DoS); WAF rule set should be defensive but not severe.
security_performance.recommendation: Enable WAF with OWASP CRS 3.3.2 baseline + custom rules for malicious document detection. Baseline latency ~10 ms; monitor and tune to stay under 20 ms p95. Cache WAF decisions per source IP to reduce overhead on repeated requests.

security_performance.feature: OAuth 2.1 / OIDC authentication (MOD + CAA IdP)
security_performance.reasoning: PKCE-based OAuth 2.1 with MOD IdP (classified users) and CAA IdP (civil users). First auth per session: 150-250 ms (IdP round-trip + JWT validation). Cached sessions: <5 ms. Noyce NFR-LAT-001 'web_server_auth' budgets 200 ms; first-auth at p95 is 100 ms overhead. Critical risk: IdP outage becomes single point of failure. Sessions are stateful for panel-facing endpoints and stateless for applicant portal. Classification tier transitions require re-authentication at higher tier (cannot cross-auth).
security_performance.recommendation: Cache OAuth tokens for 1-hour TTL with refresh-token rotation. Use distributed cache (Redis Cluster) to support multi-region failover. Implement graceful degradation: if IdP is down >30s, serve cached token for low-risk applicant actions (read-only). Monitor IdP latency with synthetic tests; set SLA alert if p95 > 100 ms. Separate IdP endpoints for MOD (classified) and CAA (civil) to enable independent failover.

security_performance.feature: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) + classification boundary check
security_performance.reasoning: RBAC decision + classification level check (OFFICIAL vs OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE vs SECRET) on every authorised action. Typical overhead: 15-30 ms (policy evaluation + cache lookup). Noyce NFR-LAT-009 'classification_check_inline' budgets 150 ms for panel-action audit write; RBAC + classification check is part of that. Workbench enforces clearance-level boundaries: a RESTRICTED-USER cannot view SECRET-marked evidence even if RBAC permits. Cache RBAC decisions per user session to amortize cost.
security_performance.recommendation: Use cached RBAC decisions (5-minute TTL) + inline classification check. Pre-compute classification boundary matrix on session init to avoid repeated computation. Monitor AuthZ cache hit rate; target >90%. Flag any AuthZ decision >30 ms as anomaly.

security_performance.feature: Classification marking validation on document read (OFFICIAL/SENSITIVE/SECRET)
security_performance.reasoning: On evidence download or audit log read, system must validate and mark classification headers (Content-Type, x-gov-uk-classification). PDF metadata and metadata stripping adds latency. Noyce NFR-LAT-002 'content_classification' budgets 2000 ms for initial upload; subsequent reads are cheaper (<50 ms per document per Noyce guidance). Noyce NFR-LAT-017 'classification_boundary_check' budgets 500 ms out of 5000 ms total search latency. Classification validation is critical security control; cannot be skipped even at performance cost.
security_performance.recommendation: Validate classification on first read from database; cache result with TTL=1 hour. Batch classification checks for bulk exports (e.g., decision record PDF). Monitor classification validation latency; set SLA alert if p95 > 50 ms per document. Use asynchronous metadata marking for non-blocking workflow.

security_performance.feature: Encryption at rest (AES-256 GCM per classification tier)
security_performance.reasoning: Evidence database and audit logs encrypted at rest with AES-256 GCM. Key hierarchy: per-classification-tier keys (OFFICIAL key, SENSITIVE key, SECRET key). Decryption on read adds 20-50 ms depending on key fetching from external HSM. Noyce NFR-LAT-006 'evidence_metadata_load' budgets 1500 ms for case assembly; encryption overhead is ~2% of that. Parent NFR requires handling SECRET material on UK sovereign infrastructure; GCHQ standards demand encryption.
security_performance.recommendation: Use AWS KMS or UK Crown-hosted HSM with 1-second key fetch SLA. Pre-load decryption keys into application memory at session init (with secure purging). For large bulk exports (500 MB), use streaming decryption to avoid memory exhaustion. Monitor HSM latency; alert if key fetch >100 ms.

security_performance.feature: Evidence chain-of-custody hash verification (SHA-256)
security_performance.reasoning: When panel opens a case, system verifies hash chain of all evidence artefacts to detect tampering. Noyce NFR-LAT-006 'case_open' budgets 10 seconds end-to-end. Hash verification for typical 100 MB pack: 50-500 ms (CPU-bound SHA-256 + I/O). This is a high-confidence forensic control; latency cannot be sacrificed. Parallel hash verification across multiple CPU cores is essential. MOR/AAIB incident response depends on provable evidence integrity.
security_performance.recommendation: Use parallel SHA-256 verification (one thread per 10 MB chunk). Cache hash results per artefact ID.
