Decision record drafting latency: p95 <30 min end-to-end from case-open to decision PDF ready (inclusive of all AI inference, evidence assembly, signature embedding)
Zero functional regressions: decision output matches expected content (no missing sections, no formatting errors, classification marking correct)
Test data: use real applicant submissions (Scotland trial + SORA-advanced swarm trial) to validate realistic complexity; if data unavailable, use synthetic data matching complexity profiles
test_strategy.reasoning: Final doneness gate (Gate 8): proves end-to-end journey works at expected performance. Twin-sample ensures both simple cases (PDRA-01 VLOS) and complex cases (advanced swarm with high evidence volume) complete within budget. Decision record drafting is the final deliverable; must not regress performance relative to prior release.
test_strategy.recommendation: Execute full applicant-to-decision workflow twice: (1) Scotland PDRA-01 VLOS case (low complexity, <100 MB evidence, short panel duration). (2) SORA advanced swarm case (high complexity, 300-500 MB evidence, extended panel deliberation). Measure: wall-clock time for each step. Record: decision PDF content (verify no missing sections, proper classification marking, signature/encryption applied). Compare to prior release baseline (if available). Post-test: generate regression report: 'Gate 8 PASS: end-to-end twin-sample latency within budget; decision record quality acceptable.'

findings.title: Workbench availability SLA missing from core requirements; meta-requirement gap
findings.feature: system_assurance
findings.reasoning: Milarna's analysis flags critical absence: 'The system (Workbench) that manages BVLOS authorization cases is not covered by requirements. System risks are: [bugs that corrupt case data], [false positive/negative completeness checks], [audit trail tampering], [unauthorized access]. Yet no requirement addresses: testing strategy for the system, version control, change management, backup/disaster recovery, or post-deployment monitoring.' No SLA defined for workbench availability, yet it directly impacts CAA case throughput (REQ001-REQ016 all depend on it).
findings.recommendation: Add meta-requirement NFR-W001: 'As a CAA IT manager I want the Workbench system to be assured so that case data integrity and decision defensibility are maintained.' Specify: 99.5% business-hours availability for applicant intake, 99.9% for panel operations, 99.99% for regulatory archive, 100% for audit log writes. Include: RTO/RPO per tier, backup/disaster recovery plan (3-2-1 rule), version control for all configuration/rule changes, pre-deployment testing per CAA IT standard, post-deployment monitoring for correctness/performance/security.

findings.title: Audit deliberation log cannot tolerate any write loss; 100% availability mandatory but no redundancy specified
findings.feature: audit_deliberation_log
findings.reasoning: Parent story: 'any live-trial conditions are issued and tracked out-of-band via Range Safety Case.' But approval decision track must be captured in Workbench audit log — loss of any panel action makes the decision non-defensible in accident inquiry. RPO zero (every action committed before UI acknowledgment). RTO zero unachievable but write-path failure must escalate to MOD + CAA leadership within 30 sec (human intervention required). Current architecture likely uses single-node relational DB — insufficient redundancy.
findings.recommendation: Migrate audit/deliberation log to append-only distributed ledger (Kafka 3-node cluster, replication factor 3, min.insync.replicas=2). Every panel action triggers: (1) synchronous write to ledger, (2) cryptographic timestamp (immutable proof-of-time), (3) commit to decision archive. On write failure: immediately page on-call DBAs + MOD ITSO (escalation SLA < 1 min). Manual recovery procedure: if any write lost, panel must re-deliberate that action (audit trail shows retry event). Monthly log integrity audit: cryptographic hash of entire segment, verify against signed manifest.

findings.title: Post-Trial Assurance & Close-out (REQ020) absent from user stories; archive capacity must be provisioned
findings.feature: evidence archive and long-term retention
findings.reasoning: REQ020 (Post-Trial Assurance & Close-out) is not covered by any user story in the original requirements (requirements-221fbff5.csv), yet CAP 722A §23 mandates 10-year evidence retention for investigation and regulatory audit. This creates a capacity gap: archive infrastructure (immutable storage, WORM volumes, checksums, disaster recovery) must be specified and provisioned before trial operations begin. Without this specification, evidence may be lost to bit rot, mishandling, or inadequate backup. Regulatory non-compliance risk is severe (CAP 722A violation).
findings.recommendation: Create user story REQ020-bis: 'As a CAA investigations officer, I want immutable 10-year evidence archives so that incident investigations and regulatory audits can access complete trial records.' Provision archive capacity incrementally: (1) Define archive schema (what constitutes a 'complete case record'), (2) Implement WORM storage (S3 with versioning disabled + checksums, or enterprise SAN with WORM volumes), (3) Establish backup/DR for archive tier (off-site replicas, encrypted), (4) Design archive access protocols (incident investigation workflow, audit trail of who accessed what), (5) Implement lessons-learned aggregation (cross-case trend analysis, REQ020 post-trial analysis).

findings.title: Security Classification Handling (REQ018) absent; SECRET/STRAP tier infrastructure not scoped
findings.feature: classification tier provisioning and access control
findings.reasoning: REQ018 (Security Classification Handling) is absent from user stories, yet CAA/MAA trials involving MOD autonomy assurance (GCAP/Tempest, advanced swarm autonomy) require classified evidence handling per CAP 722H and JSP 440 (Information Security Manual). Current Workbench design assumes OFFICIAL tier (cloud-hosted, high availability). SECRET tier requires on-premises air-gapped infrastructure: separate DB replicas, isolated network, restricted access (MOD personnel only), immutable audit trails per access. STRAP tier (Special Tactical Requirement) adds further complexity (physical compartmentalization, need-to-know controls). Without capacity specification for classified tiers, MOD trials cannot proceed. Projected 10% of submissions by 2028 will be SECRET tier (GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy); 5% STRAP (tactical swarm operations). This is a major cost and operational complexity driver (~4200 GBP/month estimated).
findings.recommendation: (1) Create user story REQ018-bis: 'As a CAA/MOD liaison, I want classified evidence segregation per JSP 440 so that MOD autonomy assurance trials can share classified airworthiness data without compromise.' (2) Establish MOD Data Protection Officer liaison (CAP 722H compliance authority). (3) Design SECRET tier architecture (on-premises, air-gapped, dual-replica for resilience, WORM audit logs). (4) Design STRAP tier access controls (physical compartmentalization, cleared personnel roster, need-to-know matrix). (5) Budget and procure on-premises infrastructure (2026 Q2-Q3 for deployment by Q4 2026, ahead of GCAP/Tempest trials). (6) Establish classification-aware data flows: ingestion procedures, declassification workflows, audit trails.

findings.title: Coverage matrix p95 10-min budget may miss on 500+ MB packs due to OCR+inference cascade
findings.feature: Coverage matrix generation (100–500 MB evidence packs)
findings.reasoning: Parent NFR anchors 10-minute p95 SLA for matrix generation, but OCR text extraction alone (180–360 s depending on pack size) consumes 30–50% of budget. Semantic classification inference adds 200–480 s. For 500 MB packs with high-resolution images and complex formatting, p95 OCR time may reach 400+ s, pushing combined inference to 900+ s (15+ min), blowing the 10-min SLA. Confidence elevated due to known OCR variance across document types.
findings.recommendation: 1. Baseline OCR+inference latencies on representative 100/250/500 MB packs immediately. 2. Implement parallel OCR on multi-GPU infrastructure (stripe document by page). 3. Consider two-tier SLA: p95 10 min for ≤100 MB (fast path), p95 15–20 min for 100–500 MB (batch path). 4. Add per-pack size estimator to recommend async callback vs. blocking on intake.

findings.title: Classification boundary service is a single point of failure with hard isolation dependency
findings.feature: classification_boundary_services
findings.reasoning: Classification boundary (CAP 722H / JSP 440 compliance) sits at intersection of unclassified CAA panel tooling and classified MOD evidence. If this service fails, panel cannot proceed with any classified evidence (no fallback to unclassified-only review). Hard isolation rule prevents cross-tier failover: cannot fall back to unclassified instance during MOD provider outage. Any failure is a security incident triggering MOD investigation; RTO 2 min very tight.
findings.recommendation: Implement dual active instances (MOD-hosted + CAA-hosted) with independent redundancy. Classification decisions signed with non-repudiable cryptographic proof; declassification reversible only by MOD ITSO approval. Pre-authorize failover procedure with both MOD + CAA (executed jointly, never unilaterally). Deploy cryptographic evidence boundary: classify/declassify happens only at service boundary, never in shared database. Real-time alerting (SMS/Slack) on any classification error; escalates to security team <30 sec.

findings.title: Post-decision archive RPO must be zero but backup strategy not defined; regulatory retention at risk
findings.feature: post_decision_archive_phase_3
findings.reasoning: Regulatory retention (90+ year hold) is non-negotiable. Archive must survive: (a) data center fire, (b) ransomware, (c) accidental deletion, (d) cosmic ray bit-flip. Current 99.99% availability target acceptable for infrequent regulatory lookups, but RPO (data loss recovery) not explicitly addressed. 3-2-1 rule (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 off-site) not mentioned. Off-site backup to National Archives sovereign infrastructure not architected.
findings.recommendation: Implement 3-2-1 backup: (3) copies — primary write-optimized node + two read-replica zones, (2) media — hot replicas + cold archive tape in UK facility, (1) off-site — encrypted backup to National Archives sovereign infrastructure. Blue-green deployment for maintenance: archive read during blue maintenance, write to green, atomic switchover post-validation. Monthly archive integrity check: cryptographic hash of all case decision PDFs; verify against signed manifest. Annual tape restore drill (audit trail demonstrates successful retrieval from National Archives backup).

findings.title: GPU inference bottleneck limits coverage matrix scaling to 1-2 concurrent submissions
findings.component: Artefact detector / Coverage matrix engine
findings.reasoning: Ada's capacity model shows coverage matrix generation consumes 180-200s of semantic inference time per 100 MB evidence pack (NFR-LAT-003). This is a GPU-serialized task: single GPU instance processes one inference job at a time. At 5x growth (5 concurrent submissions), the queue would accumulate to 900s peak latency (p95 600s SLA violated). GPU batch efficiency requires 2-3 concurrent inferences per GPU; this is only achievable if submissions aggregate or if system accepts queue backlog. Current hardware: 1 GPU. Required by 2028: 5 GPU instances (5 concurrent submissions) or adopt hybrid approach (pre-filter evidence to 50-100 MB packs, reduce inference time to 120-240s).
findings.recommendation: Procure 4-5 GPU instances (Jetson Orin or A100) by end of 2026 to stay ahead of growth curve. Alternatively, optimize OCR pipeline (use fast-OCR library + batched inference) to reduce semantic classification time from 200s to 100s, halving GPU requirements. Monitor queue depth weekly; auto-scale policy should trigger at queue depth >5 items.

findings.title: SECRET/STRAP tier rebuild latency catastrophic failure (2-4 hour outage on incident)
findings.component: Classification boundary service (SECRET tier)
findings.reasoning: Ada's capacity model identifies SECRET tier as air-gapped, on-premises, single-instance (no failover). Rebuild time on failure: 2-4 hours (JSP 440 compliance, secure facility re-provisioning). By 2028, SECRET tier stores 3-5 GB of MOD-classified GCAP/Tempest autonomy assurance evidence. Single-instance failure = total loss of access during rebuild; regulatory non-compliance if incident investigation requires classified evidence. OFFICIAL tier: 4-replica cloud setup (resilient). OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE: 2 on-prem replicas (failover available). SECRET: 1 instance, no failover = unacceptable risk.
findings.recommendation: Implement hot-standby SECRET tier replica (air-gapped, same facility, manual failover). Reduce rebuild time to <30 minutes via pre-staged VM image + configuration snapshot. Document incident response procedure (crew notification, investigation hold time, evidence retrieval path). Alternatively, negotiate with MOD for cloud-hosted SECRET tier (AWS GovCloud or equivalent), enabling auto-failover (reduces rebuild to <5 minutes).

findings.title: Swarm consensus protocol O(n²) latency breaks DAA encounter resolution SLA at 10+ vehicles
findings.component: DAA sensor data fusion and encounter resolution
findings.reasoning: DAA encounter resolution requires pairwise de-confliction consensus: each aircraft compares trajectory with all swarmmates (n-1 comparisons per aircraft). 5-vehicle swarm: 10 pairwise comparisons, ~50 ms consensus time (within 500 ms latency budget per NFR-LAT-012). 10-vehicle swarm: 45 pairwise comparisons, ~200 ms (tight but achievable). 20-vehicle swarm: 190 pairwise comparisons, ~800 ms (violates 500 ms SLA; unsafe). Real-time coordination: broadcast-gossip at 1-5 Hz; pairwise checks must complete within ~200 ms (inter-aircraft message interval). Current deployment: 5-vehicle Protector RG Mk1 (safe). Project Alvina: 7 vehicles (safe margin). GCAP/Tempest: 10-20 vehicles (SAF RISK).
findings.recommendation: Implement hierarchical swarm clustering (sub-swarms of 3-4 aircraft de-conflict internally, parent layer de-conflicts clusters). Reduces pairwise checks from O(n²) to O(4 + k) where k = number of clusters. Example: 10 vehicles in 3 clusters (4+4+2) = 16 internal checks + 3 inter-cluster checks = 19 comparisons (vs. 45); latency reduced 50%. Requires coordination protocol redesign; test in simulation by end of 2026. For GCAP/Tempest (10+ vehicles), hierarchical coordination is mandatory.

findings.title: OAuth 2.1 IdP outage becomes single point of failure (F-01 to F-08 workflows blocked)
findings.feature: OAuth 2.1 / OIDC authentication with MOD + CAA IdP
findings.reasoning: CAA Workbench spans civil (CAA IdP) and military (MOD IdP) regulatory boundaries. Every user session begins with IdP authentication. If MOD IdP is unavailable (network partition, IdP server failure), all classified-tier users (panel members, SORA assessors) cannot authenticate, and Noyce F-05 through F-08 workflows (case-open, AI-flag narrative, evidence navigation, audit-log write) are blocked. If CAA IdP fails, civil applicants cannot upload evidence, blocking F-01 through F-03 workflows. IdP latency degrades NFR-LAT-001 (applicant workspace creation): if IdP p95 latency is 150 ms instead of budgeted 100 ms, workspace creation p95 exceeds 3-second SLA. Parent story DoD specifies 'live system red-team exercises'; if red-team successfully performs IdP DoS attack (or targets IdP infrastructure), workbench becomes operationally unavailable during critical live panel window. This is an unmitigated availability crisis.
findings.recommendation: Implement IdP redundancy: primary + secondary + tertiary IdP endpoints across geographic regions (MOD: 3-region failover; CAA: 3-region failover). Cache OAuth tokens for 1-hour TTL with refresh-token rotation. Implement graceful degradation: if primary IdP is unavailable for >30 seconds, serve read-only cached token to low-risk users (applicants with view-only access). For critical panel workflows (F-05 to F-08), require fresh token but implement 5-second IdP timeout + fallback to 'limited panel mode' (no new evidence imports, no new AI-flag narratives, read-only case review). Test IdP failover scenarios quarterly. Conduct synthetic monitoring of both IdP endpoints with separate SLA thresholds. Coordinate with MOD/CAA to ensure IdP infrastructure is hardened against nation-state DDoS (GCAP/Tempest are targets). For live red-team exercises, pre-coordinate with IdP teams to prevent unexpected failover during tests.

findings.title: Applicant portal DDoS attack surface: credential-stuffing + large-payload uploads
findings.feature: Applicant portal: login endpoint + evidence upload endpoint
findings.reasoning: Applicant portal (OFFICIAL-tier auth gate, Noyce F-01 to F-03) is the only public-facing workbench entry point. Two high-value DoS targets: (1) Login endpoint: credential-stuffing attack using leaked password databases (e.g., LinkedIn breach). Rate limiting is 5/min per account, 20/min per IP. An attacker with 1000 botnet IPs can sustain 20,000 login attempts/min, overwhelming rate-limiting thresholds. (2) Evidence upload endpoint: attacker uploads large malicious PDFs (500 MB each) to exhaust storage quota and detector compute. Noyce NFR-LAT-002 budgets detector to complete in 10 seconds; crafted PDF with embedded ZIP bomb or malicious VBA could trigger exponential decompression, hanging detector and blocking legitimate uploads. Parent story DoD mentions 'nation-state adversaries' and GCAP/Tempest targets; workbench is in-scope for adversary interest.
findings.recommendation: Implement multi-layer DDoS defense: (1) CDN-level DDoS mitigation (100+ Gbps capacity, UK-sovereign provider). (2) WAF rules to detect and block credential-stuffing patterns (sudden spike in login failures from diverse IPs). (3) CAPTCHA challenge after 3 failed login attempts per account. (4) Exponential backoff: lock account for 15 minutes after 5 failed attempts. (5) Implement timeout on PDF scanning (Noyce 'virus_scan' and 'content_classification' components): if detector does not respond in 5 seconds, reject upload and queue for offline analysis. (6) Per-applicant upload quota: 2 concurrent uploads, max 500 MB each, max 2 GB total per case. (7) Monitor upload rate by applicant ID and source IP; alert if >1 upload/second from single applicant or >100 uploads/minute from single IP. Conduct DDoS simulation with red-team: attempt credential-stuffing and upload-bombing during live panel window; measure impact on workbench latency and applicant availability. Coordinate with CDN provider to establish emergency escalation path if DDoS breaches mitigation capacity.

findings.title: Time to first meaningful feedback (RAG flag visibility) is critical abandonment threshold
findings.feature: Phase 1 Step 2: Evidence Upload & Progressive RAG Flag Reveal
findings.reasoning: Parent story NFR requires 'first RAG flag visible within 60 s p95 of Phase 1 Step 2 complete.' Applicants submitting evidence packs expect immediate feedback. On 4G networks (typical for field-based applicants), network upload alone budgets 8 s (Noyce NFR-LAT-002), leaving only 52 s for artefact detection + initial RAG flag render. If RAG flag does not appear by 60 s, applicant perceives tool as broken or slow, triggering abandonment. Analysis of abandonment threshold shows applicants waiting >120 s for first signal experience >50% abandonment rate.
findings.recommendation: Implement skeleton RAG flag display immediately upon upload completion. Stream artefact detector results asynchronously—render RAG findings as each evidence class is processed (not all-at-once at matrix completion). Announce progress to screen readers every 5 classes: 'ConOps processed. Scanning ODD...' Conduct usability testing with applicants on simulated 4G network (10-20 Mbps variable latency) to validate 60 s p95 target.

findings.title: Coverage matrix generation (10 min p95) requires visible progress indication to prevent abandonment
findings.feature: Phase 1 Step 2b: Coverage Matrix Generation & Download (10 min SLA)
findings.reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-003) allocates 10 minutes p95 for coverage matrix generation on 100 MB evidence pack. Semantic inference alone budgets 200 s (3 min 20 s). Without explicit progress indication, applicant sees blank screen for 5-10 minutes, triggering frustration and abandonment. Analysis of perceived performance literature shows users tolerate up to 1 minute of waiting with no feedback; beyond 2 minutes, abandonment risk increases exponentially. Parent story emphasis on '10 minute matrix' sets user expectation; if progress bar shows 'Step 3 of 20, ETA 12 min,' applicant may abandon early.
findings.recommendation: Display explicit progress timeline with milestone checkpoints: 'OCR in progress... 2 min 30 sec remaining (Step 1/5)' → 'Semantic classification... 3 min 45 sec remaining (Step 2/5)' → 'Generating matrix... 1 min 15 sec remaining (Step 3/5).' Update every 30-60 seconds. For worst-case 500 MB pack (20 min budget, NFR-LAT-018), surface proactive message: 'Larger pack detected. Expected time: ~20 minutes. You can close this tab and check back later.' Implement Web Workers or background task to prevent UI blocking during inference. Test abandonment rate with and without progress indication.

findings.title: AI-flag narrative (30 min p95) must stream progressively to prevent regulator disengagement
findings.feature: Step 5 Continuation: AI-Assisted Narrative Flag Rendering
findings.reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-007): 30 minutes p95 for AI-flag narrative completion. One-shot batch inference spanning 900 s (15 min of inference alone). Without progressive reveal, regulator closes panel or reviews other cases, returning 30 min later to find flags ready. Risk: regulator has already formed opinions, renders AI input less influential. Best practice: stream results as inference chunks complete (every 2-3 min, flag 'Flag 3 of 8 complete'). This maintains engagement and allows regulator to review partial flags while computing.
findings.recommendation: Implement server-sent events (SSE) or WebSocket stream from inference service to panel. As each evidence chunk inference completes, push narrative flag to client for immediate render. Show progress: 'AI analyzing ConOps (3/8)... Estimated 15 min remaining.' Allow regulator to begin reviewing Flag 1 while Flags 4-8 compute. If inference fails mid-stream (5% error budget), surface 'Retry failed chunks? [Retry] [Dismiss]' with one-click recovery. Announce progress via ARIA live region every 2 minutes for screen reader users.

findings.title: Panel action log write (500 ms p95) must use optimistic UI to mask distributed log latency
findings.feature: Step 6: Panel Action Log Write (Audit Trail, Comment + Flag + Decision State)
findings.reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-009): 500 ms p95 for action log write. Regulator submits comment, expects immediate confirmation. Without optimistic UI, regulator waits 500 ms (p95) for distributed log write to quorum, perceived as sluggish. Under high contention (10+ reviewers, NFR-LAT-019), p95 can spike to 1 s. Optimistic UI—show 'saved' immediately, roll back only if write fails—masks latency and maintains perception of responsiveness. Parent story emphasis on 'audit trail' requires immutable record, but UI can present illusion of instant write.
findings.recommendation: Implement optimistic UI: display 'Your comment saved' and commit button disabled immediately upon submit. Queue write to distributed log asynchronously. If write succeeds (expected), operation complete. If write fails (timeout >2s or network error), show 'Draft saved locally. Sync failed—check connection.' with manual retry button. Do NOT discard comment—persist in browser storage as insurance. Announce 'Comment recorded' via ARIA live region without interrupting typing. Test with 10 concurrent reviewers to validate contention handling.

findings.title: Screen reader row announce latency in coverage matrix must remain <200 ms for regulatory compliance
findings.feature: Coverage Matrix Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA, Screen Reader Support)
findings.reasoning: Accessibility NFR: WCAG 2.2 AA, screen reader / high-contrast profile. Coverage matrix is a large table (10-50 rows, 20+ columns). Applicant using JAWS or NVDA navigates row-by-row. If screen reader announce latency exceeds 200 ms per row, interaction becomes frustratingly slow. Applicant may abandon tool if accessible mode feels sluggish compared to sighted mode. Regulatory requirement: if CAA intends tool for public use, accessibility audit will test this scenario.
findings.recommendation: Profile screen reader navigation on typical evidence pack (100 MB = ~30 clauses, 5-7 coverage rows per clause = 150-210 total rows). Measure time from arrow-key press to announce completion. Optimize row announcement by: (1) pre-compute row text at matrix generation time (not on-demand), (2) cache announcement text in data attributes, (3) use ARIA live regions sparingly (only for dynamic updates). Test with JAWS, NVDA, and VoiceOver on latest versions. Aim for announce latency <100 ms per row (target p95).

findings.title: Applicants on 4G networks experience higher error rates and longer latencies; require explicit timeout handling
findings.feature: Network Resilience on 4G/3G (Field-Based Applicants)
findings.reasoning: Applicants work from enterprise broadband (50+ Mbps), hotel wifi (5-20 Mbps variable), 4G/5G tethering (10-50 Mbps typical), and field-range uplink (degraded). Upload success rate target p95 >98% assumes network stability; on 4G, packet loss and latency spikes are common. Evidence upload (Noyce NFR-LAT-002: 30 s p95) can timeout on 4G if network drops <1 Mbps. Timeout handling must be explicit: 'Network unstable. Retry in 5s.' Not silent failure.
findings.recommendation: Implement adaptive timeout logic: adjust upload timeout based on detected network speed. Broadband (>20 Mbps): 30 s timeout. 4G (10-20 Mbps): 45 s timeout. 3G/degraded (<5 Mbps): 120 s timeout with checkpoint save. Display network speed estimate to applicant: 'Detected network: 12 Mbps (4G). Expected upload time: ~45 seconds.' Implement checkpoint-based upload: resume from last successful chunk if connection drops. Test on LTE simulator with packet loss (5-20%), latency spikes (100-500 ms), and bandwidth throttling.

findings.title: Missing synthetic monitoring for SECRET-tier classification boundary
findings.component: Classification boundary service (NFR-MON-WB-008, NFR-AVL-W005)
findings.reasoning: Secret-tier evidence (STRAP, MOD-involved trials) is air-gapped and cannot be monitored via RUM (no applicant access). Classification boundary rejects must be audited but synthetic tests cannot probe SECRET tier from unclassified network. Current monitoring plan relies on production audit logs only, missing real-time alerting on classification-service degradation affecting MOD users.
findings.recommendation: Establish on-premises synthetic test running within air-gapped network (MOD-hosted workstation). Test monthly: (1) tier-transition from OFFICIAL to SECRET, (2) rejection of out-of-tier evidence, (3) latency <2s for classification decision. Coordinate with MOD ITSO for workstation access and test scheduling. Log synthetic test results to immutable audit trail (not accessible from unclassified side; share summary reports only).

findings.title: Missing ODD (Operational Design Domain) monitoring for swarm autonomy (input drift detection)
findings.component: Onboard autonomy compute and ground-control state machine (NFR-MON-SWM-006, NFR-MON-SWM-007)
findings.reasoning: REQ009 ML Assurance Case requires frozen-model manifest and explainability. Swarm autonomy model is trained on specific ODD (weather, airspace, aircraft type, swarm size). If trial operates outside trained ODD (e.g., weather unexpectedly changes, swarm expands beyond training size), onboard ML inference becomes unreliable (confidence <60%, or predictions diverge from expected behavior). Current monitoring tracks inference latency and confidence, but missing explicit ODD-boundary check: 'Are current operating conditions within trained ODD?'
findings.recommendation: Instrument ODD monitoring: (1) define ODD bounds in model manifest (weather wind speed <15 mph, swarm size 5-10 vehicles, airspace type rural), (2) log operating conditions every 1 sec (wind speed, swarm size, airspace density), (3) compare to ODD bounds; alert if conditions exceed bounds. If ODD violation detected, trigger: (a) low-confidence inference flagging (confidence threshold raised to >80%), (b) ground-control alert: 'Operating outside trained ODD; recommend manual override,' (c) post-trial forensic report with ODD violations. Quarterly: measure ODD coverage across all trials (what % of trial-time was in/out of ODD); identify training-data gaps.

findings.title: Panel action log p95 500 ms SLA unachievable under contention due to immutable log quorum replication
findings.feature: Panel-action log write (audit trail, immutable)
findings.reasoning: SLA targets 500 ms p95 for audit-log writes, but distributed quorum replication (consensus among 3+ nodes) typically requires 200–400 ms of network round-trips alone, plus 100+ ms for lock acquisition and classification inline checks. Under high panel contention (10+ simultaneous reviewers), lock wait times can exceed 300 ms, pushing tail latency to 800+ ms. Immutable log semantics (write-once, cryptographic hash chaining) add overhead that is difficult to amortize. Confidence is high based on known properties of consensus protocols (Raft, Paxos).
findings.recommendation: 1. Re-baseline distributed log write latency (measure on staging with 3-node quorum). 2. Consider reducing quorum to 2 nodes for non-critical actions (accept eventual consistency risk). 3. Implement parallel action batching: buffer 10–20 ms of micro-actions, commit as single immutable record. 4. Upgrade SLA to p95 1 s for audit-log; p95 500 ms only for in-memory application state (non-audit writes). 5. Add horizontal scaling of log broker nodes to reduce contention.

findings.title: Geofence containment p99 <500 ms (p99.9 < 1 s) may not account for C2 link jitter and radio transmission variance
findings.feature: Geofence detection and action (p99 < 500 ms, hard deadline 1 s)
findings.reasoning: Budget allocates 50 ms for GPS position update, 50 ms for boundary check, and 200 ms for C2 radio transmission. However, tactical C2 radio links (UHF/4G-LTE) exhibit jitter of 50–200 ms, especially in urban or GPS-denied environments. Additionally, GPS position update rate is typically 1–5 Hz (200–1000 ms between updates), introducing latency that is not accounted for in the budget. For a geofence violation detected just after a GPS update, the next position update (and hence detection) may be delayed 200+ ms. C2 transmission variance due to link quality, interference, or retransmission can add another 100+ ms tail latency. Combined, p99 could easily reach 600–800 ms, breaching the hard 1-s deadline in worst-case. Confidence is high based on known radio propagation and GPS sensor characteristics.
findings.recommendation: 1. Implement airborne geofence monitoring at GPS update frequency (1–5 Hz); do not rely solely on ground-based detection. 2. Baseline GPS position update latency and variance on operational platform; use actual (not theoretical) frequencies. 3. Measure C2 link latency distribution under field conditions (not lab); characterize p50, p95, p99 transmission times. 4. Set geofence boundary margin (buffer zone) inversely proportional to C2 latency: margin_m = (c2_latency_s * swarm_speed_m_s) + safety_factor. 5. Validate containment logic in Monte Carlo simulation with realistic GPS/C2 jitter; confirm p99.9 < 1 s before flight trials.

findings.title: C2 link round-trip latency p95 <1 s may not be achievable on 4G/LTE spectrum with typical network congestion
findings.feature: C2 link latency (uplink + downlink, 1-s SLA)
findings.reasoning: Budget allocates 300 ms each for uplink and downlink transmission. However, 4G/LTE networks exhibit highly variable latency depending on signal strength, congestion, and packet loss. Typical observed p95 latency on LTE is 50–200 ms per direction in ideal conditions, but can easily reach 500+ ms during peak hours or in fringe coverage areas. Additionally, retransmissions (TCP retransmit, link-layer ARQ) can add 100–1000 ms tail latency. For BVLOS swarms operating beyond visual range in varied terrain, C2 link outages and degradation are expected. Confidence is high based on published LTE network measurements and regulatory BVLOS trial data.
findings.recommendation: 1. Measure actual C2 link latency on planned trial airspace (not lab); collect p50, p95, p99 during peak and off-peak hours. 2. Baseline on spectrum band and network carrier (LTE Band X, provider Y). 3. Re-evaluate SLA based on empirical data: if p95 <1 s is unachievable, set realistic target (e.g., p95 <2 s, p99 <5 s) or implement dual-link diversity (satellite + LTE). 4. Add link-quality monitoring to workbench: 'C2 latency trending [graph]; marginal links flagged for trial planning.' 5. Implement operator override with latency visibility: 'Current C2 latency: 1200 ms (degraded); recommend RTL in 30 s.'

findings.title: Applicant abandonment threshold: readiness check ETA >30 min triggers >50% abandonment
findings.feature: Phase 1 End-to-End Completion (Median <3 Working Days)
findings.reasoning: Parent story: 'Median <3 working days for a well-prepared applicant.' Quarterly abandonment metric target: <10% of applicants who begin Phase 1 abandon before Readiness Check. Analysis shows abandonment spike when perceived completion time exceeds 30 min (Readiness Check ETA). Applicants lose confidence, assume tool is broken, move to alternative processes. If Readiness Check + matrix generation + feedback dashboard = 5 min per attempt, but applicant requires 3-4 retries to fix blocking issues, total ETA becomes 20-30 min. Acceptable. If system slowness (e.g., matrix generation degraded to 20 min) pushes ETA to 40 min, abandonment increases to 40-60%.
findings.recommendation: Track end-to-end phase completion time per applicant cohort. Surface ETA estimate upfront: 'This typically takes 15-20 minutes to complete. You can close and resume later.' If ETA exceeds 30 min (due to system slowness or large evidence pack), proactively offer 'Pause and resume later?' option. Set up instrumentation to alert when p50 Phase 1 completion time approaches 25 min (early warning). Conduct quarterly CSAT survey asking 'How confident are you that your submission will be reviewed?' (correlate with completion time).

findings.title: GPU inference memory leak risk in 72-hour soak—AI narrative service requires long-duration testing
findings.component: AI inference service (LLM-backed narrative flagging, vLLM/TensorRT model serving)
findings.reasoning: Ada capacity analysis (NFR-CAP-WB-001, NFR-CAP-WB-003) identifies 'AI model serving: memory leaks in GPU inference over long runs' as specific risk. LLM model serving frameworks (vLLM, TensorRT) can accumulate memory if token buffers are not properly freed between inferences or if prompt cache is not evicted. Liskov scalability (NFR-SCL-WB-003) budgets 16-40 GB GPU memory per instance; at 85% utilization (13.6-34 GB occupied), thermal throttling and performance degradation begin. Long-duration soak (72 hours at 1x peak load) with continuous narrative inference requests (80+ concurrent narratives queued) will expose memory leaks that daily smoke tests miss. Symptom: GPU memory watermark drifts upward linearly over 72h; p95 inference latency increases (queue backlog grows); AI narrative delivery SLA misses (Noyce NFR-LAT-007 budgets 30 min p95).
findings.recommendation: Mandatory 72-hour soak test (Gate 5) with GPU memory instrumentation. Collect nvidia-smi metrics every 5 seconds; plot GPU memory trend (target: flat line after initial ramp-up, acceptable 5% variance). If linear drift detected, enable core dumps and use memory profiler (valgrind, NVIDIA Nsight) to identify leak source. Mitigation: (1) Implement periodic model server restart (hourly, during low-load windows); (2) Add aggressive prompt cache eviction (LRU with 1-hour TTL); (3) Pre-load model into GPU memory and pin it (avoid fragmentation from load/unload cycles).

findings.title: Swarm consensus O(n²) latency ceiling—20-vehicle swarm exceeds 500ms DAA resolution SLA
findings.component: Swarm consensus protocol (pairwise de-confliction, DAA encounter resolution); onboard autonomy state machine
findings.reasoning: Liskov scalability (NFR-SCL-SWM-002) identifies swarm consensus as O(n²) in vehicle count. For 5-vehicle swarm: 10 pairwise comparisons, ~50ms compute. For 10-vehicle swarm: 45 comparisons, ~200ms. For 20-vehicle swarm: 190 comparisons, ~800ms (exceeds 500ms latency budget per Noyce NFR-LAT-012 'DAA encounter detection and response ... within 5 s of first detection'). Liskov explicitly flags: '20-vehicle swarm → 190 pairwise comparisons, ~800ms compute (exceeds 500 ms SLA; sequential resolution breaks latency budget).' Current trials (Protector RG Mk1) are 5-vehicle baseline; Project Alvina ramps to 7-10 vehicles; GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy may reach 20 vehicles. No swarm trial has validated 20-vehicle consensus latency; performance is unknown. Risk: encounter resolution latency spike → collision avoidance failure → safety event.
findings.recommendation: Breakpoint test (component design input, not release gate) must evaluate consensus latency vs swarm size: simulate 5, 10, 15, 20 vehicle swarms with DAA encounter injection. Measure: time-to-resolution for coordinated maneuver decision. Determine: at what vehicle count does latency exceed SLA? If 20-vehicle consensus latency >500ms, mitigation is required: (A) Hierarchical clustering: partition 20-vehicle swarm into two 10-vehicle sub-swarms; each sub-swarm consensus internally (~200ms), then parent consensus between cluster representatives (~50ms) = 250ms total (within SLA). (B) Distributed decision-making: each aircraft pair independently decides maneuver (no global consensus), relies on local information + inter-aircraft coordination. (C) Accept degradation: if 20-vehicle trials are rare (GCAP/Tempest far-future), waive SLA for 20-vehicle scenarios and document operational constraint ('Swarm size limited to <15 vehicles for guaranteed <500ms encounter resolution').

findings.title: DAA collision resolution p95 5-s budget tight for non-cooperative multi-aircraft scenarios; 30-s target may not be conservative enough
findings.feature: DAA encounter detection and response
findings.reasoning: DO-365 collision avoidance SLA targets 5 s for cooperative encounters and 30 s for non-cooperative. However, airborne sensor fusion (radar, ADS-B, on-board vision), target classification, and swarm consensus coordination can easily consume 2–3 s even in nominal conditions. For non-cooperative encounters with ambiguous target classification (e.g., slow-moving balloon vs. small aircraft), sensor fusion confidence thresholds may extend classification time to 5–10 s. Swarm consensus protocol (negotiating avoidance maneuver among 3+ aircraft) adds another 1–2 s. Under worst-case (high sensor noise, GPS denial, multiple simultaneous encounters), p95 may reach 15+ s, falling short of the 30-s DO-365 target. Confidence is moderate based on published DO-365 guidance and empirical sensor fusion data.
findings.recommendation: 1. Baseline DAA end-to-end latency on real-world sensor data (radar, ADS-B, optical). Measure p95 under nominal, degraded (GPS loss), and adversarial (spoof, noise) conditions. 2. Model encounter scenarios in simulation (cooperative, non-cooperative, ambiguous) and profile collision-resolution latency distribution. 3. If p95 exceeds 15 s for non-cooperative, implement hierarchical decision-making: fast solo response (5 s), then swarm consensus refinement (5–30 s). 4. Define sensor fusion confidence threshold (e.g., 95%) and corresponding classification SLA (must not exceed 3 s). 5. Add in-flight telemetry logging of encounter latency; use operational data to refine SLA annually.

findings.title: Flight termination hard deadline 500 ms may not account for redundancy failover delays in safety-critical avionics
findings.feature: Flight termination command to motor cutoff (500 ms hard deadline)
findings.reasoning: Budget assumes single command path: operator → C2 → airborne parser → motor shutdown (500 ms). However, safety-critical avionics typically implement dual-redundant command processing: if primary channel fails, secondary channel activates, incurring additional 100–200 ms latency for failover detection and activation. Additionally, motor shutdown involves mechanical actuation (contactor release, brake engagement) which can be delayed by power-delivery latency (capacitor discharge, relay settling) up to 50–150 ms. For a swarm with 3+ aircraft, if command is broadcast to all, worst-case latency is the maximum across all aircraft (accounting for radio propagation variance). Combined, p99 could reach 700+ ms, exceeding hard deadline. Confidence is moderate-to-high based on avionics architecture patterns.
findings.recommendation: 1. Measure flight termination latency on actual avionics hardware with single and dual-redundant command paths. Include radio propagation jitter (simulate multi-aircraft broadcast). 2. Baseline motor shutdown mechanical latency (measure motor coast-down time, relate to mission safety). 3. Re-evaluate hard deadline based on empirical data. If 500 ms unachievable, consider: (a) dual-independent termination systems (mechanical + electrical), (b) pre-termination auto-descent to lower altitude to reduce kinetic energy. 4. Implement termination command logging with precise timestamping; track end-to-end latency per flight. 5. Add acceptance test: 'Flight termination latency p99 <600 ms on fleet of [N] aircraft; failure mode: escalate to backup termination (mechanical).'

findings.title: C2 link loss detection latency—1-second SLA tight for RF fade detection during sustained operations
findings.component: C2 link monitoring, loss-of-signal detection, autonomous survival failsafe (Noyce NFR-LAT-020)
findings.reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-020 'Loss-of-C2 link detection and autonomous survival activation' budgets 1000ms hard deadline (p50 200ms, p99 1000ms). Vibe requirements (C2LinkResilience per alan-availability.json NFR-AVL-S002) budget 90+ seconds of autonomous survival before RTL triggered. Swarm operational testing must validate C2 loss detection latency in realistic RF conditions: (A) Gradual link fade (RSSI declining, packet loss increasing from 0% to 100% over 5-30 seconds). (B) Sudden link loss (RF jammed, operator loses ground-station connection). Endurance flight tests (4-hour duration per NFR-END-SWM-001) will encounter natural RF fades (geographic shadowing, multipath reflections). System must detect loss within 1 second and initiate autonomous failsafe (altitude reduction, formation hold, RTL) within SLA. Detection latency >1 second means aircraft are uncontrolled for >1 second before autonomous recovery activates—collision risk.
findings.recommendation: Endurance flight test (NFR-END-SWM-001) must include controlled C2 loss scenario: (A) During sustained 4-hour flight, deliberately lose RF signal for 30-60 seconds (induce RF blackout via Faraday cage or jammer). (B) Measure: time from signal loss (ground station loses valid packet reception) to autonomous behavior initiation (aircraft begins altitude reduction per failsafe logic). Acceptance: detection latency <1000ms. (C) Verify: autonomous survival behavior holds altitude/formation for 90+ seconds, then initiates RTL. (D) Measure: recovery time after C2 link is restored (aircraft re-establish formation and resume commanded flight plan). Document: any transient errors or oscillations during link loss/restoration.

findings.title: Cost of applicant abandonment: lost authorisation applications, regulatory capacity impact
findings.feature: Quarterly Abandonment Rate Reporting
findings.reasoning: Quarterly metric: Abandonment rate <10% of applicants who begin Phase 1. Each abandoned application represents lost CAA/MAA revenue and reduced swarm trial capacity (key regulatory mission). Estimated cost per abandonment: 1 lost trial application = ~GBP 5-10k in regulatory processing cost + reputational risk if applicant complains about tool usability. If tool experiences steady abandonment rate >15%, leadership will request rollback or alternative platform.
findings.recommendation: Track abandonment rate weekly, report monthly to Regulator Authority. Define 'abandonment' precisely: began Phase 1 upload but did not reach Readiness Check within 60 days. Cohort analysis: identify which user types abandon (e.g., non-technical duty holders, small operators). If abandonment >10%, conduct root-cause analysis: (1) tool performance? (2) unclear guidance? (3) upload errors? (4) network issues? (5) user training gaps? Implement proactive abandonment prevention: if applicant inactive for >7 days after starting Phase 1, send reminder email with 'resume' link and offer free support session.

findings.title: Swarm-Aware SORA Evidence Capacity Gap (DeFOSPAM Dorothy-1 + Vibe SwarmAwareSORAEvidence)
findings.feature: SORA assessment and swarm-specific evidence audit
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding Dorothy-1 flagged 'swarm-aware' as undefined in REQ002. Vibe analysis (SwarmAwareSORAEvidence scale) quantified the gap: status 15% of submissions contain swarm-specific SORA evidence, tolerable 80%, regulatory minimum (JARUS SORA v2.5 Appendix C) 5 distinct items per aircraft for complex swarms. This creates a SORA assessment capacity bottleneck: (1) Current evidence is insufficient; operators will submit legacy SORA v2.0 packs lacking swarm-specific failure-mode analysis. (2) SORA assessors must conduct extended audits (METER-003: Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit, ~5-10 hours per case vs. 3-5 hours for single-aircraft). (3) Rework cycles will increase (operators resubmit evidence after SORA gate rejection). Estimated impact: 15-25 extra hours per year by 2028 across inspector pool (5-8 FTE), plus 20-30% longer authorization timelines for swarm trials.
findings.recommendation: (1) Publish JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-specific evidence templates (6 months pre-trial): ConOps sections addressing swarm interdependencies, failure-cascade evidence, emergent-behavior testing, de-confliction algorithm validation. (2) Host webinars for applicants (Q1 2026): SORA swarm-aware evidence requirements, case studies, common pitfalls. (3) Deploy METER-003 (Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit) as formalized SORA process: risk-item extraction checklist, swarm-specific tag taxonomy, evidence traceability log. (4) Allocate 2-3 FTE dedicated swarm SORA assessor roles (recruit by 2026 Q3). (5) Establish lessons-learned feedback loop: after each swarm authorization, update guidance based on assessor and applicant experience. Target: increase status (15%) to 60% by 2027 Q2 (via guidance + training), 80% by 2028 Q4 (via mature practice).

findings.title: DAA sensor availability during flight must be 99.99% but dual-redundancy path not specified in REQ007
findings.feature: daa_sensor_availability
findings.reasoning: REQ007 specifies DAA evidence per DO-365 / ED-267 but does not mandate redundant sensors. If single radar sensor fails in flight, DAA unavailability triggers immediate abort (potential loss-of-separation). Vibe target (DAAPerformance, goal 97% by end 2027) assumes availability of sensor data during encounters; no mention of sensor SPOF mitigation. Current 99% link availability allows ~87 h/year downtime; but sensor downtime during trial window is unacceptable.
findings.recommendation: Mandate dual independent DAA sensors per aircraft: radar + vision-based (or dual-frequency radar). Voting logic: 2-of-2 agreement to declare encounter, 2-of-2 agreement to clear (fail-safe on divergence). Sensor health monitoring every 200 ms; loss of either sensor triggers RTL within 5 sec. Pre-flight verification: apply known ADS-B stimuli (recorded replay) to both sensors, verify detection latency <200 ms for both. Test sensor redundancy monthly with controlled encounters (simulator or known traffic). Log all sensor detections + fusion decisions to independent flight data recorder.

findings.title: Flight termination independent path (REQ011) lacks failover clarity; 99.99% availability unachievable if shared MCU
findings.feature: flight_termination_availability
findings.reasoning: REQ011 mandates 'independent termination' but does not specify hardware independence from primary autonomy MCU. If both primary flight control + termination command logic share same microcontroller, a single firmware bug or power-domain failure cascades: cannot terminate if autonomy firmware crashes. K6 principle (independent path) requires hardware isolation: separate RF receiver, separate power supply, separate motor driver (hardware cutoff only, no firmware). Current architecture likely violates this; RTO zero unachievable with shared MCU.
findings.recommendation: Design independent hardware termination path: (1) dedicated RF receiver (separate crystal oscillator + demodulator), (2) independent power supply (super-capacitor or isolated battery), (3) hardware-only motor cutoff (no microcontroller involved, direct solid-state relay logic). Termination command must execute in <50 ms with zero software intervention. Crew activation: redundant operator input (2-person verification + automatic watchdog on lost-link). Pre-flight verification: apply termination command to one test motor (ground test), verify instant cutoff without delay. Log termination command + motor response to separate flight data recorder (independent from primary). Annual third-party certification audit (CAA-approved independent test lab).

findings.title: RFC 3161 TSA call blocks audit-log latency SLA (NFR-LAT-009)
findings.feature: Panel-action audit log write with cryptographic timestamp
findings.reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-009 budgets 500 ms p95 for panel-action log write (review comment, flag, decision state change). This is a critical-tier SLA for forensic audit trail. RFC 3161 Time Stamp Authority round-trip adds 80-150 ms to each write. If TSA call is synchronous, p95 latency will exceed 500 ms budget (500 ms - 100 ms reserved for network/app = 400 ms available, but 80-150 ms TSA + 100-150 ms immutable-log-write = 180-300 ms taken, leaving only 100-200 ms for actual app work and validation). If TSA call is asynchronous (fire-and-forget), audit trail is weakened: if process crashes before TSA response arrives, cryptographic timestamp is lost. Incident investigation depends on provable evidence of decision timing; a weak audit trail defeats forensic accountability.
findings.recommendation: Use asynchronous TSA call with durable queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ) to decouple user-facing latency from TSA round-trip. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff and dead-letter handling. Monitor TSA availability and set SLA alert if TSA response time exceeds 100 ms or availability drops below 99.5%. Use local clock to timestamp at time of action, then backfill cryptographic TSA token asynchronously. Test impact with synthetic load: validate that p95 NFR-LAT-009 remains <500 ms with async TSA. Conduct incident drills to verify forensic audit trail completeness after process crash scenarios.

findings.title: Missing distributed-trace instrumentation for end-to-end coverage matrix journey (applicant → matrix PDF)
findings.component: Coverage matrix generation (NFR-MON-WB-003, NFR-LAT-003)
findings.reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-003 budgets 600s p95 for matrix generation (100 MB pack). Component budgets are: OCR 180s, semantic classification 200s, matrix computation 90s, PDF rendering 40s. Current APM monitoring tracks aggregate inference latency, but missing distributed-trace correlation IDs linking: upload request → evidence store fetch → OCR batch → semantic classifier → matrix computation → PDF renderer. When p95 exceeds 600s, cannot pinpoint which component(s) are over budget; optimization is blind.
findings.recommendation: Inject OpenTelemetry trace IDs (W3C format) at evidence-upload initiation. Propagate through: storage fetch, OCR job submission, semantic inference queue, matrix DB query, PDF rendering. Instrument each stage with start/end timestamps and component-specific metrics (OCR: pages processed, semantic: tokens classified, PDF: elements rendered). Visualize end-to-end trace in distributed-trace UI (Jaeger/Datadog). Alert if any trace exceeds 600s; highlight slowest component in trace.

findings.title: Missing CI/CD regression detection for coverage matrix generation (inference model changes)
findings.component: Coverage matrix generation (NFR-MON-WB-003, NFR-LAT-003)
findings.reasoning: Coverage matrix engine (OCR + semantic classification) is ML-based. When model is retrained (monthly per adaptive training), new model may have higher latency (larger model) or different accuracy (may over/under-flag evidence). CI/CD deploys new model to staging; if p95 latency regresses >10% vs baseline, or if flag distribution shifts >20%, regression should be caught before production deployment. Current monitoring lacks 'build-over-build' regression test (compare staged model vs previous production model on held-out test set).
findings.recommendation: Implement regression test in CI/CD pipeline: (1) on model update, compute p95 latency on fixed 100 MB test pack (Scotland case + SORA case per Ada benchmarks), (2) compare to previous model; fail if >10% latency regression, (3) compute flag distribution (% RED/AMBER/GREEN) on test set; fail if kappa <0.85 vs previous model (possible accuracy regression). Fail CI gate if regression detected; require explicit approval from ML/platform leads to override. Generate weekly report: model performance trend (latency, accuracy, calibration).

findings.title: Evidence store metadata index degradation—object listing latency at 10M objects requires indexing strategy validation
findings.component: Evidence store (S3-like object storage) metadata query layer; database object inventory index
findings.reasoning: Ada capacity (NFR-CAP-WB-005) explicitly identifies 'slow growth of object metadata index causing search degradation' as a known risk. By 2028, evidence store will contain 10M+ objects (140 GB cumulative, ~20 MB per case, 7,000 cases). Evidence search (Noyce NFR-LAT-017) budgets p95 <5s across 10-year archive. Object listing (enumerating objects by case, date, classification tier) is a foundational query; if index fragmentation grows, latency can degrade to 10-20s, breaking interactive workload. Risk is highest at 3-year mark (36-month projection: 140 GB, 7,000 cases). Volume test (Gate 6) must populate full 10-year projection and measure index behavior.
findings.recommendation: Volume test (Gate 6) must execute against 140 GB replica with 10M objects. Benchmark: (1) Object listing by case_id (expected <100ms). (2) Object search by keyword (expected p95 <5s per Noyce). (3) Index statistics query (count objects by classification tier). Capture index size and page count before/after test. Monitor: query plan cost (EXPLAIN ANALYZE). If latency degrades >10% from baseline, implement indexing mitigation: (A) Partition evidence store by classification tier (separate S3 buckets: s3://caa-wb-official, s3://caa-wb-official-sensitive, etc.) to reduce per-partition object count; (B) Implement secondary metadata cache (Redis) for hot-path queries (recent cases); (C) Archive evidence older than 5 years to cold storage (Glacier) and rebuild index on remaining 5-year subset.

findings.title: Phased Trial Build-Up (REQ016) drives sustained multi-month submission and review load
findings.feature: multi-stage application review and phased gates
findings.reasoning: REQ016 (Phased Trial Build-Up) specifies that evidence builds progressively through gates, allowing reversibility. This design choice creates sustained capacity demand (long-running submissions in-progress) vs. batch processing. Vibe benchmark PackCompleteness indicates 28% first-submission failure rate; under phased-gate design, applicants will resubmit evidence multiple times (per-gate rework). Impact: (1) Concurrent active submissions remain high (10-20 simultaneously), each occupying evidence-store space and requiring reviewer bandwidth. (2) Review panels meet more frequently (5-15 per quarter vs. 2-3 if upfront completeness were stricter). (3) Database indexes must optimize for partial-evidence queries (what evidence is available for gate N vs. gate N+1?). Capacity model assumes sustained load; underestimating will cause database contention and reviewer burnout.
findings.recommendation: (1) Establish phased-gate SLA per REQ016: gate 1 (application intake + completeness check): 2 weeks turnaround; gate 2 (SORA + safety-case review): 4 weeks; gate 3 (crew training + ConOps validation): 2 weeks; gate 4 (joint panel authorization): 3-6 weeks. (2) Publish gate-specific checklists (Alexa's scales from vibe analysis): PackCompleteness (gate 1), SwarmAwareSORAEvidence (gate 2), ConOpsCompleteness (gate 3), AuthorizationReadiness (gate 4). (3) Implement per-gate evidence staging (Workbench data model): evidence required for gate N is archived; evidence for gate N+1 is in active review; older gates offloaded to cold storage. (4) Size database for 20 concurrent active submissions (peak); partition by gate to reduce index cardinality. (5) Monitor gate turnaround times; establish escalation procedures if gates begin to queue (bottleneck detection).

findings.title: ML Assurance Case (REQ009) adds inference-intensive evidence review workload
findings.feature: AI/ML assurance case review and explainability evidence analysis
findings.reasoning: REQ009 (ML Assurance Case) specifies AMLAS/SACE evidence + frozen-model manifest + explainability evidence. This is new content type not previously handled: (1) Evidence volume: AMLAS case study ~50-200 MB (training data summaries, model architecture docs, test reports, confidence scores); (2) Review complexity: ML assurance requires specialist review (AI/ML safety engineer); current CAA inspector pool has ~2-3 ML-qualified personnel (insufficient for ramp-up); (3) Evidence analysis: explainability outputs (saliency maps, decision trees) and training-data statistics require computational tools (ML model introspection libraries, data profiling). Estimated impact: 3-5 extra hours per case (AMLAS pilot cohort ~5-10 cases/year by 2028), plus capital cost for ML evidence analysis tools (GPUs, libraries, licenses).
findings.recommendation: (1) Recruit 2-3 AI/ML safety engineers to CAA inspector pool (by 2026 Q3); consider contract specialists from University of Sheffield CAA centre or Bristol University. (2) Establish AMLAS/SACE evidence checklist (per Vibe scale SwarmAwareSORAEvidence as precedent): minimum 20 evidence items per ML component, covering training-data provenance, model validation, robustness testing, failure modes, explainability. (3) Procure ML evidence analysis tools: MLflow (model registry), SHAP (explainability), Weights & Biases (training experiment tracking) as cloud services (integration with Workbench evidence store). (4) Create AI/ML assurance user story: 'As a CAA AI/ML safety engineer, I want interpretable ML evidence and frozen-model manifests so that I can assess the safety and airworthiness of autonomous flight systems.' (5) Target: by 2027 Q4, 50% of advanced autonomy submissions (GCAP/Tempest, Project Alvina cohort 2) include AMLAS evidence; by 2028 Q4, 100% of submissions with ML components.

findings.title: Submission intake processing capacity must scale to 4-5 submissions/week by 2028
findings.feature: application intake and evidence store
findings.reasoning: Capacity model projects intake growth from 1-2/week (2026 Q2) to 4-5/week by 2028 Q4 (assumes successful Protector RG Mk1 + Project Alvina + GCAP/Tempest rollout). This drives database transaction rate from 25 TPS (baseline) to 100+ TPS peak (concurrent reviews + panel deliberations). Evidence store grows from 5 GB (2026) to 60+ GB (2028). Database indexes must remain performant; traditional RDBMS (PostgreSQL) will require tuning (partitioning by year + case stage, incremental index builds, statistics updates). Peak concurrent users: 60-70 (applicants + inspectors + panelists) by 2028 Q4. Web tier should auto-scale (Kubernetes or equivalent) to handle 50-100 req/sec during peak review seasons.
findings.recommendation: (1) Perform database load test (2026 Q3): validate 100+ TPS sustained throughput with 20 concurrent active submissions, 50 concurrent users. Identify bottlenecks (index scans, full-table scans, locking contention). (2) Implement partitioning strategy: submissions partitioned by year + stage; archive tables partitioned by retention tier. (3) Deploy read replicas (primary + 2x replicas) for high-volume inspector review queries (read-heavy workload); async replication lag <100 ms acceptable. (4) Monitor key metrics: P95 query latency, transaction throughput, index fragmentation. Set alerts for degradation (P95 >500 ms, TPS <80 when expected 100+). (5) Plan database upgrade cycle (2026 Q2: PostgreSQL 15, 2027 Q2: add sharding if growth exceeds single-node capacity).

findings.title: Swarm C2 bandwidth budget must support 10-20 concurrent vehicles, 200+ KB/sec peak
findings.feature: C2 link and inter-vehicle coordination
findings.reasoning: Capacity model projects C2 bandwidth per REQ006 (authorized monitored C2): 5-vehicle swarm at 5 Hz telemetry + 1 Hz coordination = 25 msg/sec telemetry + 20 msg/sec coordination = 50 msg/sec nominal = 25-50 KB/sec. Peak scenarios (DAA encounter, high-frequency sensor updates): 10 vehicles at 10 Hz = 200 KB/sec (exceeds LTE single-link capacity ~150 kbps per carrier). For 10-vehicle swarm, must provision 2x LTE links or hybrid (LTE + fixed RF backup). Terrestrial network availability assumption: 99.5% uptime is insufficient for safety-critical C2 (worst-case: link loss during critical maneuver). Link-loss handling (REQ006 link loss survivable) requires onboard autonomy fallback + geofence containment (REQ005). Capacity constraint: remote trial locations (Scottish Highlands, Welsh countryside) may have poor LTE coverage; satellite C2 backup (~400 kbps max) may be only option.
findings.recommendation: (1) Define C2 bandwidth SLA per trial location: urban (LTE primary, 500+ kbps available) vs. rural (LTE + satellite hybrid) vs. remote (satellite primary, 400 kbps guaranteed). (2) Conduct LTE coverage survey (2026 Q2) for planned trial sites (Protector RG Mk1 locations, Project Alvina routes); map coverage gaps; identify satellite providers. (3) Procure dual-modem hardware (each vehicle): primary LTE (4G/5G) + secondary satellite (Inmarsat IDP or Iridium). (4) Establish C2 link monitoring (REQ006 monitored C2): real-time link quality metrics (RSSI, packet loss, latency); crew dashboard alerts if link quality degrades below safety threshold. (5) Define link-loss procedures (REQ011 contingency matrix): <100 ms uplink loss = continue current plan; 100 ms - 5 sec = trigger geofence RTL (return-to-launch); >5 sec = autonomy failsafe (land in-place or descend). (6) Validate with flight test: confirm link-loss detection + failsafe triggering in simulation and low-altitude trials before high-altitude BVLOS.

findings.title: DAA sensor fusion compute latency <500 ms; swarm coordination must scale to 10+ vehicles without exceeding decision-making budget
findings.feature: DAA sensor fusion and encounter de-confliction
findings.reasoning: REQ007 (Detect-and-Avoid Performance) specifies DO-365 / F3442 alignment, implying <500 ms latency budget for de-confliction decisions. Swarm coordination adds complexity: 5 vehicles = 10 pairwise coordination messages per cycle; 10 vehicles = 45 pairwise messages. At 5 Hz coordination rate, 10-vehicle swarm = 225 msg/sec inter-vehicle = 450+ KB/sec if uncompressed. Onboard compute (Jetson Nano, 10 GFLOPS) cannot sustain full-rate fusion for 10 vehicles; must compress (filter out non-critical tracks, batch messages). Latency risk: fusion algorithm takes >200 ms, decision-making <300 ms available, but margin is thin. Network latency (C2 relay of fusion data) adds 50-100 ms; total latency stack >500 ms during adverse conditions (congested airspace, multiple encounters). Safety impact: delayed de-confliction decision = collision risk.
findings.recommendation: (1) Conduct DAA fusion latency analysis (2026 Q2-Q3): measure end-to-end latency (sensor input → decision output) for 5, 7, 10-vehicle swarms in simulation. Identify latency bottlenecks (sensor processing, fusion algorithm, decision logic). (2) Establish latency budget allocation: sensor processing 50 ms, fusion algorithm 150 ms, decision logic 100 ms, network delay 50 ms, margin 50 ms = 500 ms total. (3) Design fusion algorithm with graceful degradation: nominal case (all sensor data available) uses full fusion; degraded case (missed messages, high latency) uses simplified tracks (last-known position + velocity). (4) Implement adaptive message compression: fusion messages include only tracks with delta >threshold (e.g., bearing change >2 degrees). Nominal: 100-200 msg/sec; peak: 225 msg/sec. (5) Deploy on heterogeneous compute (dual-core ARM + GPU or TPU for ML acceleration); validate <500 ms latency in flight test. (6) Establish contingency: if fusion latency exceeds budget, revert to point-defense DAA (each aircraft responsible for own de-confliction; loss of swarm coordinated maneuvers but preserves safety).

findings.title: Inspector pool must scale from 30-40 FTE (2026) to 50-60 FTE (2028); training and onboarding capacity critical
findings.feature: inspector review capacity and SORA assessment
findings.reasoning: Current CAA + MAA inspector pool: ~30-60 active users (estimate). By 2028 Q4, expected ramp: (1) Protector RG Mk1 trials (2026-2027): 1-2 inspectors dedicated; (2) Project Alvina BVLOS cohort (2027-2028): 3-5 dedicated; (3) GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy (2028+): 5-10 dedicated (including ML safety specialists). Total headcount growth: 30 → 60 FTE. Challenge: swarm-aware SORA assessment (REQ002) and ML assurance (REQ009) require specialist training; CAA currently has <5 JARUS SORA v2.5 certified assessors and <3 ML-qualified engineers. Training pipeline lag: new hires need 3-6 months to reach productivity; 2027-2028 bottleneck likely if recruitment delayed. Workbench must scale to support 60 concurrent users with low latency; current design (2-3 application servers) will be under-capacity by 2027 Q4.
findings.recommendation: (1) Establish CAA/MAA recruitment plan (2026 Q1): identify 20-30 inspector positions to fill by 2027 Q4. (2) Partner with universities for training: University of Sheffield CAA centre (SORA/JARUS specialists), Bristol University (ML safety), Loughborough (human factors). (3) Create inspector training curriculum (6 months intensive): SORA v2.5 swarm-aware assessment, AMLAS/SACE ML assurance, ConOps review, safety-case evaluation. (4) Establish mentorship program (pair new inspectors with experienced reviewers for 3 months). (5) Allocate 2-3 FTE to training delivery and curriculum maintenance. (6) Scale Workbench infrastructure ahead of headcount: upgrade application servers (3 → 5 instances, 2026 Q3); upgrade database replicas (2 → 3, 2026 Q4); add caching layer (2027 Q2). (7) Monitor inspection cycle times (gate turnaround per step): if >SLA, escalate hiring or prioritize training.

findings.title: Panel deliberation audit trail (immutable WORM logs) must survive system failures; recovery capability essential
findings.feature: joint review panel deliberation recording and audit trail
findings.reasoning: Panel deliberation audit trail (REQ012 MOR obligations) is immutable and forensically critical: if a panel decision is later challenged, the audit trail must prove what evidence was reviewed, who decided, and what conditions were set. Current capacity model assumes ~100 KB per action × 15 panels/year × 100 actions/panel = 150 MB/year cumulative audit trail. By 2028, cumulative audit trail 450+ MB. Database replication (normal failover) is insufficient: if primary DB is corrupted, replica may also be corrupted (cascading failure). Must implement write-once storage (WORM) with separate backup: S3 with versioning disabled + MFA delete protection, or dedicated WORM NAS. Disaster recovery test: simulate data loss, confirm audit trail recoverable within <24 hours (operational requirement: cannot delay authorization decisions pending archive recovery).
findings.recommendation: (1) Implement dual-layer audit trail: live transaction log (PostgreSQL WAL, fast recovery) + immutable archive (S3 WORM, long-term forensics). (2) Configure S3 WORM bucket: object lock with retain-until-date (10 years minimum per REQ012), MFA delete protection, separate AWS account from operational systems (air-gapped credentials). (3) Establish backup-to-archive pipeline: every 6 hours, dump audit trail from live DB to S3 WORM; checksums computed and verified. (4) Conduct disaster recovery drill (2026 Q4, 2027 Q4): simulate live DB corruption; confirm recovery from S3 WORM <24 hours; verify audit trail integrity (no missing records). (5) Monitor S3 WORM bucket for compliance (audit trail should only grow, never shrink or change). Alert if delete attempts (MFA delete should block). (6) Plan for long-term preservation: by 2036, convert S3 objects to enterprise archive media (optical discs, tape) for cost efficiency and resilience.

findings.title: AI-flag narrative p95 30-min budget at risk due to inference scaling and evidence chunking latency
findings.feature: AI-flag narrative rendering (30-min SLA)
findings.reasoning: End-to-end p95 1800 s budget allocates 900 s to AI inference per-chunk. For a typical evidence pack with 100+ distinct sections (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, Training, Personnel, Environmental, etc.), serial inference at 10–15 s per section results in 1000–1500 s baseline. Parallel inference across sections mitigates this, but requires reservation of GPU resources; contention during peak submission periods (e.g., near trial approval deadlines) may cause queuing delays of 300+ s. Additionally, evidence chunking strategy (semantic vs. sliding-window) and chunk boundary optimization add unpredictability. Confidence is moderate-to-high based on observed transformer inference latencies at scale.
findings.recommendation: 1. Implement streaming narrative flag generation: show first-pass flags at 10 min, iterative refinement to 30 min. 2. Baseline inference latency per evidence section type (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, etc.); create section-type-specific SLAs. 3. Reserve dedicated GPU capacity for narrative workload; implement priority queue (urgent cases bump lower-priority). 4. Measure end-to-end latency percentiles on a 100-case cohort; if p95 > 25 min, explore model quantization or distillation. 5. Add user-facing progress bar and ETA; set expectation that full narrative will be available within 30 min but first-pass flags appear sooner.

findings.title: Evidence upload SLA does not specify throughput for OFFICIAL vs. CONFIDENTIAL vs. UNCLASSIFIED evidence; classification overhead not budgeted
findings.feature: Evidence upload throughput (classification handling)
findings.reasoning: Parent NFR specifies 30-s p95 for 100 MB pack under OFFICIAL classification, but the upload SLA does not differentiate latency for CONFIDENTIAL or UNCLASSIFIED evidence, nor does it account for mandatory inline classification validation. OFFICIAL evidence may require additional cryptographic checks (MOD classification marks, compartment validation) that add 100–500 ms per document. The current budget (3–5 MB/s net throughput) assumes classification is negligible, but may be violated if classification overhead is substantial. Additionally, no SLA is defined for hybrid packs that span multiple classification levels.
findings.recommendation: 1. Measure classification-overhead latency per evidence type (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, etc.) for OFFICIAL vs. CONFIDENTIAL vs. UNCLASSIFIED. 2. Define separate SLAs: (a) UNCLASSIFIED: p95 <30 s for 100 MB, (b) OFFICIAL: p95 <40 s for 100 MB, (c) hybrid pack: p95 <45 s (worst-case classification path). 3. Implement fast-track for UNCLASSIFIED (skip crypto checks); queue OFFICIAL/CONFIDENTIAL for async classification validator. 4. Add workbench UI warning: 'Uploads containing OFFICIAL evidence may take up to 40 s; use batch upload for efficiency.'

findings.title: No latency SLA defined for Completeness Check (REQ001) beyond generic 'within submission workflow'; actual duration and acceptance criteria undefined
findings.feature: Completeness Check (F02, initial intake gateway)
findings.reasoning: Parent NFR REQ001 ('complete Operational Authorisation pack') and parent user story NFR ('Full workbench open-and-review cycle shall not require more than two working days') do not explicitly define a latency target for the Completeness Check step that gates case intake. Vibe Requirement PackCompleteness defines a scale (percentage of packs passing on first check) but no latency budget. Current NFR-LAT-005 targets Readiness Check (Step 4, post-upload) but there is a logical mismatch: intake-phase completeness validation (Step 1, synchronous) vs. in-panel readiness check (Step 4, asynchronous). Confidence is high that this gap exists.
findings.recommendation: 1. Define explicit Completeness Check SLA for Step 1: 'p95 <10 s for intake-phase completeness screening; full document validation (PackCompleteness scale) p95 <60 s.' 2. Clarify: is Completeness Check a blocking gateway (hold submission until pass), or advisory (flag issues but allow proceeding)? 3. If blocking, allocate <10 s p95 for automated checks (file count, mandatory mime types, document header validation); defer manual review to asynchronous process. 4. Create acceptance criteria checklist for case officers ('Completeness Check pass = [list] all present').

findings.title: Coverage matrix generation latency can be improved 30–50% by splitting text extraction and inference into separate queue tiers
findings.feature: Coverage matrix generation (OCR + semantic inference pipeline)
findings.reasoning: Current end-to-end budget (NFR-LAT-003) allocates 180–360 s for OCR and 200–480 s for semantic inference in series. However, OCR is I/O-bound (disk read, image processing) while semantic inference is compute-bound (GPU); they can be parallelized. By decoupling, OCR can run on CPU workers while a separate batch of inference jobs (from prior pack uploads) runs on GPU. Additionally, OCR can be spun up on-demand across multiple machines (stateless, horizontal scale), reducing bottleneck risk. Confidence is high due to known workload characteristics of batch ML pipelines.
findings.recommendation: 1. Refactor coverage matrix pipeline: (a) OCR tier: CPU-based document processing, output token stream to message queue; (b) Inference tier: GPU-based semantic classification, consume token stream from queue. 2. Scale OCR tier horizontally (auto-scale based on document queue depth). 3. Implement per-document OCR timeout (5 min/doc); if exceeded, report 'partial coverage' matrix and allow manual remediation. 4. Baseline latency reduction; target 30–50% improvement (10–15 min p95 for 500 MB pack). 5. Monitor queue depths and tier utilization; implement cost-aware scaling (turn down GPU when no pending inference).

findings.title: Shared database backend between applicant workspace and panel tooling creates correlation failure risk (K1 style)
findings.feature: system_architecture
findings.reasoning: If applicant workspace and panel tooling share primary DB backend, a single DB failure cascades: (1) applicants cannot submit, (2) panel cannot deliberate simultaneously. Per Effective Performance Engineering 'keystone' principle (K1): if geofence and autonomy share compute, they share failure. Same logic here: if audit log and panel action succeed but case metadata fails to write, audit shows decision but case is missing, resulting in non-defensible state. Correlation risk unmitigated.
findings.recommendation: Decouple storage tiers: (1) applicant workspace DB — cost-optimized, 99.5% availability acceptable, async replication OK, (2) panel operations DB — high-availability (99.9%), synchronous replication required, (3) audit/deliberation ledger — append-only, separate from relational DB, 100% write guarantee, (4) archive DB — immutable snapshot after decision finalized. Each tier has independent failover strategy; no shared critical path. Enforce 'no shared single point of failure' rule: audit log writes must succeed even if case metadata fails (transaction commits atomically to ledger, then to archive, asynchronously to case DB).

findings.title: Identity provider as external dependency SPOF; no local fallback for classified or unclassified access
findings.feature: authentication_services
findings.reasoning: Both classified (MOD LDAP) and unclassified (CAA OAuth2) access depend on external identity providers. If MOD LDAP unreachable, no classified evidence can be accessed (panel cannot proceed). If CAA OAuth2 unreachable, applicants cannot log in (but offline pack submission possible). No mention of local authentication cache or fallback. REQ015 (CAA/MAA Interface Management) assumes identity is seamless; not true.
findings.recommendation: Implement OAuth2/LDAP token caching with 24h TTL (cached identity valid for extended session even if provider unavailable). Fallback to time-limited session refresh using cached token + biometric re-verification (fingerprint on panel device). For classified access: implement local MOD LDAP replica (cached from primary, updated hourly, read-only). Test provider outage failover quarterly: block identity service, verify cached tokens still allow access, measure time-to-first-cache-miss. Emergency procedure: manual identity re-verification by CAA/MOD administrator if both primary + cache unavailable (audit log events marked 'offline mode').

findings.title: C2 link resilience target (REQ006, Vibe 90 sec) implies autonomous swarm capability not yet proven; gap between wish (60 sec) and goal (90 sec) reflects immaturity
findings.feature: c2_link_availability
findings.reasoning: Vibe targets show progression: wish 60 sec (end Phase 3, current status 45 sec), goal 90 sec (end Phase 5, stretch feasibility), stretch 300 sec with combined C2+GPS loss (uncertain feasibility). Current C2 link resilience (45 sec) insufficient for BVLOS swarm with autonomous decision-making. Goal (90 sec) requires mature swarm coordination algorithms; not yet demonstrated. If goal not achieved by end Phase 5, trial must continue with crew takeover as failsafe (limits autonomy benefit).
