findings.recommendation: Implement tiered C2 loss response: (1) 0-5 sec loss: autonomous hold formation, reduce throttle to slow descent, (2) 5-30 sec loss: autonomous RTL to last-known safe zone (geofence center), (3) 30-90 sec loss: crew takeover window (if C2 restored, crew resumes control; if not, RTL continues), (4) >90 sec: flight termination (if no other option). Dual-path C2: licensed UHF primary + satellite fallback (or dual-frequency ground stations). Test autonomous survival monthly: controlled RF blackout, verify formation hold <200 ms response time, RTL initiated correctly. Document which behaviors remain autonomous vs crew-dependent; use to set realistic SAIL/OSO envelopes in SORA.

findings.title: Ground control segment availability during trial window not explicitly required; but unspecified failover creates trial abort risk
findings.feature: ground_control_segment
findings.reasoning: Parent user story: Workbench unavailability shall not block trial (out-of-band via Range Safety Case). But ground control station failure = trial abort, potential safety risk if swarm enters off-nominal state during abort. Current architecture likely single control station per trial; dual-operator crew provides human redundancy but not system redundancy. Network path to airfield (ISP/fiber) is SPOF. Pre-flight ground-station validation not mandated.
findings.recommendation: Deploy dual ground control stations (primary + hot-standby) at trial location with identical crew briefing and switchover procedure. Network redundancy: dual ISP paths (different providers/technologies) or satellite backup. Automated failover: if primary station loses telemetry >500 ms, alert standby crew (pre-briefed on last-known swarm state). Telemetry logging to redundant independent recorders. Test ground-station failover monthly with live crew; post-test debrief documents switchover time + crew workload. Pre-trial validation: 30 min ground-test with both stations active, verify both receive same telemetry stream.

findings.title: AI narrative service GPU memory ceiling at 10 concurrent narratives (2028 demand)
findings.component: AI flag narrative service
findings.reasoning: Noyce's latency budget allocates 900s for AI inference per narrative (NFR-LAT-007, 30 min SLA). At 20 concurrent narrative requests (5x growth), system requires 10 GPU instances (1800s / 900s inference per GPU = 2 concurrent narratives per GPU). Each 40GB A100 GPU serves 2 concurrent narratives at 100 tokens/sec streaming rate. GPU memory bottleneck: 13B LLM model = 26 GB; 2 concurrent inferences = 52 GB RAM (exceeds 40 GB). Mitigation: smaller model (7B = 14 GB; fits 2 concurrent), quantization (4-bit = 7 GB per 7B model), or vLLM paged attention (reduces memory by 30%). Current deployment: 0 instances (feature not yet live). By 2028, vLLM or equivalent is mandatory.
findings.recommendation: Deploy vLLM inference engine (paged attention reduces memory overhead 30-40%). Test 7B model quantization (4-bit) for quality/latency trade-off. Procure 10 A100 GPU instances by end 2027 to handle 20 concurrent narratives at 1800s p95 SLA. Fallback: if GPU cost prohibitive, implement synchronous narrative batch processing (reduce concurrency to 3-5, accept longer queue latency 30-45 min).

findings.title: Panel action-log write contention at p95 500 ms SLA will break under 10+ concurrent panelists
findings.component: Primary database / Panel action-log write
findings.reasoning: Noyce's latency budget (NFR-LAT-009) allocates 150 ms for immutable-log write + 100 ms replication quorum. At 10 concurrent panelists during voting, action-log write rate peaks at 30-50 actions/sec (3-5 actions per panelist per second during rapid polling). Distributed ledger quorum write (2-of-3 replicas ACK) exhibits tight coupling: if any replica is slow (network jitter, fsync latency), entire quorum stalls. Testing shows single-instance latency baseline 150 ms; under 10-way contention, lock-wait overhead + fsync jitter can push p95 to 600-800 ms (violating 500 ms SLA by 20-60%). Current panel size: 8-12 members; peak concurrency ~8 (not all writing simultaneously). By 2028, larger panels (15+ members, longer deliberations) may trigger contention.
findings.recommendation: Implement optimistic locking for panel action-log writes (version-based conflicts; retry on collision). Separate action-log into per-panelist write path (no contention) with async aggregation to master log (trades 1-5 sec consistency delay for contention-free writes). Alternatively, upgrade replication to quorum-gossip (3-way replication with early-abort if 2 replicas ACK, no wait for 3rd) to reduce consensus latency from 150 ms to 80-100 ms.

findings.title: WAN failover breaks audit-log write SLA (500 ms p95); air-gapping SECRET tier replication costly
findings.component: Audit / Deliberation log
findings.reasoning: Audit-log distributed ledger requires 3-replica quorum write (2-of-3 ACK). LAN latency (same AZ, <5 ms round-trip) allows 150 ms quorum consensus (Noyce budget). WAN failover (cross-region replica for disaster recovery) introduces 30-100 ms latency per network hop. In WAN mode, 500 ms p95 SLA becomes unachievable (3-way quorum = 300 ms baseline + jitter). SECRET tier air-gap (on-prem facility) makes WAN failover infeasible (dedicated air-gapped replicas required; cost prohibitive for third replica). OFFICIAL tier: can use cloud cross-region (AZ failover sufficient). OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE: on-prem 2 replicas (no WAN).
findings.recommendation: For OFFICIAL tier: implement AZ failover (2 replicas in AZ-1, 1 in AZ-2, both same region <20 ms latency). Audit-log SLA remains <500 ms. For SECRET tier: establish hot-standby replica in same facility (air-gapped); eliminate WAN failover. Disaster recovery (multi-site backup): async archive to S3 with 1-2 hour RPO (acceptable for audit trail). Accept that SECRET tier has 2-4 hour RTO on total facility loss; mitigation = strict physical security + redundant power/cooling.

findings.title: Evidence store metadata query index must be separate from object store to avoid O(n) scan at scale
findings.component: Evidence store metadata indexing
findings.reasoning: Ada's capacity model projects 140 GB cumulative evidence by 2028 (1000+ objects if average 100 MB per case). Evidence-discovery queries (NFR-LAT-017: 'find all evidence from aircraft X, 2026-2027, OFFICIAL tier') require metadata search. S3 ListObjects is O(n) scan (slow for >1000 objects). Without indexed metadata, archive search SLA (5s p95) becomes unachievable at scale. Current architecture: not specified. Gap: evidence metadata (case_id, trial_date, classification_tier, aircraft_id, keywords) must be indexed in separate DB (not S3 object metadata alone).
findings.recommendation: Implement metadata index (PostgreSQL or Elasticsearch): track every evidence object with (s3_key, case_id, trial_date, tier, aircraft_id, keywords). Index size: ~1 KB per object × 1000 objects = 1 MB (negligible). Query: 'aircraft A1 + 2026-2027 + OFFICIAL' → index lookup <100 ms; S3 GetObject for results <1s. Update metadata on every evidence upload (add to index before S3 PutObject completes). Cost: <GBP 100/month for indexed metadata DB.

findings.title: Hash-chain verification overhead risks case-open SLA (NFR-LAT-006 p95 10s)
findings.feature: Evidence chain-of-custody hash verification (SHA-256, parallelized)
findings.reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-006 'Panel case-open and load' budgets 10 seconds (p95) for regulator to click a case and see interactive panel. Hash-chain verification (ensuring evidence has not been tampered with) is a critical forensic control. Typical 100 MB evidence pack: 50-500 ms to compute SHA-256 hashes (CPU-bound on single thread). If system processes cases sequentially or if CPU contention occurs during peak panel review (10+ simultaneous reviewers per Noyce NFR-LAT-019), hash verification latency could balloon to 1-2 seconds per case. Noyce NFR-LAT-006 component budget for 'evidence_metadata_load' is only 1500 ms; hash verification cannot consume >200 ms without risking p95 overrun. Incident investigation (AAIB, Service Inquiry) depends on provable evidence integrity; cannot skip hash verification, but parallel computation is essential.
findings.recommendation: Implement parallel SHA-256 verification using worker threads (1 thread per 10 MB chunk, or use GPU acceleration if available). Pre-compute hashes during evidence upload (Noyce NFR-LAT-002) and cache in fast-access tier (Redis, in-process cache). Use cryptographic hash acceleration (AES-NI, SHA-NI CPU extensions) if available on compute platform. Target hash verification latency: <200 ms p95 for 100 MB typical case, <500 ms p95 for 500 MB worst-case pack (Noyce NFR-LAT-018). Implement incremental verification: verify hashes in background while rendering case panel (non-blocking, alert user if verification fails). Conduct stress-testing with 10+ simultaneous case-opens to measure hash verification contention. If p95 exceeds 200 ms under load, scale hash-verification pool horizontally.

findings.title: Classification marking validation overhead scales poorly with search results (NFR-LAT-017)
findings.feature: Classification boundary check during evidence search across 10-year archive
findings.reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-017 'Evidence search across classification boundary' budgets 5 seconds (p95) for a query returning multiple results across 10-year archive. Classification boundary check (ensuring user's clearance level permits access to each result) is required per result. If query returns 1000 results and classification check is 5 ms per result, total overhead is 5 seconds alone, consuming the entire SLA budget before search index lookup or result rendering. Workbench stores 10 years of trial records (estimated 10,000+ evidence items per year = 100,000+ items searchable). Classification boundary logic is non-negotiable (UK OFFICIAL/OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE/SECRET segregation per parent NFR), but lazy evaluation (checking classification after result fetching) could degrade UX if checks block result rendering.
findings.recommendation: Pre-filter evidence by user's classification clearance level at search index level (Elasticsearch, Solr) rather than post-query filtering. Index evidence with clearance-level tags (OFFICIAL, OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE, SECRET) and use index-level access control to eliminate ineligible results before retrieval. Cache classification-clearance decisions per user session (TTL = 1 hour) to amortize repeated lookups. For bulk export (decision record PDF, Noyce NFR-LAT-010), batch classification checks asynchronously. Test search latency with realistic 10-year archive size (100,000+ items); measure p95 latency scaling with result count. Set performance regression alert if classification boundary check pushes p95 search latency >5 seconds.

findings.title: DPIA redaction blocks decision-record export SLA (REQ012, Noyce NFR-LAT-010)
findings.feature: MOR/DPIA-driven PII redaction on decision-record PDF export
findings.reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-010 'Decision record export as PDF' budgets 300 seconds (p95) for a complete PDF with evidence citations, panel decisions, and audit summary. DPIA-required redaction (removing witness names, applicant PII, addresses per Data Protection Act 2018 / UK GDPR) adds significant CPU overhead. PII pattern matching (regex or NLP-based) on large text corpus (100+ MB evidence) can consume 50-200 ms per pattern check. If DPIA redaction rules are complex (20+ patterns for different PII types), total redaction latency could be 1-4 seconds per export. Parent NFR specifies handling OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE material (which contains names of applicants and witnesses); redaction is non-negotiable but latency-sensitive. Simple redaction (list of known witness names) is fast; complex redaction (identifying unknown PII via ML) is slow.
findings.recommendation: Implement two-tier redaction: fast-path (whitelist of known PII fields: witness names, contact info from submission metadata) and slow-path (ML-based PII detection for unknown entities). For fast-path redaction, pre-extract PII at evidence upload time and cache redaction list. Offer fast-export option (simple redaction, <60 seconds SLA) for common cases and slow-export option (comprehensive redaction, <5 minutes SLA) for complex cases. Implement async redaction: generate unredacted PDF immediately, redact in background, notify user when complete. For 500 MB worst-case evidence pack (Noyce NFR-LAT-018), estimate redaction latency and implement streaming redaction to avoid memory exhaustion. Test DPIA redaction latency with realistic witness/applicant data (100-500 entities per case); measure p95 latency and ensure it does not dominate NFR-LAT-010 budget.

findings.title: Classification boundary service vulnerable to slow-loris attack during live panel
findings.feature: Classification boundary check in evidence search (Noyce NFR-LAT-017)
findings.reasoning: Classification boundary checker is a dedicated microservice (per Noyce architecture) called for every evidence search and panel-action authorization. Slow-loris attack (attacker sends incomplete HTTP requests, holding connections open) could exhaust server connection pool, blocking legitimate searches and panel decisions. Parent story DoD specifies red-team exercises against live system during panel operation. If red-team performs coordinated slow-loris attack at panel-facing boundary-check endpoint (which would be known to red-team during architecture review), panel members could lose ability to navigate evidence or log actions, effectively halting case review. Noyce NFR-LAT-006 'case-open' and NFR-LAT-008 'evidence navigation' both depend on responsive boundary-check service.
findings.recommendation: Implement request timeout (30-second read timeout) at HTTP layer to drop incomplete requests. Use connection pooling with configurable connection limits (e.g., max 1000 concurrent connections). Implement connection-limit per source IP (e.g., max 10 concurrent connections per IP). Deploy reverse-proxy (nginx, HAProxy) in front of boundary-check service with slow-loris mitigation enabled. Monitor connection state histogram; alert if >5% of connections are in incomplete-request state. Implement adaptive timeouts: if connection queue is growing, reduce timeout from 30s to 10s to force faster cleanup. Conduct red-team exercise targeting slow-loris attack; measure impact on NFR-LAT-006 and NFR-LAT-008 latency. If p95 latency exceeds SLA during slow-loris attack, implement circuit-breaker: if boundary-check response time exceeds 2 seconds, serve cached classification decision (with staleness warning).

findings.title: Readiness Check (60 s p95) must render verdict before blocking issues list to minimize cognitive load
findings.feature: Phase 1 Step 4: Submission Readiness Check
findings.reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-005) targets 60 s p95 for Readiness Check completion. Applicant submits check and waits for PASS/FAIL decision. If system responds with verbose blocking issues list before verdict (e.g., 30-second scrolling list of failures), applicant loses confidence and perception of time-to-answer lengthens. UX best practice: announce verdict first, then details. Applicant's cognitive load is highest at this decision point—they need immediate clarity on pass/fail before navigating remediation steps.
findings.recommendation: Render PASS/FAIL verdict immediately (large green checkmark or red X with clear text: 'Ready to submit' or 'Blocking issues found'). Surface blocking issues list below verdict, sorted by severity (RED/AMBER first). Provide estimated remediation time per issue: 'ODD incomplete: ~15 min to fix. ConOps review pending: ~2-3 days.' Implement timeout handling: if check exceeds 120 s, surface 'Still checking...' with refresh button and estimated remaining time. Log all timeouts and errors for analysis of validation engine bottlenecks.

findings.title: Panel case-open latency (10 s p95) impacts regulator efficiency in multi-day sessions
findings.feature: Step 5: Panel Case-Open and Load (Click to Rendered)
findings.reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-006): 10 s p95 for case-open operation. Regulator in panel session opens 10-20 cases per day across 2-day deliberation. If each case takes 10 s to open, 10 cases = 100 s of waiting (plus network variability spikes). Cumulative latency frustration impacts regulator satisfaction and decision quality. Classified network can experience latency spikes if MAA networks are congested. Case assembly is split between app-server (2s budget) and panel UI rendering (3.9s budget)—rendering is the bottleneck.
findings.recommendation: Prefetch next 3 likely cases based on case list scroll position (background fetch of case metadata without full rendering). Render skeleton case panel (header + classification badge) immediately while evidence metadata loads. Prioritize rendering of classification marking (must appear within 2 s to prevent accidental disclosure). Implement case-open instrumentation: log p50/p90/p95 latency per case to identify outliers. If case-open exceeds 15 s, alert infrastructure team for network investigation.

findings.title: Evidence navigation (2 s p99) must remain responsive during high-frequency panel pointer clicks
findings.feature: Step 5 Continuation: Evidence Page/Paragraph Navigation (Clause Pointer Resolution)
findings.reasoning: Noyce budget (NFR-LAT-008): 2 s p99 for evidence section navigation. Regulator navigates via clause references mapped to evidence paragraphs. High-frequency operation: 50-100 clicks per case during deliberation. If navigation latency creeps to 3-4 s, regulator loses flow state and makes annotation errors. DOM rendering (0.8s budget) + JavaScript interaction (0.6s budget) are tight. Cache behavior is critical: evidence pages must be cached after initial case load to avoid repeated server fetches.
findings.recommendation: Cache all evidence pages in browser once case is loaded. Implement hash-based navigation (#clause-123) for instant in-page jumps without server fetch. Highlight target paragraph with subtle animation (yellow background, then fade to normal). For evidence archive searches (NFR-LAT-017: 5 s p95 across 10-year archive), accept slightly longer latency but prefetch top 3 search results. Monitor navigation latency via field instrumentation (PerformanceObserver). Alert if p99 exceeds 3 s.

findings.title: Upload success rate (p95 >98%) requires robust retry logic and clear error messaging
findings.feature: Evidence Upload & Error Handling
findings.reasoning: Target: p95 completion on first attempt >98%. Network conditions vary widely (4G tethering to enterprise broadband). Virus scan + content classification introduce failure modes (file rejected, timeout). Every failed upload requires manual retry, doubling time-to-matrix and abandonment risk. Analysis: 2% error rate is acceptable (e.g., 1 failure per 50 uploads); unacceptable is silent failure or cryptic error message ('Upload failed') with no clear remediation.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish error types in UX: Network timeout → 'Connection lost. Retrying in 5s...' with exponential backoff. Virus scan failure → 'File quarantined for security. Contact support.' Classification rejection → 'This document marked OFFICIAL. Please verify classification level and reupload.' Implement auto-retry up to 3x with user-visible countdown. Log all errors with timestamp, file hash, and network conditions for post-analysis. Ensure error messages are announced by screen reader before interactive controls.

findings.title: Keyboard navigation must be first-class, not secondary path
findings.feature: Keyboard-Operable Panel Workflows (WCAG 2.2 AA, Keyboard Accessibility)
findings.reasoning: Reasonable-adjustments profile requires keyboard-operable interface for users with motor impairments. Panel workflows (case-open, evidence navigation, comment submission) must be fully keyboard-accessible. Risk: if keyboard navigation requires additional steps compared to mouse (e.g., Tab 20 times to reach button vs. click), regulator perceives tool as inaccessible and may refuse to use it. Regulatory requirement: CAA may audit keyboard accessibility during tool validation.
findings.recommendation: Implement keyboard shortcuts for common panel actions: Ctrl+S (save comment), Ctrl+N (next case), Ctrl+P (previous case), ? (show help). Ensure tab order matches visual flow (left-to-right, top-to-bottom). Provide skip links for evidence list (jump to evidence table, skipping navigation). Test keyboard navigation with screen readers: ensure focus announcements are clear and descriptive. Run through WAVE or Axe accessibility audit before each release. Conduct usability testing with motor-impaired users (keyboard-only mode).

findings.title: No real-time alert correlation between classification-tier changes and regulator calibration drift
findings.component: Classification boundary service + AI-flag narrative service (NFR-MON-WB-008, NFR-MON-WB-011)
findings.reasoning: When classification library is updated (3-4 times/year per Ada capacity) or clause versioning changes, AI-flag generation may drift (different evidence retrieval logic, different semantic search results). Simultaneously, regulator override rates may spike because flags no longer align with their previous calibration. Current monitoring treats these as independent; no cross-correlation to identify root cause (library change) vs symptom (calibration drift).
findings.recommendation: Implement alert correlation: if library revision advances AND regulator override rate increases >15% within 24 hours, trigger 'Possible library-change impact on AI flags' alert (confidence: medium). Include in alert: which clauses changed, override-rate magnitude, affected case types. Do NOT auto-remediate; escalate to CAA Innovation Hub for investigation. Add monthly report: library changes vs override-rate deltas (train regulator expectations on expected variance).

findings.title: High-volume panel-action log write alerts (quorum replication latency spikes) may cause alert fatigue
findings.component: Panel-action log write (NFR-MON-WB-007, NFR-LAT-009)
findings.reasoning: Audit-trail writes during high-concurrency panel deliberation (10+ reviewers, 30-50 actions/sec) experience quorum-replication latency spikes >500ms due to network jitter, fsync stalls, or GC pauses on Kafka broker. Current alert config: alert_critical if p95 > 500ms sustained for 10 minutes. In practice, cluster-wide GC pause might spike 15-20 writes in a row to >500ms, triggering 10-minute sustained alert → operations team gets paged for false-positive (system recovered after GC finished). Leads to alert fatigue and slower incident response.
findings.recommendation: Refine alert thresholds: (1) warn if p95 > 500ms for 30+ consecutive seconds (short spikes are expected under load), (2) critical if p95 > 500ms for 10+ minutes AND error rate >0.1% (indicates structural problem, not transient GC). Exclude GC pause windows: detect GC events via JVM metrics, suppress alert during known GC phases. Additionally, implement alert aggregation: if >50 panel-action-write warnings in 1 hour, group into single 'High write-latency period' incident (prevent individual-message alerts). Provide runbook: 'Write latency spike > 500ms' → check Kafka broker CPU, check replication lag, check fsync errors.

findings.title: C2 link packet-loss alerts will spike during legitimate RF stress tests and red-team exercises
findings.component: C2 link SNR and packet loss (NFR-MON-SWM-002)
findings.reasoning: Current alert: 'packet loss > 5% sustained for 10 seconds → critical.' Monthly red-team exercises intentionally degrade C2 link to test swarm resilience (RF jamming, spectrum interference). Similarly, authorized field trials in poor RF environments (maritime, mountains) will naturally experience higher packet loss. If alerts are not context-aware (don't know red-team window or trial location), every legitimate test will trigger false-positive criticality alerts, causing alert fatigue and slower response to real problems.
findings.recommendation: Implement context-aware alerts: associate each trial window with expected RF conditions (baseline, degraded, or poor). Set alert thresholds relative to expected baseline: warn if loss rate > 1.5x baseline, critical if > 3x baseline (anomaly outside expectation). During red-team exercises, suppress loss-rate alerts (but keep SNR/jitter alerts active for safety). Tag all trial metadata with expected RF conditions; use as alert-suppression context. Generate post-trial report: actual vs expected packet loss, any anomalies outside RF envelope.

findings.title: No canary-deployment monitoring for workbench feature rollouts (AI-flag narrative service)
findings.component: AI-flag narrative service (NFR-MON-WB-004, NFR-LAT-007)
findings.reasoning: AI-flag narrative is long-running (30 min), high-stakes (regulator decisions depend on it). New LLM versions or prompt-tuning changes can alter output quality subtly (e.g., narrative becomes more verbose, flags shift from RED to AMBER). Current monitoring tracks latency and error rate, but missing quality metrics (narrative helpfulness, regulator trust). Rollout should be canary (5% traffic) with hold-back comparison: old vs new LLM on same evidence set, measure inter-rater agreement on which flags are better. Missing this, bad rollout reaches 100% production before quality regression detected.
findings.recommendation: Implement canary rollout: (1) deploy new LLM to 5% of narrative requests for 24 hours, (2) hold-back comparison: run both old and new LLM on same evidence set (offline, not impacting regulator). Generate comparison report: latency delta, error-rate delta, narrative similarity (semantic comparison via embedding distance). Measure regulator satisfaction via survey on canary cohort (do they find new narrative more helpful?). Fail rollout if latency regresses >10%, or if satisfaction score <7/10 vs baseline. Proceed to 25% → 100% rollout in stages over 5 days.

findings.title: Missing regression detection for clause library versioning (impact on readiness checks)
findings.component: Clause library and readiness check (NFR-MON-WB-009, NFR-MON-WB-010)
findings.reasoning: Clause library updates (3-4 major revisions/year per Ada capacity) change readiness validation rules. New clause may tighten acceptance criteria, causing historical pass-rate to drop (previously-passing submissions now fail). Without regression test, deployment of tightened clause library goes live → next day, 50% of submissions fail readiness check (spike in rejections) → applicants confused, support team overwhelmed. Regression should be caught pre-deployment: twin-sample test (Scotland case + SORA case) must maintain >95% pass rate on clause library update.
findings.recommendation: Create regression test set: 100 representative submissions (mix of trial types: VLOS, BVLOS, autonomy). Before library deployment, run readiness check on test set with old library, then with new library. Fail deployment if pass-rate drops >5% (indicates rule tightening likely to impact production). If drop is acceptable (intentional tightening), generate summary: 'Pass rate dropped 10% due to ConOps clause tightening; expected impact 50 submissions/quarter.' Alert CAA communications team to prepare applicant guidance. Monitor production pass-rate for 1 week post-deployment; alert if drop exceeds predicted impact.

findings.title: Missing confidence-threshold calibration for workbench AI inference (semantic classification and narrative generation)
findings.component: Coverage matrix engine and AI-flag narrative service (NFR-MON-WB-003, NFR-MON-WB-004)
findings.reasoning: Coverage matrix engine outputs confidence scores per evidence class (0-100%). Low-confidence classifications (40-60% range) should be flagged for manual review, but current monitoring only tracks aggregate confidence distribution. No threshold calibration: at what confidence level should system escalate to human reviewer? If threshold is too loose (>30%), legitimate low-confidence flags are not escalated; if too tight (<80%), system escalates everything (alert fatigue). As evidence distribution shifts with new trial types (autonomy vs swarm), confidence threshold needs adjustment.
findings.recommendation: Implement confidence-threshold calibration: quarterly, measure precision/recall of classification at different thresholds (30%, 40%, 50%, 60%, 70%, 80%, 90%). Plot ROC curve: as threshold rises, recall drops but precision rises. Choose threshold at elbow (typically 60-70%): balances flagging genuinely ambiguous evidence vs over-escalating. If new trial type introduced (e.g., advanced ML swarm trials), retrain threshold on that cohort. Generate per-trial report: confidence histogram, % of classifications below threshold (escalation rate), regulator feedback on whether escalations were justified.

findings.title: Missing applicant dashboard with readiness progression and ETA to gate pass
findings.component: Applicant workspace and readiness check (NFR-USE-002, NFR-USE-003, NFR-USE-005)
findings.reasoning: Parent story: 'Full workbench open-and-review cycle shall not require more than two working days.' Applicant experiences submission as long-running process: upload → matrix generation (10 min) → readiness check (1 min) → gate decision (days). Current dashboard shows current step only; applicant cannot see: time spent per step, remaining time to gate pass, which blocking issues are high-impact. High abandonment risk if applicant perceives progress is stuck (no feedback for 2+ hours during matrix generation). Monitoring collects telemetry; missing dashboard to visualize it for applicant perspective.
findings.recommendation: Create applicant dashboard: (1) submission status (received, upload-in-progress, matrix-generating, readiness-checking, awaiting-panel), (2) progress timeline: matrix generation 5/10 min complete (50%), expected finish 10 min from now, (3) blocking issues (if any): 'ConOps document missing - estimate 30 min to fix,' (4) ETA to panel review: 'Your submission is 3rd in queue; expect panel review to start 2 working days from now.' Refresh dashboard every 10s during long operations. Use optimistic estimates (show expected completion time, not hard deadline) to avoid unrealistic expectations.

findings.title: Missing inspector/regulator dashboard: my active cases + SLA countdowns + escalation queue
findings.component: Inspector review sessions and panel deliberation (NFR-USE-007, NFR-USE-015)
findings.reasoning: Regulators manage multiple cases in parallel during a session (panel, SORA review, post-decision verification). No unified view of: (1) my active cases and their status (waiting for evidence, awaiting my annotation, ready for panel), (2) SLA countdowns (this case must be decided in 5 days, clock started on submission date), (3) escalation queue (cases flagged by other inspectors as awaiting my input). Missing dashboard leads to context-switching overhead and SLA misses. Parent story: regulator must maintain panel productivity; missing visibility costs time.
findings.recommendation: Create regulator dashboard: (1) 'My Active Cases' table: case ID, applicant, case status, my pending action, SLA countdown (color-coded: green >5 days, amber 2-5 days, red <2 days), (2) 'Escalation Queue': cases assigned to me for second opinion or complex review (count, priority), (3) 'Next Panel Session': when is my next panel, how many cases on agenda, any prep items due. Refresh every 5 min. Implement drill-down: click case → jump to case-open view (NFR-LAT-006). Add calendar integration: show panel session windows blocked on calendar.

findings.title: Missing CAA Innovation Hub dashboard: portfolio view of calibration statistics and drift signals
findings.component: Calibration and drift monitoring (NFR-MON-WB-011, NFR-MON-WB-012)
findings.reasoning: CAA Innovation Hub oversees AI-flag narrative service quality and regulator calibration. Key metrics: (1) inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) across panel cohorts, (2) AI-flag override rate trends (is narrative becoming more trustworthy over time?), (3) input distribution drift (evidence types changing), (4) model confidence trends. Missing dashboard means Innovation Hub cannot answer: 'Are we improving AI reliability?' or 'Should we retrain the model?' Strategic decisions lack data visibility.
findings.recommendation: Create Innovation Hub dashboard: (1) calibration scorecard: inter-rater kappa per panel, kappa trend over quarters (target >0.7), (2) AI-flag trustworthiness: override rate per regulator (histogram), trend over quarters, (3) evidence distribution: top-10 evidence types by frequency, % change month-over-month, (4) model drift: input distribution shift (KS test p-value), prediction distribution shift (flag rate trend), confidence trend. Refresh daily. Add drill-down: click panel → see kappa breakdown by case type (VLOS vs BVLOS). Generate quarterly report for leadership with key findings and recommendations (retrain model? recalibrate threshold?).

findings.title: Missing MAA/MOD regulatory dashboard: swarm trial performance metrics and safety signals
findings.component: Swarm operations and telemetry (NFR-MON-SWM-001 through SWM-007)
findings.reasoning: MAA Regulatory Branch (MOD-aligned) must oversee approved swarm operations. Key visibility: (1) DAA encounter rate and resolution latency per trial, (2) C2 link quality (SNR, packet loss), (3) battery/contingency margins per swarm, (4) autonomous consensus health, (5) safety events (geofence breaches, C2 loss, sensor failures). Missing dashboard means MOD cannot rapidly assess trial safety posture or identify trends (e.g., 'C2 quality degrading over time' → suggest RF path optimization). REQ006 mandates MOD oversight; monitoring must support that.
findings.recommendation: Create MOD regulatory dashboard (on-premises access): (1) active-trials map: swarm location, status (in-flight, landed, RTL), personnel (pilot, mission controller), (2) per-trial metrics: DAA encounter count + resolution latency, C2 SNR/loss-rate, battery state of charge (per vehicle), geofence compliance, (3) safety-event log: any geofence breach, C2 loss >10s, sensor failure (last 24h), (4) trends: encounter rate per flight hour, C2 link quality correlation with location (identifies bad RF zones). Real-time updates every 5s during active trials. Incident escalation: if any safety event detected, alert MOD ops (red banner, SMS).

findings.title: Gate 4 (Stress) missing acceptance criteria for panel session shedding under overload—must define graceful degradation behavior
findings.component: Panel session management, classification boundary routing, concurrent-user capacity
findings.reasoning: Ada capacity (NFR-CAP-WB-003) defines peak panel concurrency: 20 users during plenary vote sessions (8-20 concurrent panelists per panel, 1-3 simultaneous panels = 15-20 peak). Stress gate (Gate 4, NFR-END-WB-002) requires graceful degradation at 2x peak (40+ concurrent users). Current test strategy specifies 'Panel-mode session overflow: shed at session creation (reject new panel open), not mid-session.' But acceptance criteria is vague: what is 'graceful shedding'? Acceptable p95 latency during shedding? Allowable rejection rate? This creates test ambiguity. Alan availability (NFR-AVL-W002) budgets 5-minute RTO for active-active failover; implies resilience but does not define overload behavior. Without clear acceptance criteria, test pass/fail is subjective.
findings.recommendation: Refine Gate 4 acceptance criteria: (A) Define capacity limit: 'System supports max 20 concurrent panel members active-active. At 21+ concurrent members, new panel-open requests are rejected with HTTP 503 Service Unavailable + user-friendly message: "This panel is at capacity. Please try again in 5 minutes or contact CAA support."' (B) Define latency during overload: 'p95 latency for existing panel members (already logged in) must remain <10s p95 even during overload rejection phase.' (C) Define recovery: 'Once load drops below 20 concurrent, new panel opens are accepted within 30 seconds.' (D) Test procedure: ramp to 25 concurrent panelists; verify new panel opens are rejected; existing panelists can continue work; measure p95 latency; verify recovery time. Document results in Gate 4 release report.

findings.title: Audit log write latency drift risk—ledger fragmentation or fsync overhead can degrade 500ms p95 budget over 72 hours
findings.component: Audit / deliberation log (immutable append-only distributed ledger, see Liskov NFR-SCL-WB-008)
findings.reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-009 budgets 500ms p95 for panel-action log write (audit trail is regulatory-critical per REQ012). Liskov NFR-SCL-WB-008 describes audit log as sharded append-only ledger (Kafka or similar). Write performance depends on fsync latency (disk durability) and quorum ACK latency (replication consensus). On Liskov design: '1 shard (1 panel) handles ~50 actions/sec. Quorum write: 2-of-3 replicas ACK within 150 ms.' Over 72-hour soak with cumulative 10,000+ panel actions, ledger grows; if fsync overhead increases (disk cache pressure, file fragmentation), quorum ACK latency can drift from 150ms baseline to 250-300ms, pushing total write latency to 600-700ms (exceeds 500ms SLA). Symptom: p95 latency drifts linearly upward over 72h; panelists perceive 'saving comment is getting slow'.
findings.recommendation: Soak test (Gate 5) must monitor audit log write latency histogram every 10 minutes. Plot trends: (A) Quorum ACK latency component (time for 2-of-3 replicas to ACK); target stable <150ms. (B) Total write latency p95; target stable <500ms. If drift detected, investigate: (i) Disk I/O saturation (iostat, fio); (ii) fsync overhead increase (strace -e fsync); (iii) Replication lag (Kafka partition lag metrics). Mitigation: (A) Batch writes: aggregate 10-100 actions into single ledger entry (reduces write frequency by 10-100x, amortizes fsync overhead). (B) Async fsync: return write ACK to client before durability confirmation (acceptable for audit trail if fsync is durably queued and guaranteed to succeed). (C) Separate ledger shards per panel: distribute write load across multiple ledger instances (already in Liskov design).

findings.title: Clause library versioning and semantic search must support quarterly regulatory updates with <500 ms query latency
findings.feature: clause library and compliance guidance search
findings.reasoning: Clause library is the authoritative source of CAA compliance guidance for applicants and inspectors. Quarterly revision cadence (JARUS SORA v2.5, AMLAS/SACE updates, CAP 722 amendments, ML explainability templates) adds 50-100 MB per revision. Case officers expect sub-500 ms search latency for interactive compliance lookups (What is the current SORA requirement for loss-of-separation risk? What ML explainability evidence is required?). Full-text indexing + semantic embeddings (vector search for similar clauses) requires significant compute. Archive of 5+ years (4 GB cumulative) makes traditional search tools (simple LIKE queries) too slow. Classification tier variants (OFFICIAL + OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE clauses for upper tiers, SECRET clauses for MOD trials) multiply storage and index size.
findings.recommendation: (1) Deploy vector search database (Pinecone, Weaviate, or Elasticsearch with vector plugin, 2026 Q3): index all clause library versions with semantic embeddings (HuggingFace all-MiniLM-L6-v2 or OpenAI embeddings). (2) Establish library versioning strategy: publish release notes for each quarterly update; maintain backward-compatible old-version indices (inspectors may need to cite guidance applicable at time of case opening). (3) Implement caching layer (Redis): cache hot queries (e.g., 'SORA loss-of-separation', 'ML explainability evidence') with 24-hour TTL. (4) Set latency SLA: P95 search latency <500 ms, P99 <1 sec. Monitor via application performance metrics; alert if degraded. (5) Design classification-aware search: OFFICIAL tier searches use public library; OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE tier searches restricted to CAA personnel; SECRET tier searches restricted to MOD liaison. (6) Conduct search UX study (2026 Q4): gather feedback from case officers and inspectors on clause discovery; refine search UI and taxonomy.

findings.title: No latency SLA for Phased Gates (F18) or trial progression workflow; 'two-day cycle' anchor is system-level, not step-level
findings.feature: Phased Trial Gates & Progressive Evidence Build (F18, workflow orchestration)
findings.reasoning: Parent NFR REQ016 ('phased gates so that evidence builds progressively and is reversible') and parent user story ('two-day cycle for representative swarm submission') do not break down step-level latency targets for gate progression (gate approval, evidence sign-off, phase transition). Current budgets focus on individual upload, matrix, and panel-action steps, but do not address the latency of gate state-machine transitions (approval decision propagation, evidence lock, phase unlock). Phased gate progression may be gated by human review latency (case officer decision time), which is not a system latency but may block downstream work.
findings.recommendation: 1. Define gate progression SLAs: 'p95 gate approval decision rendered within 24 h of evidence submission.' 2. Separate system latency (gate state update: p95 <1 s) from human latency (approval decision: p95 <24 h). 3. Implement gate-readiness dashboard for case officers: 'Gate X ready for decision: [evidence summary, risk flags, AI recommendations].' 4. Add escalation SLA: 'If gate approval pending >24 h, escalate to supervisor.' 5. Track 'time in gate' metric per phased trial; use to refine two-day estimate.

findings.title: No latency SLA for Security Classification Handling (F20) and MOD compartment validation; 'CAP 722H compliance' lacks measurable latency target
findings.feature: Security Classification Handling (F20, MOD compartment validation)
findings.reasoning: Parent NFR REQ018 ('classified MOD evidence is presented to the CAA under CAP 722H / JSP 440 without compromise') is a policy/compliance requirement, not a latency requirement. No SLA is defined for how quickly classified evidence must be validated, segregated, or made available to authorized reviewers. Classification validation (verifying MOD compartment markings, security clearance levels, need-to-know) may add 500+ ms per document (crypto validation, clearance database lookup). For a BVLOS swarm trial with classified avionics and autonomy evidence, this could introduce 5–10 s of latency per pack segment.
findings.recommendation: 1. Define SLA: 'p95 classification validation (compartment check, clearance matching) <500 ms per document segment; p95 <30 s per 100 MB pack.' 2. Implement parallel classification processing (don't serialize on upload). 3. Pre-filter evidence at intake: separate OFFICIAL/CONFIDENTIAL from UNCLASSIFIED; route OFFICIAL to encrypted, access-controlled storage; render to clearance-appropriate reviewers only. 4. Add classification-status field to all workbench metadata: 'This evidence contains OFFICIAL compartment [XXXXXX]; access restricted to [role list].'

findings.title: Panel-action log write latency can be reduced by implementing write-behind buffering and eventual consistency for non-critical actions
findings.feature: Panel-action log write (audit trail optimization)
findings.reasoning: Current SLA (NFR-LAT-009) enforces synchronous quorum replication for all audit writes, driving p95 to 500 ms. However, not all actions require immediate quorum durability: transient UI state (panel scroll position, collapse/expand), non-binding review notes (drafts), and temporary flags can use write-behind buffering (write to local cache, async flush to quorum). Only binding actions (decision state changes, submitted verdicts, digital signatures) require immediate durability. This stratification can reduce average latency to <200 ms and still maintain audit integrity for critical actions.
findings.recommendation: 1. Classify panel actions: (a) immediate-durability: decision verdicts, digital signatures, final flags (continue quorum SLA); (b) eventual-consistency: draft notes, temporary holds, UI state (implement write-behind, flush every 5–10 s or on 'save'). 2. Implement dual-write path: non-durable actions → local in-memory log + async flush; durable actions → sync quorum write. 3. Measure impact on user experience (perceived latency, usability of draft notes). 4. Add workbench audit report: distinguish between immediate and eventually-consistent actions. 5. Target p95 <200 ms for non-durable actions; p95 <1 s for durable actions.

findings.title: Onboard ML inference p95 200 ms budget may not account for model load latency and GPU context switching on edge hardware
findings.feature: Onboard ML inference for autonomy decisions
findings.reasoning: Budget assumes 120 ms inference execution on already-loaded model, but edge avionics (Nvidia Jetson, commercial flight compute modules) may not pre-load large models in VRAM due to power/weight constraints. First inference after model swap can incur 500+ ms load latency. Additionally, context switching between multiple lightweight models (waypoint selector, DAA evasion, formation controller) can add 50+ ms per switch if not carefully orchestrated. Confidence is moderate based on empirical edge ML latencies and known avionics constraints.
findings.recommendation: 1. Profile avionics hardware (CPU, GPU, thermal limits) and measure actual model load time for each autonomy model. 2. Implement model pre-loading strategy: keep all active models in VRAM (may require model quantization to fit). 3. Use single unified model or model ensemble where possible (reduce load/context-switching overhead). 4. Measure p95 inference latency on representative mission profiles (nominal transit, DAA encounter, loss-of-C2). 5. If p95 exceeds 250 ms, implement hierarchical response: (a) fast reactive model (<100 ms); (b) deliberative refinement (100–200 ms); queue re-planning for later cycle.

findings.title: C2 link bandwidth shared-spectrum contention at 3+ concurrent swarms
findings.component: Swarm telemetry and C2 communication
findings.reasoning: C2 link budget: 100-500 kbps per vehicle (Noyce NFR-LAT-013 SLA <1s round-trip). Single swarm (5 vehicles): 500-2500 kbps total (< 3 Mbps LTE per-device capacity; no issue). Three concurrent swarms (15 vehicles): 1500-7500 kbps (7.5 Mbps total). LTE network cell capacity varies; urban cells 10-50 Mbps shared across users. Three concurrent swarms at same test site may exceed fair-share if other traffic present. Shared spectrum (unlicensed 2.4 GHz ISM band used by many trials) risks interference. Mitigation: ensure each swarm has dedicated cellular data plan (separate LTE APN) or licensed RF spectrum allocation per trial.
findings.recommendation: Negotiate dedicated LTE APNs with operator for each authorized trial (ensures fair-share QoS). For remote trials without LTE, fall back to satellite C2 (400 kbps max; reduces real-time telemetry to 1 Hz, acceptable for non-DAA encounters). By 2028, if 3+ concurrent swarm trials planned, reserve licensed RF spectrum band (CAA airspace regulator can coordinate). Cost: GBP 5-10K per trial for spectrum licensing.

findings.title: Applicant intake traffic spikes during quarterly panel season will require pre-scaling +2 web instances
findings.component: Web front-end / BFF
findings.reasoning: Ada's capacity model shows peak concurrent applicants = 25 (peak intake rate 2-3 applications/week during trial season). Web tier throughput: ~500 req/s per instance (measured). At 25 concurrent applicants with 20 req/sec per applicant (submission workflow = workspace create + upload + readiness check = 10-20 sequential requests), expect ~500 req/s sustained. Single instance breaks at 500-800 req/s. By 2028, peak intake rate = 8-10 applications/week (Project Alvina, GCAP/Tempest cohorts), peak concurrent = 50 applicants (expect ~1000 req/s). Current capacity: 2 instances. Required by 2028: 3-4 instances baseline + 1 auto-scale buffer.
findings.recommendation: Implement scheduled auto-scaling: 2 weeks before each quarterly panel (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4), increase web tier from 2 to 4 instances. CDN edge cache (CloudFront) should front the BFF to absorb spike in static asset requests (reduces origin load 30-40%). Monitor request rate per applicant; if average >30 req/applicant (indicating rework/re-upload cycles), alert case officers to prioritize readiness coaching.

findings.title: Onboard compute TDP budget (10-20 W) limits autonomy complexity; advanced trials require Jetson Orin upgrade
findings.component: Onboard autonomy compute
findings.reasoning: Ada's capacity model shows onboard compute TDP budget 10-20 W per vehicle (battery endurance trade-off: 1 W = 10-20 min flight time lost). Protector RG Mk1 (basic autonomy): 40-50% CPU utilization = 5-10 W (safe margin). Project Alvina (swarm coordination): 60-70% CPU = 10-15 W (tight). GCAP/Tempest (advanced ML inference + formation control): 90%+ CPU = 18-20 W (thermal throttling risk). Current hardware: Jetson Nano or TX2 (8-15 W TDP). At 85% utilization, Nano throttles. Jetson Orin (25 W TDP) can sustain 85% utilization without throttling, but exceeds battery endurance budget (reduces flight time 20-30%).
findings.recommendation: For GCAP/Tempest trials (2027-2028), mandate Jetson Orin (or equivalent 15-20 W ARM compute) + power budget review (reduce flight duration from 90 min to 60-75 min). Alternatively, off-load non-critical ML inference to ground (post-flight analysis only), keeping onboard compute at <70% utilization. Test battery thermal curves by Q4 2026 to quantify flight-time penalty of advanced autonomy (should be <10% reduction for acceptable trials).

findings.title: Frozen-model manifest validation latency could degrade autonomy inference (NFR-LAT-015)
findings.feature: Onboard ML model validation (SHA-256 frozen-model hash)
findings.reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-015 'Onboard ML inference for autonomy decisions' budgets 200 ms (p95) for tactical ML decisions (waypoint selection, DAA evasion). Frozen-model hash verification (REQ009 forensic explainability requirement) adds ~50 ms on ARM Cortex compute. If model validation is synchronous and called before every inference, SLA is tight: 200 ms - 50 ms (hash) = 150 ms for actual inference, which is constrained but achievable with quantized models. However, if model validation fails (hash mismatch due to firmware corruption), system must trigger failsafe (flight termination). Failsafe latency is safety-critical (Noyce NFR-LAT-016 'Flight termination' budgets 500 ms), so hash validation must be <100 ms to leave room for failsafe execution. On slower compute platforms (ARM Cortex-A9), SHA-256 validation could exceed 100 ms, delaying failsafe response.
findings.recommendation: Implement model validation at mission-startup time, not at each inference call. Pre-load frozen-model hash into secure element (HSM or eFuse) during mission upload. At each inference call, do lightweight hash check (compare pre-computed hash against cached value in secure memory). This reduces per-inference overhead from 50 ms to <1 ms. For hash validation at startup, allocate 5 seconds (long-running operation, not time-critical). Test model validation latency on target compute hardware (ARM Cortex-A72, Cortex-A53); measure hash verification time and ensure startup validation completes within 5 seconds. Log any hash mismatch as security event and trigger immediate flight-termination failsafe.

findings.title: High-contrast mode must not introduce rendering latency penalties
findings.feature: High-Contrast Mode Rendering Performance (WCAG 2.2 AA)
findings.reasoning: Accessibility NFR mandates WCAG 2.2 AA support, including high-contrast mode. If high-contrast CSS (overriding background colors, increasing font weight) introduces additional rendering latency (e.g., forced layout recalculations), screen reader users or users with low vision may perceive tool as slower. Risk: accessible mode feels penalized compared to standard mode, reducing adoption.
findings.recommendation: Ensure high-contrast CSS uses only low-cost style changes: color overrides (no layout thrashing), border weight (no reflow), text shadows (gpu-accelerated). Test rendering performance in high-contrast mode: measure First Contentful Paint (FCP) and Time to Interactive (TTI) against standard mode. Target: no more than 50 ms additional latency in high-contrast mode. Use CSS custom properties (--contrast-background, --contrast-text) to avoid specificity wars. Test on Windows High Contrast Mode and MacOS Increase Contrast setting.

findings.title: Classified networks (MAA) may have latency spikes and bandwidth limitations; require graceful degradation
findings.feature: Classified Network Performance (MAA Air-Gapped / Latency-Constrained)
findings.reasoning: Regulators work on MAA classified networks (air-gapped or limited uplink). Latency can spike to 2-3s on classified connections. Case-open (Noyce NFR-LAT-006: 10 s p95) and evidence navigation (NFR-LAT-008: 2 s p99) must remain responsive despite network variability. Risk: regulator perceives tool as slow on classified network, loses confidence in platform, defaults to email-based case management.
findings.recommendation: Implement regional caching: cache case metadata and evidence pages on classified network edge server (if MAA infrastructure permits). Reduce payload size: compress evidence metadata, lazy-load non-critical sections (e.g., AI flags). Implement graceful degradation: if case-open exceeds 15 s, show 'Loading... [Cancel]' with option to retry or exit. Monitor latency variance on classified network: log p50/p90/p99 separately from corporate network to identify infrastructure bottlenecks. Coordinate with MAA network team to identify optimization opportunities.

findings.title: Regulator NPS target >50 driven by case-open responsiveness, evidence navigation fluidity, and decision-record reliability
findings.feature: Regulator Satisfaction & Multi-Day Panel Session Productivity
findings.reasoning: Target: Regulator NPS >50 by steady state. Primary drivers: (1) case-open latency <10 s p95, (2) evidence navigation <2 s p99, (3) zero crashes during multi-day session, (4) decision record export success >95%. Quarterly survey: 'Would you recommend this tool to another regulator?' Regulators in high-stakes CAA/MAA panel sessions demand rock-solid reliability and snappy responsiveness. If tool feels slow or crashes mid-session, regulator will request return to manual processes.
findings.recommendation: Implement monthly regulator satisfaction pulse survey (2-3 questions, <1 min). Questions: (1) 'Case-open speed: excellent / good / acceptable / slow / very slow', (2) 'Evidence navigation fluidity: excellent / good / acceptable / sluggish / broken', (3) 'Session stability: zero crashes / one crash / multiple crashes / tool unusable'. Correlate responses with latency metrics. If satisfaction drops below 50, investigate specific latency spike or crash. Conduct quarterly deep-dive interviews with 5-10 regulators to identify pain points beyond metrics.

findings.title: Classification boundary reject rate alerts may spike during normal tier transitions (low signal-to-noise)
findings.component: Classification boundary service (NFR-MON-WB-008)
findings.reasoning: Current alert: 'reject rate > 0.1% of access attempts triggers critical alert.' During normal operations, some rejects are expected (users misclick evidence tier, classification marking fails, etc.). When GCAP/Tempest trials ramp up (2026-2028), evidence volume increases, so absolute reject count grows even if percentage stays constant. Alert fatigue risk: if absolute rejects grow from 1-2 per day to 10-20 per day (legitimate growth), but alert fires on any spike >0.1%, operations team becomes desensitized.
findings.recommendation: Establish baseline reject rate per trial phase: (1) Q2 2026: <0.05%, (2) Q4 2026: <0.1%, (3) Q4 2027: <0.15% (higher volume expected). Set alert thresholds dynamically based on forecast. Additionally, segment by reject reason: classify rejects as (a) expected (user in wrong tier), (b) suspicious (access-control bypass attempt), (c) data-quality (classification marking missing). Alert only on suspicious rejects (count any access attempt from OFFICIAL user to SECRET evidence). Generate daily report: reject counts by reason, no alert for expected baseline rejects.

findings.title: Missing DDH/RAISO accountability dashboard: authority exercised + decisions made per regulator
findings.component: Audit trail and decision recording (NFR-MON-WB-007, NFR-AVL-W006)
findings.reasoning: DDH (Deputy Director, Horizon) and RAISO (Regulatory Authority Swarm Inspection Operator) must maintain regulatory accountability. Dashboard tracks: (1) which regulator made which decision (case X approved by regulator Y on date Z), (2) decision reversals (case initially approved, later revoked due to new evidence), (3) anomalies (regulator X approves cases at 10% override rate, regulator Y at 70% → investigate bias), (4) decision consistency (similar cases decided differently by different regulators → train cadre). Missing dashboard hides accountability and decision quality issues.
findings.recommendation: Create DDH/RAISO accountability dashboard (CAA officers only): (1) decision log: case ID, regulator, decision (APPROVED/CONDITIONAL/REJECTED), decision date, conditions imposed, (2) decision-maker activity: regulator ID, cases decided this quarter, average decision time, override rate on AI flags, (3) anomaly detection: regulator X has unusual decision pattern (approve rate > 2 std dev from cohort mean) → flag for leadership review, (4) reversals: any decision reversed post-approval (new evidence, appeal) → track and analyze root cause. Refresh daily. Generate monthly report: decision quality metrics, any regulators needing additional training, appeals/reversals analysis.

findings.title: Gate 3 (Load) missing duration and consistency-variance acceptance criteria—1-hour load test insufficient for 72h mission SLA
findings.component: Applicant intake, inspector review, evidence processing pipelines
findings.reasoning: Doneness criteria (Gate 3 - Load): 'critical journeys pass p95 budget at 1x peak for 1h'. One-hour duration is insufficient for long-duration workflow validation. Applicant submission lifecycle (REQ016, ada-capacity.json) is 8-12 weeks; a 1-hour load test exercises only initial intake phase (workspace creation, evidence upload, quick-gate processing). Missing: (A) Multiple upload cycles (day 1, day 3, day 7, day 20) to detect degradation during multi-week submission lifecycle. (B) Inspector review patterns (async over days/weeks); 1-hour test does not exercise concurrent applicant+inspector workflows. (C) Panel deliberation (5-10 day event); 1-hour test cannot validate panel session stability across deliberation lifecycle. Noyce SLA for decision-record drafting (end-to-end <30 min p95 per test strategy NFR-END-WB-008) is not validated by 1-hour load test.
findings.recommendation: Extend Gate 3 duration and add acceptance criteria: (A) Duration: minimum 8 hours load test at 1x peak, simulating 1-week of operational load in compressed time. (B) Consistency-variance: 'p95 latency variance across 3 independent 1-hour run windows must be <10% (e.g., if run 1 is 3.0s, run 2 is 3.1s, run 3 is 2.95s; coefficient of variation <10%).' (C) Multi-user journeys: '3+ concurrent applicants each completing full Phase 1 workflow (upload → readiness check → submission) during test; 100% success rate required.' (D) Measurement: aggregate p95 over 8-hour window from running 5-min percentile windows (avoids statistical artifacts from short observation windows). Document Gate 3 results with latency histogram + variance analysis.

findings.title: Gate 2 (Integration) missing multi-journey cross-functional scenarios—single-journey testing inadequate for multi-user interactions
findings.component: Applicant intake + inspector review + panel deliberation pipelines (cross-journey interactions)
findings.reasoning: Gate 2 (Integration) specifies: 'critical journeys pass p95 budget at 1x load with 3-run variance <10%'. Liskov scalability identifies separate journeys: applicant intake (Noyce NFR-LAT-001 through -005), inspector review (Noyce NFR-LAT-006, -008, -007), panel deliberation (Noyce NFR-LAT-009, -010). Gate 2 tests each journey in isolation (e.g., applicant journey 3 consecutive times, measure variance). Missing: concurrent applicant + inspector + panel workflows. Example: while applicant uploads evidence to Case A, inspector opens Case B for review, panel deliberates Case C. Contention on shared resources (database connection pool, evidence store, audit log ledger) could cause latency cross-talk between journeys. Single-journey integration testing does not expose cross-journey latency coupling.
findings.recommendation: Enhance Gate 2 acceptance criteria: (A) Add multi-journey concurrent scenario: '3 concurrent workflows: applicant uploads 100 MB pack to Case 1, inspector reviews 5 evidence sections in Case 2 (Noyce NFR-LAT-008), panel action logs 10 comments to Case 3. All three workflows execute simultaneously. Measure: p95 latency for each journey isolated and concurrent. Acceptance: concurrent p95 latencies do not exceed isolated baselines by >10%.' (B) Repeat 3-run consistency variance check on concurrent scenario (not just isolated journeys). (C) Measure resource contention: database connection pool utilization, evidence store PUT/GET latency histograms. Document: any observed latency coupling (e.g., applicant upload slowed by inspector queries) and mitigation (e.g., read replicas for inspector queries, separate connection pools).

findings.title: Gate 1 (Unit) missing performance budget inheritance from Liskov scalability—GPU inference budgets not validated in unit tests
findings.component: GPU inference components (coverage matrix OCR, semantic classification, AI narrative LLM); unit test harness
findings.reasoning: Gate 1 (Unit) specifies: 'new performance-sensitive functions have unit perf tests with budgets'. Liskov scalability (NFR-SCL-WB-002, NFR-SCL-WB-003) identifies two GPU-intensive components: (A) Coverage matrix engine (OCR 180s budget, semantic classification 200s, matrix computation 90s per Noyce NFR-LAT-003). (B) AI narrative service (chunk inference 900s total per Noyce NFR-LAT-007). These components have significant performance budgets; unit tests should validate that individual inference calls (OCR on single PDF page, semantic classification on single clause, LLM inference on single evidence chunk) meet sub-budgets. Current Gate 1 is vague: does it require unit tests for GPU inference functions? Unit test framework (JUnit, PyTest) may not have GPU-capable runners; testing GPU code typically requires container-based or cloud-based test infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Define Gate 1 GPU inference testing approach: (A) Create GPU-capable unit test suite (Docker container with nvidia-base image, CUDA 12.x, Jetson or A100 simulator). (B) Define performance budgets for unit-level operations: (i) OCR on single page: <1s per Noyce budget (OCR is 180s for ~180 page evidence pack). (ii) Semantic classification inference on single clause: <100ms (200s for ~2000 clauses). (iii) LLM inference on single chunk: <5s (900s for ~180 chunks). (C) Unit tests must measure latency histogram (p50, p90, p95) for each operation; assert p95 < budget. (D) Add memory profiling: GPU memory allocated during inference should be <1 GB for single inference call (catch memory leaks early). (E) Run unit tests as pre-commit hook in CI/CD pipeline; fail build if any unit perf test exceeds budget.

findings.title: Clause library semantic search adds 5-10s latency; vector embedding model serving must be async
findings.component: Clause library search
findings.reasoning: Ada's capacity model estimates clause library at 200-500 MB by 2028. Full-text search (Elasticsearch): <500 ms p95 (interactive). Semantic search (vector embedding + cosine similarity): requires LLM embedding inference (separate GPU worker). Current design: not specified in requirements (gap in NFR-LAT-017 which specifies 5s search SLA but doesn't distinguish full-text vs. semantic). If semantic search invoked synchronously per-query, 5-10s embedding latency exceeds 5s total SLA. Mitigation: pre-compute embeddings on library update (quarterly); serve from vector DB (Pinecone, Weaviate, or pgvector in PostgreSQL). At 500 MB library × 4 versions retained × 5-year retention = 10 GB cumulative embeddings (~80 GB in vector space).
findings.recommendation: Deploy async embedding pre-computation: on library revision (3-4x/year), trigger batch embedding job (5-30 min compute time per library version). Store embeddings in pgvector plugin (PostgreSQL) or separate vector DB (Pinecone; cost ~GBP 100/month). At query time, hybrid search: full-text index (Elasticsearch) + vector search (pgvector) executed in parallel; return merged results in <500 ms p95. Fallback to full-text only if vector search stalls.

findings.title: Clause library search endpoint susceptible to enumeration attack
findings.feature: Clause library read-only endpoint (OFFICIAL-tier auth gate)
findings.reasoning: Clause library is public read-only content (regulatory clauses, guidance documents) available to all authenticated users. Rate limiting is 100 requests/min per user. An attacker with compromised or brute-forced credentials could enumerate entire clause library by performing sequential queries (e.g., search for each term from dictionary). Over 10 minutes, attacker can issue 1000 queries, potentially downloading entire library. Clause library is not classified SECRET, but CAA may want to limit unauthorized mass-download. Rate limiting is defensive but not airtight (100/min is high; attacker can download ~100 KB clauses/min, feasible for small database).
findings.recommendation: Rate limiting of 100 req/min per user is reasonable baseline. Implement additional safeguards: (1) Bulk-export endpoint: offer authorized users a full-dump download link (updated monthly) rather than forcing enumeration queries. (2) Implement exponential backoff: after user hits rate limit, increase cooldown from 60s to 5 minutes. (3) Implement geolocation-based anomaly detection: flag searches from unusual geographic locations (e.g., attacker in hostile nation). (4) Log clause-search patterns; alert if single user downloads >50% of clause library in <1 hour. (5) Implement query complexity limits: block queries with >10 disjunctive terms (potential relevance-ranking DoS). No urgent action needed; low severity due to public nature of clause content.

findings.title: WAF ruleset depth trades latency vs. threat coverage (applicant portal)
findings.feature: WAF inspection on applicant portal ingress
findings.reasoning: WAF overhead estimated at 10-30 ms depending on ruleset depth. OWASP CRS 3.3.2 baseline ruleset (100+ rules) adds ~10 ms. Customized ruleset for malicious PDF detection (Noyce 'virus_scan' and 'content_classification' are downstream, but WAF can pre-filter obvious payloads) adds another 5-10 ms. Aggressive ruleset (200+ rules) could add 20-30 ms. Noyce NFR-LAT-001 'Applicant workspace creation' budgets 200 ms for 'web_server_auth' component; WAF is upstream, so 20 ms overhead is 10% of that budget. Acceptable but not negligible. Heavy WAF inspection could also cause false positives: legitimate applicant requests (e.g., large JSON evidence metadata) could be blocked as suspicious. False-positive rate must remain <1%.
findings.recommendation: Use OWASP CRS 3.3.2 baseline with paranoia level 2 (balanced coverage + low false-positive rate). Add custom rules for malicious PDF signatures (e.g., suspicious JavaScript, embedded executables) but keep rule count <150. Implement WAF exclusion list for known-legitimate request patterns (e.g., applicant uploads with large Content-Length). Monitor WAF logs for false-positive rate; target <0.5%. Conduct quarterly review of WAF rule effectiveness; retire rules with zero matches. Test WAF latency impact with synthetic load; ensure p95 latency remains <15 ms.
